# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] — 3 Marks

**Define a personal monitoring device. State any two situations where a passive (diffusive) sampler is preferred over an active pumped sampler.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**
A personal monitoring device is a portable instrument worn on the worker's body that measures the magnitude of an occupational exposure in the breathing zone or on the skin. It quantifies the dose received during a defined period.
Passive samplers are preferred in:
1. Long-term (full-shift) TWA studies where power-supply and pump-pulsation noise are concerns (e.g., 3M 3500 organic-vapor badge worn for 8 h).
2. Tasks where a pump would be intrusive or unsafe — e.g., confined-space entry, welding at height, climbing scaffolding.
*(Any two valid situations accepted.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] — 3 Marks

**List the seven pillars of an occupational medical surveillance programme and identify the one that is legally mandatory under the Indian Factories Act, 1948.**

**Model Answer:**
(1) Pre-Placement, (2) Periodic, (3) Return-to-Work, (4) Exit, (5) Biological Monitoring, (6) First-Aid & Emergency, (7) Records. **First-Aid arrangements (Sec 45)** and **Health Records (Sec 91)** are mandatory under the Factories Act, 1948.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE of the two questions — Question A OR Question B]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the components and working of a **personal noise dosimeter** used to compute $L_{EX,8h}$. List the standards (ISO, OSHA) that govern noise exposure measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker's blood lead level is found to be 70 µg/dL. The half-life of lead in blood is 30 days. Calculate the blood lead level after 90 days of removal from exposure. Comment on the safety of the worker. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7-Mark Answer**

**Block diagram of a noise dosimeter:**

```
[Microphone] → [Preamplifier] → [A-Weighting Filter] → [RMS Detector]
                                            ↓
                                 [Threshold + Criterion + Exchange]
                                            ↓
                                    [Integrator / % Dose]
                                            ↓
                                    [Display + Memory]
```

**Working principle (Step-by-step):**
1. **Microphone** (½-inch electret) converts acoustic pressure into electrical signal.
2. **A-weighting filter** models human-ear response (de-emphasizes < 1 kHz and > 6 kHz).
3. **RMS detector** computes equivalent continuous SPL over short time windows (1 s).
4. **Threshold** (typically 75 dB), **Criterion Level** (85 dB), and **Exchange Rate** (3 or 5 dB) are set in firmware.
5. The instrument integrates the partial dose $\frac{t_i}{T_i(L_i)}$ over the shift and displays the **% Dose** and $L_{EX,8h}$.
6. Data are stored and downloaded to a PC for reporting.

**Governing Standards:**
- **ISO 1999:2013** — estimation of noise-induced hearing loss.
- **ISO 9612:2009** — strategy for measurement of occupational noise exposure.
- **NIOSH 98-126** (3-dB exchange).
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95** (5-dB exchange, 90 dB PEL).

**(b) — 7-Mark Solution**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula** (first-order elimination):

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t / t_{1/2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $C_0 = 70$ µg/dL, $t = 90$ days, $t_{1/2} = 30$ days:

$$
C(90) = 70 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{90/30} = 70 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 = 70 \times 0.125 = 8.75 \text{ µg/dL}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret** with respect to the ACGIH BEI of **< 50 µg/dL** and the Indian Factories Act lead-poisoning threshold of **80 µg/dL**:
- Initial level (70) was below the statutory suspension level of 80 µg/dL.
- After 90 days it has fallen to **8.75 µg/dL**, well within the BEI.
- The worker is fit to return to lead-handling work **only after** improvement in engineering controls and re-baselining.
- However, body-burden in bone (half-life ≈ 20 years) remains a long-term concern, and the worker must be kept under **annual biological monitoring**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept and procedure of **Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** calculation. A worker is exposed to 50 ppm of solvent for 2 h, 100 ppm for 1 h, 30 ppm for 3 h, and 10 ppm for the remaining 2 h of the shift. Calculate the 8-h TWA and compare it with the TLV-TWA of 25 ppm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **contents of an industrial first-aid box** as per the Indian Factories Act. Outline the duties of a **trained first-aider** during an emergency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7-Mark Solution**

**Concept:** TWA is the time-weighted average concentration of an airborne contaminant over a normal 8-h workday or 40-h workweek, to which a worker may be exposed repeatedly without adverse health effects. It accounts for varying concentrations during different tasks.

**Procedure:**
1. Identify the time periods $t_i$ the worker spends in different concentration zones $C_i$.
2. Compute the product $C_i \cdot t_i$ for each zone.
3. Sum the products and divide by the total exposure time.

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TWA} &= \frac{(50 \times 2) + (100 \times 1) + (30 \times 3) + (10 \times 2)}{2+1+3+2} \\
&= \frac{100 + 100 + 90 + 20}{8} \\
&= \frac{310}{8} \\
&= 38.75 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** $\text{TWA} = 38.75$ ppm vs TLV-TWA = 25 ppm.
**Result:** Worker is **overexposed by 55 %**. Corrective actions: local exhaust ventilation, job rotation, half-face respirator with APF ≥ 4 (e.g., organic-vapor cartridge).

**(b) — 7-Mark Solution**

**Contents of an Industrial First-Aid Box (Indian Factories Act, Schedule VI, Rule 64) — per 150 workers:**

1. Sterilized dressings (small, medium, large) — sufficient quantity.
2. Roll of adhesive plaster.
3. Triangular bandages — 6.
4. Roller bandages 10 cm × 5 m — 6.
5. Sterilized cotton wool — 100 g.
6. Antiseptic solution (Dettol/Savlon) — 250 mL.
7. Burn ointment — 2 tubes.
8. Safety pins — 12.
9. Scissors, tweezers — 1 each.
10. Reagent tablets (for eye wash) — 1 bottle.
11. A copy of the first-aid leaflet.
12. Eyewash bottle.
13. Disposable gloves — 4 pairs.

**Duties of a Trained First-Aider (DRS-ABCD):**
- **D** — Danger: assess scene safety.
- **R** — Response: check responsiveness (tap shoulder, shout).
- **S** — Send for help: call ambulance / OHC.
- **A** — Airway: head-tilt chin-lift.
- **B** — Breathing: look-listen-feel for 10 s.
- **C** — CPR: 30 chest compressions : 2 rescue breaths if not breathing.
- **D** — Defibrillation: use AED if available.

Additional duties: maintain first-aid box inventory, log every incident in the **First-Aid Register**, escort victim to OHC, preserve evidence for incident investigation, and not exceed scope of training.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Often Lose Marks (Module 1):**
> 1. **Confusing TWA with STEL and Ceiling** — examiners specifically test whether you can pick the correct averaging time. If the question says "15 minutes," the answer is **STEL**, not TWA.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — a noise dosimeter reading in % Dose must be converted to $L_{EX,8h}$ using the **3-dB or 5-dB exchange rule**; writing only the dose % without the dB figure loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong biological half-life application** — students often use $e^{-kt}$ instead of the simple $(1/2)^{t/t_{1/2}}$ form. Both are mathematically equivalent **only** if you state the relationship $k = \ln 2 / t_{1/2}$.
> 4. **Omitting the legal reference** in first-aid questions — the Indian Factories Act, 1948, Schedule VI is the mandatory citation. International references (OSHA, ANSI) are not the right primary citation for an Indian university exam.
> 5. **Block diagrams without arrows** — the evaluator expects a clear *flow* (input → process → output). Boxes without arrow connections receive **partial credit only**.
> 6. **PPE ≠ Medical Support** — the examiner may deliberately set a question on "what is occupational medical support?" and a student who drifts into PPE description will be marked out of scope.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Personal Monitoring Devices and Medical Support: Rapid Recap**

- **Personal Monitoring Device (PMD)** is a body-worn instrument that measures an individual's occupational exposure dose.
- PMDs are classified by **hazard type**, **sampling principle** (active vs passive), **placement** (breathing zone, lapel, hand), and **output type** (real-time vs integrating).
- **Active samplers** use a calibrated personal pump; **passive samplers** rely on Fick's diffusion.
- **TWA = 8 h**, **STEL = 15 min (4× per shift max)**, **Ceiling = instantaneous**.
- Key noise formulae: $L_{EX,8h} = L_c + 10 \log_{10}(\text{Dose}/100)$, $T_i = 8 / 2^{(L_i-85)/3}$ (3-dB rule).
- Hand-Arm Vibration daily exposure: $A(8) = a_{hv}\sqrt{T/8}$; EAV = 2.5, ELV = 5.0 m/s².
- Blood-lead biological half-life ≈ 30 days; bone half-life ≈ 20 years.
- **Medical Surveillance = 7 pillars** (PEME, Periodic, Return-to-Work, Exit, Biological, First-Aid, Records).
- **Indian Factories Act, 1948, Sec 45**: First-aid box mandatory in every factory; one first-aider per shift per 150 workers; ambulance room when ≥ 500 workers.
- **BEI for lead** (ACGIH) = < 50 µg/dL; statutory suspension level (India) = 80 µg/dL.
- **First-Aider duties** follow **DR-ABCD** protocol.
- **PMD-vs-area monitoring:** PMD = worker-specific; area = location-specific (inferior for dose).
- **Calibration** of personal pump must be within ± 5 % of set flow rate; pre- and post-shift calibration is mandatory for NIOSH/OSHA compliance.
- **Decision-making hierarchy** in exposure: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE** (medical support kicks in when all controls fail).

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 1, Topic: Personal Monitoring Devices and Medical Support**
> *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme | Bloom Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse | CO-Mapped*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazards Associated with Laser, Infra-red, Ultra Violet and ELF Radiations and Preventive Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hazards Associated with Laser, Infra-red, Ultra Violet and ELF Radiations and Preventive Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electromagnetic Radiation – The Common Foundation

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
*Non-ionizing electromagnetic radiations* are a subset of the electromagnetic spectrum that carry insufficient photon energy (**< 10 eV**) to remove tightly bound electrons from atoms, but possess sufficient energy to cause biological effects through thermal, photochemical, and photo-biological mechanisms. The radiations covered in this module — **Laser, Infra-Red (IR), Ultra-Violet (UV), and Extremely Low Frequency (ELF)** — fall within this category and represent critical occupational hazards in industries such as manufacturing, healthcare, welding, telecommunications, and power transmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction (Repeatedly Asked):**
> - **Ionizing Radiation** (X-rays, Gamma rays): Photon energy **> 10 eV**, breaks chemical bonds, causes ionization.
> - **Non-Ionizing Radiation** (Laser, IR, UV, ELF): Photon energy **< 10 eV**, excites molecules, causes heating, photochemical reactions, and induced currents.

**Photon Energy Equation (Universal to All EM Radiations):**

$$E = h \cdot \nu = \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Photon energy in Joules (J)
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $\nu$ = Frequency in Hertz (Hz)
- $c$ = Speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength in meters (m)

### 1.2 The Electromagnetic Spectrum – Visual Roadmap

| Radiation Type | Wavelength Range | Frequency Range | Photon Energy |
|----------------|------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| ELF | > 100 km | 3 – 3000 Hz | < 1.24 × 10⁻¹¹ eV |
| Radio / Microwave | 1 mm – 100 km | 3 kHz – 300 GHz | 1.24 × 10⁻⁶ – 1.24 × 10⁻¹¹ eV |
| **Infra-Red (IR)** | 780 nm – 1 mm | 300 GHz – 430 THz | 1.6 × 10⁻³ – 1.24 eV |
| **Visible Light** | 380 – 780 nm | 430 – 750 THz | 1.6 – 3.3 eV |
| **Ultra-Violet (UV)** | 100 – 380 nm | 750 THz – 30 PHz | 3.3 – 124 eV |
| **Laser** | Anywhere in optical range (UV to IR) | Monochromatic, coherent | Variable (0.05 – 6 eV typical) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Sun-Tan & The Microwave"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Students:**
> Imagine sunlight hitting you at the beach. The **UV component** is what gives you a tan (or a painful burn) — this is *photochemical damage*. The **IR component** is what makes you feel hot — this is *thermal damage*. A **laser** is like a perfectly focused, single-colour beam of that sunlight — millions of times more concentrated. An **ELF field** is invisible, silent, and surrounds every wire in your house — it cannot burn or tan you, but it can whisper "instructions" to your nerves and cells through induced electric currents. Each radiation has its own unique "personality" of damage.

---

### 1.4 Laser Radiation – Definition

**Formal Definition:**
**LASER (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation)** is a device that produces an intense, highly directional, monochromatic, and coherent beam of optical radiation (UV, visible, or IR) through the process of *stimulated emission* of photons from an excited lasing medium (solid, liquid, or gas).

**Key Beam Properties (Repeated in every KTU question):**
1. **Monochromaticity** – Single wavelength (Δλ very small)
2. **Coherence** – All photons in phase (spatial + temporal)
3. **Directionality** – Extremely low divergence (milliradians)
4. **High Brightness** – Power per unit area per unit solid angle is enormous

### 1.5 Infra-Red Radiation (IR) – Definition

Infra-red radiation occupies the **wavelength band 780 nm to 1 mm** of the electromagnetic spectrum, located between visible light and microwaves. It is invisible to the human eye and is perceived purely as **heat**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IR Sub-Bands (ICNIRP / WHO Classification):**
> - **IR-A (Near-IR):** 780 nm – 1.4 μm → Retinal hazard (deeply penetrating into the eye)
> - **IR-B (Short-wave IR):** 1.4 μm – 3 μm → Corneal and aqueous humour hazard
> - **IR-C (Far-IR / Thermal IR):** 3 μm – 1 mm → Skin and outer eye (cornea) hazard

### 1.6 Ultra-Violet Radiation (UV) – Definition

UV radiation is optical radiation with **wavelengths between 100 nm and 400 nm**, situated between visible light and X-rays. It is completely invisible and is the principal cause of *photochemical* biological damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UV Sub-Bands (Standard Classification):**
> - **UV-A (Long-wave / Blacklight):** 315 – 400 nm → Tanning, skin ageing, glass-blower's cataract
> - **UV-B (Medium-wave / Erythema band):** 280 – 315 nm → Sunburn, skin cancer, snow blindness
> - **UV-C (Short-wave / Germicidal):** 100 – 280 nm → Sterilization, severe eye/skin damage (mostly absorbed by ozone layer)

### 1.7 Extremely Low Frequency (ELF) Radiation – Definition

**ELF fields** are a subset of the electromagnetic spectrum with **frequencies from 3 Hz to 3000 Hz**, predominantly associated with the **50 Hz / 60 Hz** power transmission and distribution systems worldwide. They are non-ionizing, non-thermal in most occupational settings, and have biological effects primarily through **induced electric currents** in the body.

> [!NOTE]
> **India uses 50 Hz; USA uses 60 Hz.** In Kerala, all power lines and industrial supplies operate at **50 Hz sinusoidal alternating current**, making this an everyday occupational concern.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 LASER – Hazards, Classification, and Exposure Limits

#### 2.1.1 Biological Effects of Laser Radiation

The damage potential of a laser depends on four interdependent physical parameters:

$$Hazard \propto Power \times Duration \times Wavelength \times Beam \ Geometry$$

**A. Ocular Hazards (Most Critical – The eye is the most vulnerable organ):**

| Laser Type | Wavelength | Principal Ocular Effect | Site of Damage |
|------------|-----------|------------------------|----------------|
| UV-C Laser | 200 – 280 nm | Photokeratitis ("welder's flash") | Cornea |
| UV-B Laser | 280 – 315 nm | Photokeratitis, cataractogenesis | Cornea, lens |
| UV-A Laser | 315 – 400 nm | Cataract, retinal injury | Lens, retina |
| Visible (400–780 nm) | — | Retinal burn, scotoma (blind spot) | **Retina (focused spot)** |
| IR-A Laser (780 nm – 1.4 μm) | — | Retinal burn, cataract | Retina, lens |
| IR-B / IR-C Laser | > 1.4 μm | Corneal burn, aqueous flare | Cornea |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** The cornea has a focal length of about **1.7 cm** and focuses incoming light onto the retina. Visible and near-IR lasers (400–1400 nm) can concentrate beam intensity by a factor of **~100,000×** at the retina — a 1 mW He-Ne laser can cause retinal damage even though it's harmless to skin.

**B. Skin Hazards:**
- **Thermal burns** (IR, visible laser)
- **Photochemical damage** (UV laser → DNA damage)
- **Carcinogenesis** (chronic UV exposure)
- **Flash burns** from pulsed lasers (CO₂, Nd:YAG)

#### 2.1.2 Laser Classification System (KTU High-Yield)

The internationally accepted classification (ANSI Z136.1 / IEC 60825-1) used in all KTU questions:

| Class | Power / Hazard | Description | Example |
|-------|----------------|-------------|---------|
| **Class 1** | < 0.4 µW (visible) | Inherently safe; emissions below MPE | CD player, laser printer |
| **Class 2** | < 1 mW (visible) | Blink reflex (0.25 s) protects eye | Barcode scanner, laser pointer |
| **Class 3a** | 1 – 5 mW | Eye damage via optical aids | Surveying equipment |
| **Class 3b** | 5 – 500 mW | Direct beam causes eye injury; diffuse reflection safe | Research lasers |
| **Class 4** | > 500 mW | Eye AND skin injury; diffuse reflection hazardous; fire risk | Industrial cutting, surgical lasers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** "Class 4 lasers are the most dangerous and require the strictest controls." — This line alone has scored full marks multiple times.

#### 2.1.3 Maximum Permissible Exposure (MPE) Formula

The MPE is the highest power or energy density (W/cm² or J/cm²) of a laser beam that the eye/skin can be exposed to without biological damage:

$$MPE = \frac{0.0019 \cdot t^{-0.25} \cdot 10^{(\lambda - 0.700)}{0.515} }{A} \quad \text{[J/cm²]}$$

Where:
- $t$ = exposure duration in seconds
- $\lambda$ = wavelength in micrometers
- $A$ = limiting aperture area (cm²)

**Simplified MPE for visible lasers (400–700 nm), t = 0.25 s to 10 s:**

$$MPE = 1.8 \cdot t^{0.75} \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{[J/cm²]}$$

#### 2.1.4 Nominal Ocular Hazard Distance (NOHD)

$$NOHD = \frac{1}{\theta} \left[ \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot P}{\pi \cdot MPE}} - a \right]$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = beam divergence (radians)
- $P$ = laser power (W)
- $a$ = beam radius at exit (cm)

This defines the safe viewing distance from the laser.

---

### 2.2 INFRA-RED (IR) Radiation – Hazards and Limits

#### 2.2.1 Sources of Occupational IR Exposure

- **Glass and metal furnaces** (most intense: > 1 kW/m² at 1 m)
- **Electric arc welding** (visible light + IR-A)
- **Foundry work** (molten metals)
- **Steel mills, rolling mills**
- **Kilns and ovens**
- **High-intensity incandescent lamps**
- **IR heat lamps** (therapeutic and industrial drying)

#### 2.2.2 Biological Effects of IR

| Tissue | Effect | Mechanism |
|--------|--------|-----------|
| Cornea | Aqueous flare, "glass-blower's cataract" | Thermal coagulation of lens proteins |
| Lens | Cataract (chronic exposure) | Cumulative thermal denaturation |
| Retina | Thermal burn (rare, requires direct intense source) | IR-A absorption by choroid |
| Skin | Erythema, "Erythema ab igne" (reticular pigmentation) | Direct thermal heating |
| Skin | Deep tissue heating → heat stress | Vasodilation, dehydration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Glass-blower's cataract** is the classical occupational disease of IR exposure — the name itself is a KTU exam favourite. It results from chronic, cumulative exposure to intense IR-B/IR-C from furnaces.

#### 2.2.3 ACGIH Threshold Limit Values (TLV) for IR

| Wavelength | Exposure Limit (8-hour TWA) |
|------------|----------------------------|
| IR-A (770 – 1400 nm) | 10 W/m² (eye) |
| IR-B & IR-C (1400 nm – 1 mm) | Calculated to prevent corneal injury (typically 100 W/m²) |

**Heat Stress Equation (Stefan-Boltzmann, for IR intensity from a hot source):**

$$I = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot T^{4}$$

Where:
- $I$ = radiated power density (W/m²)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of the source (0 to 1)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

---

### 2.3 ULTRA-VIOLET (UV) Radiation – Hazards and Limits

#### 2.3.1 Sources of Occupational UV Exposure

- **Sunlight** (most significant overall source)
- **Arc welding** (UV-C dominant — extremely hazardous)
- **Germicidal lamps** (UV-C, 254 nm)
- **Mercury vapour lamps**
- **Tanning beds** (UV-A + UV-B)
- **Plasma torches, electron beams**
- **Photolithography in semiconductor industry**
- **Blacklight lamps** (UV-A)

#### 2.3.2 Biological Effects of UV by Sub-Band

| Sub-band | Wavelength | Principal Effect | Action Spectrum Peak |
|----------|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **UV-A** | 315 – 400 nm | Skin tanning, ageing, "arc eye" (delayed), glass-blower's cataract | 365 nm |
| **UV-B** | 280 – 315 nm | Sunburn (erythema), skin cancer (DNA dimer formation), snow blindness | 297 nm |
| **UV-C** | 100 – 280 nm | Photokeratitis, severe erythema, sterilization (germicidal), DNA damage | 254 nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Erythema Action Spectrum (McKinlay–Diffey):** Biological effectiveness of UV is non-linear and peaks at **297 nm (UV-B)**. UV-C from welding is far more erythemal per unit dose than UV-A.

**CIE Reference Erythema Dose:**

$$1 \ MED = 21.6 \ mJ/cm^{2} \ \text{(at 297 nm)}$$

A **Minimal Erythemal Dose (MED)** of UV-B is approximately **20 mJ/cm²** for unacclimatized skin (Type II).

#### 2.3.3 UV Exposure Limits (TLV – ACGIH / ICNIRP)

| Wavelength | Daily TLV (8-h exposure) |
|------------|--------------------------|
| 270 nm | 30 J/m² |
| 297 nm | 100 J/m² (peak permissible) |
| 315 nm | 10,000 J/m² |
| 400 nm | 10,000 J/m² |

**Effective UV irradiance (weighted by action spectrum):**

$$E_{eff} = \sum_{\lambda} E_{\lambda} \cdot S_{\lambda} \cdot \Delta \lambda$$

Where $S_{\lambda}$ is the relative spectral effectiveness at wavelength $\lambda$.

---

### 2.4 EXTREMELY LOW FREQUENCY (ELF) Radiation – Hazards and Limits

#### 2.4.1 Sources of ELF Fields

- **High-voltage overhead power transmission lines** (400 kV, 220 kV)
- **Distribution transformers and substations**
- **Industrial electric motors and generators**
- **Induction heating equipment**
- **Underground power cables** (concentrated field intensity near the cable)
- **Household wiring, appliances** (typically low intensity)
- **Electric trains, traction systems**

#### 2.4.2 Field Quantities and Units

ELF radiation comprises two coupled fields:

**A. Electric Field (E-field):**
- Unit: **Volts per metre (V/m)**
- Source: Voltage on conductors
- Shielded easily by most materials (concrete, wood, trees)

**B. Magnetic Field (B-field or H-field):**
- Unit: **Tesla (T)** or **Gauss (G)** where 1 G = 10⁻⁴ T
- Source: Current flowing through conductors
- **NOT** shielded by most materials — only by special mu-metal enclosures

**Biot-Savart Law (magnetic field from a straight current-carrying wire):**

$$B = \frac{\mu_{0} \cdot I}{2 \pi \cdot r}$$

Where:
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (T)
- $\mu_{0}$ = permeability of free space = **4π × 10⁻⁷ T·m/A**
- $I$ = current (A)
- $r$ = perpendicular distance from wire (m)

#### 2.4.3 ICNIRP Reference Levels for 50 Hz ELF (Occupational)

| Field | Reference Level | Public Exposure Limit |
|-------|----------------|----------------------|
| E-field (50 Hz) | **10,000 V/m** (10 kV/m) | 5,000 V/m |
| B-field (50 Hz) | **500 µT** | 100 µT (IARC "possible carcinogen" guideline) |
| Current density (induced) | 10 mA/m² (CNS effects threshold) | 2 mA/m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **IARC Classification (2002):** ELF magnetic fields are classified as **Group 2B – "possibly carcinogenic to humans"** based on epidemiological evidence linking chronic exposure **> 0.4 µT** to a 2-fold increase in childhood leukaemia risk. This is a KTU must-know fact.

#### 2.4.4 Induced Current Density in the Human Body

$$J = \sigma \cdot E_{in}$$

Where:
- $J$ = induced current density (A/m²)
- $\sigma$ = tissue electrical conductivity (~0.2 S/m for muscle)
- $E_{in}$ = internal electric field (V/m)

**Threshold biological effects:**
- **> 1 mA/m²**: Subtle neuro-behavioural changes
- **> 10 mA/m²**: Established CNS effects, magnetophosphenes (visual flickering)
- **> 100 mA/m²**: Possible excitability changes in nerve tissue
- **> 1 A/m²**: Cardiac stimulation threshold (extreme exposure)

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 8 equations for full marks in numerical/derivation questions:**

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules / eV |
| 2 | $I = \sigma \varepsilon T^{4}$ | IR radiated intensity from hot source | W/m² |
| 3 | $B = \frac{\mu_{0} I}{2\pi r}$ | ELF magnetic field from wire | Tesla |
| 4 | $NOHD = \frac{1}{\theta}\left[\sqrt{\frac{4P}{\pi \cdot MPE}} - a\right]$ | Laser safe distance | cm or m |
| 5 | $MPE = 1.8 t^{0.75} \times 10^{-3}$ | MPE for visible laser (0.25–10 s) | J/cm² |
| 6 | $E_{eff} = \sum E_{\lambda} S_{\lambda} \Delta \lambda$ | Effective UV irradiance | W/m² |
| 7 | $J = \sigma E_{in}$ | Induced current density in tissue | A/m² |
| 8 | $1 \text{ MED} = 21.6 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$ | Minimal Erythemal Dose (at 297 nm) | mJ/cm² |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Photon Energy Conversion for UV-B Peak

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** *Calculate the photon energy of UV-B radiation at its action spectrum peak (λ = 297 nm). Express in eV.*

**Step 1 — Identify the given data.**
Wavelength $\lambda = 297 \text{ nm} = 297 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$
Planck's constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$

**Step 2 — Apply the photon energy equation in Joules.**

$$E = \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$E = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{297 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{2.97 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$E = 6.693 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Joules to electron-volts (1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J).**

$$E = \frac{6.693 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 4.18 \text{ eV}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (engineering meaning).**
At ~4.18 eV, the UV-B photon carries enough energy to break weak chemical bonds (C–H ~4.3 eV; π-bonds in DNA < 4.5 eV), explaining its strong photochemical action — it damages DNA and causes sunburn even at low intensities.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: MPE Calculation for a Class 3b Visible Laser

**Problem:** *A 5 mW He-Ne laser (λ = 632.8 nm) is used in a laboratory. The accidental exposure time is estimated at 0.25 s. Calculate the MPE in J/cm².*

**Step 1 — Identify the conditions.**
- Wavelength: 632.8 nm → falls in **visible** range (400–700 nm)
- Exposure time $t = 0.25$ s (the upper limit for blink reflex)
- Therefore the **simplified MPE formula** applies.

**Step 2 — Apply the simplified visible-laser MPE formula.**

$$MPE = 1.8 \cdot t^{0.75} \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{[J/cm²]}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the time exponent.**

$$t^{0.75} = (0.25)^{0.75}$$

$$\log(0.25) = -0.6021 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.75 \times (-0.6021) = -0.4516$$

$$(0.25)^{0.75} = 10^{-0.4516} = 0.3548$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the constants.**

$$MPE = 1.8 \times 0.3548 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$MPE = 0.6386 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{MPE \approx 6.39 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J/cm}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the actual beam power density.**
Beam area at exit aperture ≈ 0.01 cm² (typical He-Ne).
Energy delivered in 0.25 s = 5 mW × 0.25 s = 1.25 × 10⁻³ J.
Energy density = 1.25 × 10⁻³ / 0.01 = 0.125 J/cm².
Since 0.125 J/cm² > 0.000639 J/cm² → **the exposure EXCEEDS the MPE by ~196×** → serious ocular hazard.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Magnetic Field at Worker Position from a 3-Phase Power Line

**Problem:** *A maintenance worker stands 10 m below a 220 kV transmission line carrying 1000 A. Estimate the magnetic flux density at the worker's position.*

**Step 1 — Use the Biot-Savart law for a single conductor (per phase).**

$$B_{\text{per phase}} = \frac{\mu_{0} \cdot I}{2 \pi \cdot r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$B_{\text{per phase}} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000}{2\pi \times 10}$$

$$B_{\text{per phase}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{10} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$$

$$B_{\text{per phase}} = 20 \ \mu\text{T}$$

**Step 3 — Account for the 3-phase contribution (vector sum, geometric arrangement).**
In a balanced 3-phase system, the RMS magnetic field at ground level is approximately:

$$B_{3\phi} \approx \frac{\mu_{0} \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot I}{2\pi \cdot r_{\text{geometric}}}$$

Using $r_{\text{geometric}} \approx 10$ m (simplified — exact value depends on tower geometry):

$$B_{3\phi} \approx \sqrt{3} \times 20 \ \mu\text{T}$$

$$B_{3\phi} \approx 34.6 \ \mu\text{T}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ICNIRP occupational reference level.**

$$34.6 \ \mu\text{T} \ll 500 \ \mu\text{T (occupational limit)}$$

**Conclusion:** *The magnetic field at the worker's position is well within ICNIRP limits, but approaches the IARC "possible carcinogen" threshold of **0.4 µT (400 nT)** × 100 = 40 µT. For chronic occupational exposure, mitigation may be justified.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The field falls off as **1/r** from a wire and **1/r²** from a power line pair — doubling the distance quarters the magnetic field. Distance is the simplest and most effective ELF control.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Stefan-Boltzmann IR Intensity from a Furnace

**Problem:** *A steel furnace operates at 1500°C. Estimate the IR intensity at 1 m from a small viewing port (emissivity ε = 0.85).*

**Step 1 — Convert temperature to Kelvin.**

$$T = 1500 + 273 = 1773 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Stefan-Boltzmann law.**

$$I = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot T^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $T^4$.**

$$T^{4} = (1773)^{4}$$

$$1773^{2} = 3{,}143{,}529$$
$$1773^{4} = (3{,}143{,}529)^{2} \approx 9.88 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by constants.**

$$I = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.85 \times 9.88 \times 10^{12}$$

$$I = 5.67 \times 0.85 \times 9.88 \times 10^{4}$$

$$I = 47.6 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 4.76 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/m}^2 \ = \ 476 \text{ kW/m}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to relevant occupational units and compare.**

$$I = 47{,}600 \text{ mW/cm}^2 \quad \text{(intense thermal load)}$$

**Comparison:** This is **~4760× higher** than the ACGIH occupational TLV for IR-A (10 W/m²). Even with a #5 IR-absorbing welding filter (transmission < 0.1%), the residual intensity would be ~476 W/m², still requiring eye protection beyond simple goggles.

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-World Implication:** Furnace workers without proper IR-blocking face shields (shade 4–6) can develop **glass-blower's cataract** within 5–10 years of chronic exposure. The intensity scales with $T^4$ — even a 10% rise in temperature (to 1650°C) increases the IR intensity by **~46%**.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Hazard Matrix – The KTU Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most-asked KTU question format: "Compare the hazards of [X] and [Y]."**

| Parameter | **Laser** | **Infra-Red (IR)** | **Ultra-Violet (UV)** | **ELF** |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|---------|
| Wavelength | Optical (UV to IR) | 780 nm – 1 mm | 100 – 400 nm | > 100 km |
| Energy per photon | 0.05 – 6 eV | 0.001 – 1.6 eV | 3.3 – 124 eV | < 10⁻¹¹ eV |
| Principal mechanism | Photothermal + photochemical | Thermal | Photochemical | Induced currents |
| Primary target organ | Eye (retina for visible) | Eye (lens), skin | Eye (cornea), skin | Whole body, CNS |
| Key chronic disease | Retinal burn, blindness | Glass-blower's cataract | Skin cancer, photokeratitis | Possible childhood leukaemia (Group 2B) |
| Acute symptom | Retinal scotoma, flash burn | Heat sensation, skin burn | Erythema, photokeratitis, "arc eye" | Magnetophosphenes (rare) |
| Primary control | Engineering, eyewear, MPE | Shielding, distance, PPE | PPE, time limits, sunscreen | Distance, shielding (for B-field) |
| TLV/OEL (occupational) | MPE (variable) | 10 W/m² (IR-A) | 30 J/m² (270 nm) | 500 µT (B), 10 kV/m (E) |
| Main occupational source | Industrial cutters, surgical | Furnaces, welding | Welding, sun, germicidal lamps | Power lines, transformers |

### 4.4 Schematic Description: Laser Laboratory Layout (Block Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid cannot render 3-D room plans; the following block-level schematic shows the engineered safety architecture of a Class 3b/4 laser facility (a KTU favourite).**

```
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  LASER LABORATORY SAFETY LAYOUT                  |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
|  [ENTRY]  -->  [WARNING SIGN: Class 4 Laser Area]              |
|     |                                                       |
|     v                                                       |
|  [DOOR INTERLOCK]  --> cuts power if door opens             |
|     |                                                       |
|     v                                                       |
|  [CONTROL CONSOLE with key switch]  <--  [LASER SAFETY OFFICER]|
|     |                                                       |
|     v                                                       |
|  +---[RESTRICTED ZONE: NOHD perimeter marked on floor]----+  |
|  |                                                        |  |
|  |  [OPTICAL TABLE] -- Class 4 laser (Nd:YAG, 1064 nm)    |  |
|  |       |                                               |  |
|  |       v                                               |  |
|  |  [BEAM ENCLOSURE] -- opaque, interlocked panels       |  |
|  |       |                                               |  |
|  |       v                                               |  |
|  |  [BEAM STOP] -- absorbs stray radiation               |  |
|  |                                                        |  |
|  |  [WINDOW with OD7 filter @ 1064 nm]                   |  |
|  |                                                        |  |
|  +--------------------------------------------------------+  |
|     |                                                       |
|     v                                                       |
|  [OPERATOR wearing OD-rated goggles + lab coat]            |
|     |                                                       |
|     v                                                       |
|  [EMERGENCY STOP button] -- cuts power to laser            |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Key engineered layers (read top-to-bottom):**
1. **Signage** → administrative awareness
2. **Interlock** → engineering control (fail-safe)
3. **Key switch** → restricts access to trained personnel
4. **NOHD marking** → defines physical safe distance
5. **Beam enclosure** → contains primary beam
6. **Beam stop** → dissipates residual beam
7. **Filtered window** → safe observation
8. **Operator PPE** → last line of defence
9. **Emergency stop** → rapid de-energization
