$$\dfrac{E_B}{E_A} = \dfrac{10.26}{6.40} \approx 1.60$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** Even though Lamp B has **4× the luminous intensity** of Lamp A (800 vs 200 cd), it produces only **1.6× the illuminance** at the specified point. This vividly demonstrates the dominance of the inverse-square factor — increasing intensity alone is an inefficient way to improve illuminance if the mounting height is also raised.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cosine term:** Many students write $E = I/d^2$ even when the surface is tilted. KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this oversight.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Writing "$E = I/d^2$" without stating units (lux) loses a **½-mark** in professional-engineering courses.
> 3. **Mixing up Luminance and Illuminance:** $E$ (lux) is incident flux per area; $L$ (cd/m²) is the perceived brightness. These are NOT interchangeable.
> 4. **Assuming cosine law applies to the angle from vertical:** In KTU answer scripts, the angle $\theta$ is **always measured from the surface normal**, not from the horizontal. Drawing the geometry saves marks.
> 5. **Ignoring maintenance factor:** In design problems, students often calculate lamps using initial flux; the **Maintenance Factor (0.6–0.8)** must be applied for realistic industrial design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principles of Good Illumination**

- ✅ The **four photometric quantities** are Flux (lm), Intensity (cd), Illuminance (lux), Luminance (cd/m²).
- ✅ **Inverse Square Law:** $E \propto 1/d^2$ — valid for point sources and normal incidence.
- ✅ **Lambert's Cosine Law:** $E \propto \cos\theta$ — accounts for tilted surfaces.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $E = I \cos\theta / d^{2}$ — the single most important formula.
- ✅ **1 lux = 1 lumen / m²**; **1 candela = 1 lumen / steradian**.
- ✅ Tripling distance reduces illuminance to **1/9th**, not 1/3rd.
- ✅ Tilting at $60°$ from normal halves the illuminance.
- ✅ **Uniformity Ratio** $U \geq 0.7$ for offices; lower values cause eye strain.
- ✅ **UGR (Glare Index)** must be **< 19** in offices.
- ✅ **CRI** must be $\geq 80$ for general work; $\geq 90$ for colour-critical tasks.
- ✅ **Maintenance Factor** (typically 0.6–0.8) accounts for lamp ageing and dirt accumulation.
- ✅ Indian Standard governing occupational lighting: **IS 3646 (Parts I–III)**.
- ✅ The **Factories Act, 1948 (Section 14)** mandates adequate lighting in industrial premises.
- ✅ Industrial lux benchmarks (IS 3646): General stores — 100 lx; Fine bench work — 300 lx; Precision assembly — 500–1000 lx; Inspection — 1000–1500 lx.
- ✅ LED is the modern preferred source due to high luminous efficacy ($\eta > 100$ lm/W).
- ✅ **Flicker** is an underestimated occupational hazard — must be controlled in fluorescent installations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Lighting Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Lighting Installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Lighting Installation Design** is the systematic engineering process of planning, calculating, and specifying artificial illumination systems in workplaces, industrial premises, and commercial spaces to achieve a required **illuminance level (lux)** uniformly across the work plane, while satisfying visual comfort, safety, energy-efficiency, and ergonomic criteria as mandated by national standards (e.g., **IS 3646**, **BIS**, **National Building Code of India**).

The design procedure integrates three engineering disciplines:
1. **Photometry** – quantitative measurement of visible light.
2. **Illumination Engineering** – application of physical laws to compute flux distribution.
3. **Ergonomics & Human Factors** – ensuring the lit environment supports visual performance without causing fatigue, glare, or accidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** The design of a lighting installation must be approached as a *numerical engineering problem* — students must be able to compute the **number of luminaires, lamp wattage, mounting height, and luminaire layout** from first principles, not just describe components qualitatively.

## 1.2 Key Photometric Quantities (The "Vocabulary" of Lighting)

Before any design can be attempted, the designer must master the five fundamental quantities. Every formula in lighting design is built from these.

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Symbol of Unit | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ or $F$ | lumen | **lm** | Total visible light energy emitted by a source per second |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | candela | **cd** | Luminous flux radiated per unit solid angle in a given direction |
| Illuminance | $E$ | lux | **lx** | Luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface |
| Luminance | $L$ | candela/m² | **cd/m²** | "Brightness" of a surface as perceived by the eye |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | lumen/watt | **lm/W** | Light output per unit electrical power consumed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook:** Lux is to light what Pascal is to pressure — it is the *flux density on a receiving surface*. One lux = one lumen spread over one square metre.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Lighting as Watering a Garden"

Imagine you are watering a rectangular garden with a sprinkler:
- The **total water sprayed** by the sprinkler is the *luminous flux* (lumens).
- The **concentration of spray in a particular direction** is the *luminous intensity* (candelas).
- The **amount of water actually falling on each square metre of grass** is the *illuminance* (lux).
- The **visible brightness of a wet leaf** is the *luminance* (cd/m²).
- The **water delivered per unit of electricity consumed by the pump** is the *luminous efficacy* (lm/W).

A "lighting design" is therefore exactly the problem of *choosing and positioning sprinklers (luminaires) so that the garden (work plane) receives the right depth of water (lux) uniformly* — without waste, without dry patches, and without flooding any spot.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Governing Laws of Illumination

### 2.1.1 The Inverse Square Law

**Statement:** *The illuminance produced on a surface by a point source varies inversely as the square of the distance between the source and the surface.*

**Why?** Light spreads out spherically. The surface area of a sphere of radius $d$ is $4\pi d^2$, so the same flux spreads over an area that grows as $d^2$ — the "dilution" is quadratic.

$$E \;\propto\; \frac{1}{d^{2}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad E = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$

Where:
- $E$ = illuminance on the surface (lux)
- $I$ = luminous intensity of the source towards the surface (cd)
- $d$ = perpendicular distance from source to surface (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Inverse Square Law is **valid only for a true point source** and only when the receiving surface is **normal (perpendicular)** to the direction of the light ray. Real lamps approach point-source behaviour only when $d \geq 5 \times$ the largest lamp dimension.

### 2.1.2 Lambert's Cosine Law

**Statement:** *The illuminance on a surface is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence of the light ray.*

**Why?** When light strikes a surface obliquely, the same beam is "stretched" over a larger projected area, so the lux falling on each square metre drops.

$$E = \frac{I \,\cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = angle between the incident ray and the **normal** to the receiving surface (degrees or radians)

> [!NOTE]
> At $\theta = 0°$ (ray perpendicular to surface), $\cos\theta = 1$ and the formula collapses to the Inverse Square Law. At $\theta = 60°$, illuminance is halved; at $\theta = 90°$, illuminance is zero (grazing incidence).

### 2.2 The Combined Fundamental Equation

Both laws are universally combined into the **Cosine-Cubed Formula** used in point-by-point calculations:

$$E_{h} = \frac{I_{\theta}\,\cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$$

Where:
- $E_h$ = horizontal illuminance at the point (lux)
- $I_\theta$ = luminous intensity of the luminaire in the direction $\theta$ (cd)
- $h$ = vertical mounting height of the luminaire above the work plane (m)
- $\theta$ = angle from the downward vertical to the line joining luminaire and point

The $\cos^3\theta$ arises from substituting $d = h/\cos\theta$ into the cosine law:
$$E = \frac{I\cos\theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{I\cos\theta}{(h/\cos\theta)^{2}} = \frac{I\cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}$$

## 2.3 The Lumen Method (Zonal Cavity Method) — The Industrial Workhorse

The Lumen Method is the **standard KTU-endorsed technique** for designing the general lighting of an entire interior. Unlike the Inverse Square Law (which is point-source), the Lumen Method gives the **average illuminance** over the whole room — exactly what occupational-health codes specify.

### 2.3.1 The Master Equation

$$N \;=\; \frac{E \times A}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times n \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of luminaires required
- $E$ = design illuminance on the work plane (lux)
- $A$ = total floor area $= L \times W$ (m²)
- $\Phi_{\text{lamp}}$ = rated luminous flux of **one lamp** (lumens)
- $n$ = number of lamps per luminaire
- UF = **Utilization Factor** (dimensionless, 0 < UF ≤ 1)
- MF = **Maintenance Factor** (dimensionless, MF ≤ 1)

### 2.3.2 Room Index (RI) — the Key to UF

The Utilization Factor is **not arbitrary** — it depends on the geometry of the room and the light distribution of the luminaire. It is tabulated against the dimensionless **Room Index**:

$$\text{RI} \;=\; \frac{L \times W}{H_{m}\,\bigl(L + W\bigr)}$$

Where:
- $L$ = room length (m)
- $W$ = room width (m)
- $H_m$ = mounting height of luminaire **above the work plane** (m)

> For the more rigorous **Zonal Cavity Method**, $H_m$ is split into three cavities: ceiling cavity $\,h_{cc}$, room cavity $H_R$, and floor cavity $h_{fc}$, with $H_m = h_{cc} + H_R + h_{fc}$.

### 2.3.3 Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR)

After computing $N$, luminaires must be placed so they do not create dark spots. The **Spacing-to-Height Ratio** (provided by the luminaire manufacturer) sets the maximum permissible spacing:

$$\text{SHR} = \frac{S}{H_{m}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad S_{\max} = \text{SHR} \times H_{m}$$

Where $S$ = centre-to-centre spacing of luminaires (m).

A typical fluorescent luminaire has SHR ≈ 1.0 to 1.5; an LED panel may reach 1.25; an industrial high-bay reflector may be only 0.6–0.8.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Print-Friendly Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = I / d^{2}$ | Inverse Square Law (point source, normal surface) | lux |
| 2 | $E = I\cos\theta / d^{2}$ | Lambert's Cosine Law (oblique incidence) | lux |
| 3 | $E_h = I_\theta \cos^{3}\theta / h^{2}$ | Point-by-point horizontal illuminance | lux |
| 4 | $E_v = I_\theta \cos^{2}\theta \sin\theta / h^{2}$ | Point-by-point **vertical** illuminance | lux |
| 5 | $N = E \cdot A \,/\, (\Phi \cdot n \cdot \text{UF} \cdot \text{MF})$ | Lumen Method — number of luminaires | — |
| 6 | $\text{RI} = L \cdot W \,/\, [H_m (L+W)]$ | Room Index for UF selection | — |
| 7 | $\text{SHR} = S_{\max} / H_m$ | Spacing-to-Height Ratio | — |
| 8 | $\text{MF} = 1 / (1 + d)$ where $d$ = fractional depreciation | Maintenance Factor (accounts for lamp ageing & dirt) | — |
| 9 | $\eta = \Phi / P$ | Luminous efficacy of a lamp | lm/W |
| 10 | $L = I / A_{\text{projected}}$ | Luminance of a luminous surface | cd/m² |
| 11 | $\text{LLF} = \text{UF} \times \text{MF}$ | Combined Light Loss Factor (some texts) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In a 14-mark Part-B question, you may be required to use the *Lumen Method* end-to-end. Memorise equations (5), (6) and (7) — they appear in 90% of past paper numericals. The Inverse Square Law and Cosine Law appear in 3-mark short-answer questions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Inverse Square + Cosine Law to the Cosine-Cubed Formula

This derivation is the bridge between the "laws" and the practical point-by-point calculation. The examiner loves it because it shows *understanding*, not just substitution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Start with Lambert's Cosine Law:}\quad E = \frac{I_{\theta}\,\cos\theta}{d^{2}} \quad \text{(general, oblique incidence)} \\[6pt]
&\text{Substitute the geometric relation:}\quad d = \frac{h}{\cos\theta} \\[6pt]
&\Rightarrow\quad d^{2} = \frac{h^{2}}{\cos^{2}\theta} \quad \text{(squaring both sides)} \\[6pt]
&\text{Insert into the Cosine Law:}\quad E = \frac{I_{\theta}\,\cos\theta}{h^{2}/\cos^{2}\theta} = \frac{I_{\theta}\,\cos\theta \cdot \cos^{2}\theta}{h^{2}} \\[6pt]
&\text{Combine the cosine powers:}\quad E = \frac{I_{\theta}\,\cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}} \quad \text{(horizontal illuminance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The *vertical* illuminance $E_v$ uses the horizontal projected area instead, giving $\cos^{2}\theta \sin\theta$ instead of $\cos^{3}\theta$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — A Complete Lumen-Method Design

This is the **typical 14-mark KTU problem** for Module 2. We will solve it in full, step-by-step.

> **Problem Statement (modelled on KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Design a general lighting installation for a machine-shop of dimensions **20 m × 12 m × 5 m (height)**. The work-plane is 0.85 m above the floor. A suspended luminaire is hung 0.6 m below the ceiling. Required illuminance $E = 300$ lux. Use metal-halide lamps, each delivering 20 000 lumens, **one lamp per luminaire**. The Utilization Factor for RI ≈ 1.5 is **0.55**, and the Maintenance Factor is **0.8**. The Spacing-to-Height Ratio of the chosen luminaire is **1.0**. Find: (a) number of luminaires, (b) total connected load if each lamp is 250 W, (c) check the layout.

### Solution

#### Step 1 — Mounting height above the work plane
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_m &= (\text{room height}) - (\text{pendant drop}) - (\text{work-plane height}) \\
    &= 5.0 - 0.6 - 0.85 \\
    &= 3.55 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Floor area
$$
A = L \times W = 20 \times 12 = 240 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

#### Step 3 — Room Index
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RI} &= \frac{L \times W}{H_{m} (L + W)} = \frac{20 \times 12}{3.55 \times (20 + 12)} \\[4pt]
         &= \frac{240}{3.55 \times 32} = \frac{240}{113.6} \approx 2.11
\end{aligned}
$$

> The computed RI (2.11) is reasonably close to the tabulated value of 1.5, but a more accurate UF may be interpolated. For a first-order KTU answer, we use **UF = 0.55** as given (interpolation is acceptable in the answer script — examiners award full marks if the student *notes* the interpolation need).

#### Step 4 — Number of luminaires (Master Lumen Equation)
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times n \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}} \\
  &= \frac{300 \times 240}{20\,000 \times 1 \times 0.55 \times 0.8} \\
  &= \frac{72\,000}{8\,800} \\
  &\approx 8.18
\end{aligned}
$$

Since you cannot install 0.18 of a luminaire, **round up** to the next whole number:
$$
\boxed{N = 9 \text{ luminaires}}
$$

#### Step 5 — Check spacing using SHR
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \text{SHR} \times H_m = 1.0 \times 3.55 = 3.55 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

To cover a 20 × 12 m room with 9 luminaires, the most regular layout is **3 rows × 3 columns**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Longitudinal spacing} &= 20 / 3 \approx 6.67 \text{ m} \quad (\text{too large!})\\
\text{Transverse spacing}  &= 12 / 3 = 4.00 \text{ m} \quad (\text{too large!})
\end{aligned}
$$

> Both exceed $S_{\max} = 3.55$ m — the design fails the spacing check. We must **revise the design**.

#### Step 6 — Redesign with a denser layout

Maximum spacing along the long side:
$$
\text{rows} = \lceil L / S_{\max} \rceil = \lceil 20 / 3.55 \rceil = \lceil 5.63 \rceil = 6
$$

If we use **6 rows** along the length and **4 rows** along the width, that is $6 \times 4 = 24$ luminaires — wasteful. Try **5 × 4 = 20** luminaires with slight over-spacing allowed (typical in industrial sheds).

Re-compute the actual illuminance with $N = 20$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{actual}} &= \frac{N \times \Phi \times n \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}{A} \\
                  &= \frac{20 \times 20\,000 \times 1 \times 0.55 \times 0.8}{240} \\
                  &= \frac{176\,000}{240} \approx 733 \text{ lux}
\end{aligned}
$$

That is **144 % over-designed**. Reduce lamp wattage — pick a smaller lamp. A 12 000-lumen lamp gives:
$$
E_{\text{actual}} = \frac{20 \times 12\,000 \times 0.55 \times 0.8}{240} = 440 \text{ lux}
$$

Still 47 % high. Try **15 luminaires of 18 000 lm each**:
$$
E_{\text{actual}} = \frac{15 \times 18\,000 \times 0.55 \times 0.8}{240} = 495 \text{ lux}
$$

Try **12 luminaires of 18 000 lm each** (4 × 3 layout, $S = 6.67/2 = 3.33$ m ≈ OK):
$$
E_{\text{actual}} = \frac{12 \times 18\,000 \times 0.55 \times 0.8}{240} = 396 \text{ lux} \;\;\text{(32 \% over — acceptable in industry)}
$$

**Final design:** **12 luminaires** of 18 000 lm metal-halide lamps, in a 4 × 3 array, total connected load $= 12 \times 250 = 3000$ W = 3.0 kW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Judgment:** A small over-design (10–30 %) is standard practice because (a) lamps depreciate, (b) the actual MF may be lower in dusty machine-shops, and (c) uniformity improves with more luminaires.

## 3.3 Quick-Reference Numerical — Point-by-Point Method

> **Problem:** A 200-cd lamp is mounted **4 m** above a desk. Find the illuminance (a) directly below the lamp, and (b) at a point **3 m horizontally** away on the desk.

### Solution

**(a) Directly below:**
$$
E_a = \frac{I}{d^2} = \frac{200}{4^2} = \frac{200}{16} = 12.5 \text{ lux}
$$

**(b) At 3 m horizontal offset:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{h^{2} + x^{2}} = \sqrt{4^{2} + 3^{2}} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m} \\
\cos\theta &= \frac{h}{d} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \\
E_b &= \frac{I\,\cos\theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{200 \times 0.8}{25} = \frac{160}{25} = 6.4 \text{ lux}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Direct illuminance = 12.5 lux; offset illuminance = 6.4 lux. The drop is approximately $1/(\cos^{3}\theta)$ in agreement with the cosine-cubed formula.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lighting-Design Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Room Dimensions L x W x H] --> B[Identify Work-Plane Height]
    B --> C[Compute Mounting Height Hm = H - Pendant - WorkPlane]
    C --> D[Select Lamp Type and Lumen Package Phi]
    D --> E[Look Up Reference Illuminance E from IS 3646 / NBC]
    E --> F[Compute Room Index RI = L*W / Hm*L+W]
    F --> G[Select Utilization Factor UF from Luminaire Table]
    G --> H[Select Maintenance Factor MF based on Environment]
    H --> I[Apply Lumen Method: N = E*A / Phi*n*UF*MF]
    I --> J{N is whole number?}
    J -- No --> K[Round Up to Nearest Integer]
    J -- Yes --> L[Proceed to Layout]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Max Spacing Smax = SHR * Hm]
    M --> N{Smax * rows >= L and Smax * cols >= W?}
    N -- No --> O[Increase Number of Luminaires]
    N -- Yes --> P[Finalise Layout: Rows x Columns]
    O --> L
    P --> Q[Compute Total Connected Load kW]
    Q --> R[Check Uniformity Ratio Emin/Emax]
    R --> S[End: Issue Luminaire Schedule and Layout Drawing]
```

## 4.2 The Three-Cavity Concept (Zonal Cavity Method)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Room["INDOOR SPACE"]
        CC[Ceiling Cavity h_cc]:::cav
        RC[Room Cavity H_R = Work Volume]:::cav
        FC[Floor Cavity h_fc]:::cav
    end
    Lum[Luminaire] --> CC
    CC --> RC
    RC --> FC
    FC --> WP[(Work Plane @ 0.85 m)]
    classDef cav fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a017,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Cavity Insight:** In the full Zonal Cavity Method, the UF is split into a **Ceiling Cavity Ratio (CCR)**, a **Room Cavity Ratio (RCR)** and a **Floor Cavity Ratio (FCR)**. The Lumen Method of this module uses the simplified *single* Room Index, but KTU students should know the term "Zonal Cavity Method" and the three-cavity concept for 3-mark questions.

## 4.3 Comparison Block: Lumen vs Point-by-Point Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Lumen Method]:::lm --> L1[Computes AVERAGE illuminance over the whole room]
    L --> L2[Uses Room Index and UF tables]
    L --> L3[Best for: General interior lighting design]

    P[Point-by-Point Method]:::pp --> P1[Computes illuminance at a SPECIFIC point]
    P --> P2[Uses Inverse Square + Cosine Law]
    P --> P3[Best for: Local / task lighting, sports fields, road lighting]

    classDef lm fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef pp fill:#ffe0cc,stroke:#993300,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q1a]**
> *State and explain the Inverse Square Law and Lambert's Cosine Law of illumination. Derive the cosine-cubed formula.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Inverse Square Law** states that the illuminance $E$ at a point on a surface, due to a point source, is inversely proportional to the square of the distance $d$ from the source:
$$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$

The **Lambert's Cosine Law** states that the illuminance is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence $\theta$ between the incident ray and the normal to the surface:
$$E = \frac{I\cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$

Combining both for a horizontal work plane at mounting height $h$, with $d = h/\cos\theta$:
$$\boxed{E_{h} = \frac{I_{\theta}\cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}}}$$

*(1 mark each for stating the laws + 1 mark for the derivation.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q1b]**
> *What is Utilization Factor? Mention any four factors on which it depends.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Utilization Factor (UF)** is the ratio of the luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps. It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1.

$$\text{UF} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{received on work plane}}}{\Phi_{\text{total emitted by lamps}}}$$

UF depends on:
1. **Room Index (RI)** — higher RI means more flux reaches the work plane.
2. **Reflection factors of ceiling, walls and floor** — lighter colours increase UF.
3. **Light distribution of the luminaire** — direct, indirect or mixed.
4. **Mounting height** — closer mounting generally increases UF in low-ceiling rooms.

*(1 mark definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for factors.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Internal Choice (Module 2)

> **Question A [14 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q7a]**

**(a)** *A factory hall of size 30 m × 15 m × 6 m is to be illuminated to a level of 200 lux. Using fluorescent luminaires, each with 2 lamps of 40 W (3 000 lm per lamp), suspended 0.5 m from ceiling. Work plane is 0.8 m above floor. UF for RI ≈ 1.5 is 0.6, MF = 0.75, SHR = 1.25. Calculate: (i) the number of luminaires, (ii) the total connected load, and (iii) verify the luminaire layout. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the difference between Luminance and Illuminance. A roadway luminaire produces 800 cd intensity at 30° below the horizontal. If the mounting height is 8 m, find the illuminance on the road surface directly beneath the luminaire and at 6 m horizontally away. (7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Mounting height**
$$H_m = 6.0 - 0.5 - 0.8 = 4.7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Floor area**
$$A = 30 \times 15 = 450 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Number of luminaires**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times n \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}} \\
  &= \frac{200 \times 450}{3\,000 \times 2 \times 0.6 \times 0.75} \\
  &= \frac{90\,000}{2\,700} \\
  &= 33.33
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding up, $\boxed{N = 34 \text{ luminaires}}$.

**Step 4: Total connected load**
$$P_{\text{total}} = 34 \times 2 \times 40 = 2\,720 \text{ W} = 2.72 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Layout verification**
$$
S_{\max} = 1.25 \times 4.7 = 5.875 \text{ m}
$$

Layout options: try $5 \times 7 = 35$ or $4 \times 9 = 36$. Choose **$5 \times 7 = 35$** luminaires (the +1 over the 34 needed provides uniformity).

- Longitudinal spacing: $30 / 5 = 6.0$ m (slightly over 5.875 m — acceptable, or move to 6 × 6 = 36).
- Transverse spacing: $15 / 7 = 2.14$ m (well within limit).

Revised to $6 \times 6 = 36$ luminaires:
- Longitudinal: $30/6 = 5.0$ m ✔
- Transverse: $15/6 = 2.5$ m ✔

Revised load: $36 \times 2 \times 40 = 2.88$ kW.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Luminance vs Illuminance (3 marks):**

| Property | Illuminance ($E$) | Luminance ($L$) |
|----------|--------------------|------------------|
| Definition | Luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface | Luminous intensity per unit projected area of a luminous/emitting surface |
| Unit | lux (lx) | candela/m² (cd/m²) |
| What it measures | Light *falling on* a surface | Light *leaving* a surface (brightness) |
| Affected by | Source intensity, distance, angle | Surface reflectance, transmittance, view angle |

**Numerical (4 marks):**

*Directly beneath:* Ray is vertical, $\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$, $d = h = 8$ m.
$$E_{\text{below}} = \frac{I \cos^{3}\theta}{h^{2}} = \frac{800 \times 1}{8^{2}} = \frac{800}{64} = 12.5 \text{ lux}$$

*At 6 m horizontal:* $d = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{100} = 10$ m, $\cos\theta = 8/10 = 0.8$.
$$
E_{6\text{m}} = \frac{800 \times 0.8^{3}}{8^{2}} = \frac{800 \times 0.512}{64} = \frac{409.6}{64} = 6.4 \text{ lux}
$$

---

> **Question B [14 marks] [Internal Choice, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Describe the Lumen Method of lighting calculation in detail. List the data required and the step-by-step procedure. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 60 m × 30 m workshop has a mounting height of 5 m. Luminaires each have 4 fluorescent tubes of 36 W, 3 350 lm per tube. UF = 0.5, MF = 0.7. Required illuminance 250 lux. Determine: (i) number of luminaires, (ii) the spacing, assuming SHR = 1.2. (7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The Lumen Method (also called the Zonal Cavity Method) computes the **average illuminance** over a horizontal work plane using the **Master Equation:**

$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot n \cdot \text{UF} \cdot \text{MF}}$$

**Data required:**
1. Room dimensions $L$, $W$, $H$ (m)
2. Work-plane height and pendant drop → $H_m$
3. Required illuminance $E$ (from **IS 3646** or task analysis)
4. Lamp type, wattage, and rated luminous flux $\Phi$
5. Number of lamps per luminaire $n$
6. Utilization Factor UF (from luminaire manufacturer's tables at the calculated RI)
7. Maintenance Factor MF (from cleaning schedule and environment)
8. Spacing-to-Height Ratio SHR of the luminaire

**Step-by-step procedure:**
1. Compute floor area $A = L \times W$.
2. Compute mounting height $H_m$ above the work plane.
3. Compute Room Index $\text{RI} = L W / [H_m(L+W)]$.
4. From the luminaire UF table, read UF at the nearest RI.
5. Compute $N$ using the master equation; **round up** to whole number.
6. Compute $S_{\max} = \text{SHR} \times H_m$.
7. Choose a layout (rows × columns) such that $L / \text{rows} \le S_{\max}$ and $W / \text{cols} \le S_{\max}$.
8. Compute total load and verify uniformity.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Area**
$$A = 60 \times 30 = 1\,800 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Lumen package per luminaire**
$$\Phi \times n = 3\,350 \times 4 = 13\,400 \text{ lm per luminaire}$$

**Step 3: Number of luminaires**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot n \cdot \text{UF} \cdot \text{MF}} \\
  &= \frac{250 \times 1\,800}{13\,400 \times 0.5 \times 0.7} \\
  &= \frac{450\,000}{4\,690} \approx 95.9
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding up: $\boxed{N = 96 \text{ luminaires}}$.

**Step 4: Spacing**
$$S_{\max} = 1.2 \times 5 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Layout (rows along length, columns along width):**
- Minimum rows along length: $\lceil 60 / 6 \rceil = 10$
- Minimum columns along width: $\lceil 30 / 6 \rceil = 5$
- Minimum total: $10 \times 5 = 50$ ✔ (96 > 50, OK)

A symmetric 96-luminaire layout: $12 \times 8$ = 96.
- $S_{\text{long}} = 60 / 12 = 5.0$ m ✔
- $S_{\text{trans}} = 30 / 8 = 3.75$ m ✔

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** $N$ to the next whole luminaire (loss of 1 mark).
> 2. **Skipping the spacing check** — examiners explicitly ask to "verify the layout" and award 2 marks for it.
> 3. **Mixing up UF and MF** — UF relates to *room geometry & luminaire optics*; MF relates to *depreciation over time*. Confusing them is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Using $H$ (ceiling height) instead of $H_m$ (mounting height above work plane)** in the RI formula — this is the single most common error.
> 5. **Not writing units** in the final answer (lux, m, W) — KTU examiners penalise unit omissions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Five photometric quantities** to memorise: Flux (lm), Intensity (cd), Illuminance (lx), Luminance (cd/m²), Efficacy (lm/W).
- [ ] **Inverse Square Law** $E = I/d^2$ — point source, normal surface.
- [ ] **Lambert's Cosine Law** $E = I\cos\theta/d^2$ — oblique incidence.
- [ ] **Cosine-cubed formula** $E_h = I_\theta \cos^3\theta / h^2$ — used in point-by-point.
- [ ] **Lumen Method master equation** $N = E \cdot A \,/\, (\Phi \cdot n \cdot \text{UF} \cdot \text{MF})$.
- [ ] **Room Index** $\text{RI} = L W / [H_m (L+W)]$ — use $H_m$, *not* $H$.
- [ ] **Maintenance Factor** accounts for lamp lumen depreciation and luminaire dirt; UF is a function of RI and reflectance.
- [ ] **Spacing-to-Height Ratio** $\text{SHR} = S_{\max}/H_m$ — must verify layout against it.
- [ ] Always **round up** the number of luminaires; never round down.
- [ ] Reference standards for Indian design practice: **IS 3646**, **National Building Code (NBC) 2016**, **BIS**, and **IS 6665** for lighting of roads.
- [ ] Typical design illuminance values (lux): rough bench/machine work ≈ 300, fine bench work ≈ 500, very fine work ≈ 1 000+; offices ≈ 300–500; corridors ≈ 100.
- [ ] The **Lumen Method** computes *average* illuminance; the **Point-by-Point Method** computes illuminance at a *specific* location — use the right one as the question demands.
- [ ] In KTU numericals, always state the **assumed UF and MF** before substitution; examiners look for the designer's judgment, not just arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Occupational Health, Hygiene and First Aid**, **Maintenance** is defined as the systematic set of technical, administrative, and managerial actions taken during the life cycle of an industrial plant, machinery, equipment, or workplace infrastructure to **preserve its functional integrity, ensure continued reliability, and uphold occupational safety standards**.

According to the **British Standard BS 3811:1984** (the de-facto industrial reference adopted by KTU), maintenance is:

> *"The combination of all technical and associated administrative actions intended to retain an item in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform its required function."*

Within occupational health frameworks, maintenance extends beyond machinery to include the **upkeep of First Aid stations, emergency equipment, ventilation systems, fire-suppression networks, sanitary facilities, and personal protective equipment (PPE)** — all of which directly influence worker wellness and statutory compliance under the **Factories Act 1948** and **OSHA regulations**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT] **The "Human Body" Analogy**
> Think of an industrial plant exactly like a human body:
> - **Preventive maintenance** = Brushing teeth daily (small, regular effort prevents a painful root canal).
> - **Breakdown maintenance** = Waiting until the tooth cracks before visiting a dentist (high pain, high cost, lost workdays).
> - **Predictive maintenance** = Annual health check-ups that detect cavities via X-ray *before* you feel pain.
> - **First aid kit maintenance** = Checking the expiry date of medicines in your home pharmacy.
> - **The "body" (plant) cannot function if its "organs" (machines, safety systems) are diseased or decayed.** Just as a company must keep its workforce healthy, it must keep its equipment healthy.

## 1.3 Core Maintenance Metrics in Bold

The following standard engineering metrics govern every maintenance decision and frequently appear in KTU numerical problems:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — Average running time between two consecutive breakdowns.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** — Average time required to restore a failed item to working condition.
- **Availability (A)** — The fraction of time a system is operationally ready.
- **Reliability R(t)** — Probability that a system performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time interval.
- **Failure Rate (λ)** — Number of failures per unit time.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** — For non-repairable items, the expected time to first failure.

> [!NOTE] **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Module 2 of PCSFT503 explicitly links maintenance to **occupational health outcomes**. You are not just studying engineering maintenance — you are studying it as a **public health intervention** that prevents accidents, occupational diseases, and emergency failures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Maintenance (Structured Logic Breakdown)

Maintenance is broadly classified into **two super-categories** that further divide into specific strategies:

### 2.1.1 Preventive Maintenance (PM)
- Performed **before** failure occurs.
- **Scheduled** (calendar-based) or **usage-based** (e.g., every 5,000 hours).
- Includes routine servicing: lubrication, calibration, filter replacement, inspection.
- Reduces unexpected downtime and extends asset life.
- **Examples:** Annual servicing of an air compressor, weekly inspection of fire extinguishers, monthly calibration of gas detectors.

### 2.1.2 Corrective Maintenance (CM) / Breakdown Maintenance
- Performed **after** failure occurs.
- Two subtypes:
  - **Unplanned / Reactive** — Emergency repair; highest cost and downtime.
  - **Planned / Deferred** — Repair scheduled after failure but during a planned shutdown.
- **Example:** Replacing a failed conveyor motor after it trips on overload.

### 2.1.3 Predictive Maintenance (PdM) / Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)
- Uses real-time monitoring to predict failure *before* it occurs.
- Techniques: **Vibration analysis, thermography, oil analysis, ultrasonic testing, acoustic emission**.
- **Why it matters:** Maintenance is performed only when indicators show deterioration, optimizing cost and resource use.

### 2.1.4 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
- **Japanese-origin philosophy** (Nakajima, 1971) — Zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents.
- Eight pillars: Autonomous Maintenance, Planned Maintenance, Quality Maintenance, Focused Improvement, Early Equipment Management, Training, Safety, Office TPM.
- Operates on the principle: **"Who uses it, maintains it"** — empowering operators as first-line maintainers.

### 2.1.5 Design-Out Maintenance
- Engineering the equipment at the design stage to make maintenance easier (modular design, accessibility, standardization).
- Not a daily activity but a strategic maintenance philosophy.

## 2.2 Maintenance in Occupational Health & First Aid Context

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Maintenance is a Health Issue**
> 1. **Faulty machinery = accidents** (entanglement, crushing, electrical shock).
> 2. **Poorly maintained ventilation = respiratory diseases** (pneumoconiosis, silicosis).
> 3. **Expired first-aid supplies = treatment failure** in emergencies.
> 4. **Malfunctioning fire systems = fatalities**.
> 5. **Worn PPE = worker exposure** to chemicals, heat, radiation.

**Statutory Requirement (India):** Under **Section 21 of the Factories Act 1948**, all dangerous parts of machinery must be securely fenced; under **Rule 63**, first-aid boxes must be maintained and accessible. Maintenance is therefore a **legal obligation**, not optional.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1), often expressed as % |
| 2 | **Unavailability (U)** | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Fraction of time system is down |
| 3 | **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failures per hour (assumes exponential distribution) |
| 4 | **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of no failure up to time $t$ |
| 5 | **MTTF (non-repairable)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours (only for non-repaired items) |
| 6 | **Hazard / Hazard Rate** | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \lambda$ (constant for exponential) | Conditional probability of failure |
| 7 | **Bathtub Curve Phases** | $h(t) = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma e^{-\delta t}$ (conceptual) | Infant mortality → useful life → wear-out |
| 8 | **Number of spares (s)** | $s = \dfrac{D \cdot L}{T}$ | Where $D$ = annual demand, $L$ = lead time, $T$ = cycle |
| 9 | **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Availability × Performance × Quality |
| 10 | **Optimal PM Interval (Square root law)** | $T_{opt} = T_{0} \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{p} + C_{c}}{C_{p}}}$ | $C_{p}$ = PM cost, $C_{c}$ = CM cost |

> [!WARNING] **Markdown Safety Note on Tables**
> The table above uses **$\vert$** style separators inside math via LaTeX rendering — the raw markdown pipe `$\vert$` is preserved as an inline math token, **not** as a table column delimiter. Never write `|x|` raw inside a cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Manufacturing plants:** TPM drives 30–50% reduction in breakdown losses.
- **Hospitals:** Predictive maintenance on MRI/ventilator machines is a life-safety issue.
- **Power plants:** Refractory lining maintenance prevents catastrophic boiler explosions.
- **Construction:** Scaffold inspection and maintenance prevents falls (a leading cause of occupational fatalities per ILO).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Maintenance of cleanroom HEPA filters is essential to drug sterility and worker protection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Availability from MTBF and MTTR

We define the **steady-state availability** $A$ as the long-run fraction of time the system is operational.

**Step 1: Define the average cycle time.**
A system alternates between two states: **UP** (working) and **DOWN** (under repair). One complete cycle consists of one UP period and one DOWN period.

$$
T_{cycle} = T_{up} + T_{down}
$$

**Step 2: Express in terms of mean values.**
For a large number of cycles, $T_{up}$ converges to **MTBF** and $T_{down}$ converges to **MTTR**.

$$
T_{cycle} = MTBF + MTTR
$$

**Step 3: Define availability as the ratio of UP time to total cycle time.**

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{up}}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 4: Derive the unavailability complement.**

$$
U = 1 - A = 1 - \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{MTBF + MTTR - MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

$$
U = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 5: Final closed-form relationships.**

$$
A + U = 1
$$

This is the most tested formula in KTU maintenance problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability Function (Exponential Distribution)

We assume a constant failure rate $\lambda$ (valid during the useful life phase of the bathtub curve).

**Step 1: Define the reliability of an item surviving a small interval $\Delta t$.**

$$
R(t + \Delta t) = R(t) \cdot (1 - \lambda \, \Delta t)
$$

This says: the probability of surviving to $t + \Delta t$ equals the probability of surviving to $t$ multiplied by the probability of surviving the next instant.

**Step 2: Rearrange to form a difference equation.**

$$
R(t + \Delta t) - R(t) = -\lambda \, R(t) \, \Delta t
$$

**Step 3: Divide both sides by $\Delta t$.**

$$
\dfrac{R(t + \Delta t) - R(t)}{\Delta t} = -\lambda \, R(t)
$$

**Step 4: Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$ to get a differential equation.**

$$
\dfrac{dR}{dt} = -\lambda \, R(t)
$$

**Step 5: Solve the separable ODE.**

$$
\int \dfrac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int dt
$$

$$
\ln R = -\lambda t + C
$$

**Step 6: Apply boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (item is reliable at time zero).**

$$
\ln 1 = 0 = C
$$

**Step 7: Exponentiate to obtain the final reliability function.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 8: Substitute $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-t / MTBF}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for Non-Repairable Items

**Step 1: Recall that the probability density function of failure is:**

$$
f(t) = \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 2: MTTF is the expected value of time to failure.**

$$
MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

**Step 3: Solve by integration by parts.** Let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$
MTTF = \left[ -t \, e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the boundary term.** The first term vanishes at both limits (exponential dominates).

$$
MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the integral.**

$$
MTTF = \left[ \dfrac{-1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left( \dfrac{-1}{\lambda} \right) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 6: Final result.**

$$
MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}
$$

For exponential distribution, **MTTF = MTBF = 1/λ**, which is why these are often conflated in KTU problems (though conceptually distinct for non-repairable vs. repairable systems).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A critical pump in a chemical plant has an MTBF of **800 hours** and MTTR of **200 hours**. Calculate: (a) Availability, (b) Unavailability, (c) Reliability for a 100-hour mission, (d) Failure rate.

**Solution:**

**(a) Availability:**

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{800}{800 + 200} = \dfrac{800}{1000} = 0.80 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 80\%
$$

**(b) Unavailability:**

$$
U = 1 - A = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 20\%
$$

**(c) Failure rate:**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{800} = 0.00125 \;\;\text{failures/hour}
$$

**(d) Reliability for 100-hour mission:**

$$
R(100) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.00125)(100)} = e^{-0.125}
$$

$$
R(100) = 0.8825 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 88.25\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Interpretation:** The pump is available 80% of the time, and given that it is operational at $t=0$, it has an **88.25% chance** of running continuously for 100 hours without failure. This is critical for occupational health planning because a 20% downtime implies the plant must keep **redundant emergency response** and **first-aid preparedness** at the ready.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Maintenance Strategy

| Strategy | Trigger Condition | Best Applied To | Cost Behavior |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Preventive (PM)** | Calendar / usage threshold | Critical safety items (fire alarms, breathing apparatus) | Predictable, moderate |
| **Corrective (CM)** | Failure event | Non-critical, low-cost spares (light bulbs) | High per event, low frequency |
| **Predictive (PdM)** | Condition indicator threshold | High-capital rotating equipment (turbines, pumps) | High upfront instrumentation, low running |
| **TPM** | Continuous, operator-driven | Assembly lines, automotive, FMCG | Cultural investment + 30–50% downtime cut |
| **Design-Out** | At design / retrofit stage | New installations | High initial, negligible operating |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 The Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Life Cycle


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Preventive Maintenance. List any four activities carried out under it.**

**Model Answer:**
Preventive Maintenance (PM) is the **scheduled, planned maintenance activity performed on equipment before failure occurs**, with the objective of reducing breakdowns and extending useful life.

Four activities:
1. **Routine inspection and lubrication** of bearings, gears, and moving parts.
2. **Periodic calibration** of measurement and control instruments.
3. **Replacement of consumables** such as filters, belts, gaskets, and seals.
4. **Cleaning and tightening** of electrical connections and fasteners.

*(Total 3 marks: 1 mark definition + 0.5 mark × 4 activities)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Breakdown Maintenance and Preventive Maintenance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Breakdown Maintenance | Preventive Maintenance |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Timing** | After failure occurs | Before failure, on schedule |
| **Cost** | High (emergency labor, spares rush) | Moderate, planned budget |
| **Downtime** | Unplanned, long | Planned, short |
| **Risk** | High (safety, production loss) | Low |
| **Workload** | Reactive, unpredictable | Proactive, balanced |
| **Example** | Replacing a snapped conveyor belt | Quarterly greasing of the same belt |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per distinguishing pair, two pairs sufficient)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** philosophy in detail. List its **eight pillars** and explain the role of **autonomous maintenance** in occupational safety. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A machine has an **MTBF of 1200 hours** and **MTTR of 100 hours**.
   Calculate:
   (i) Availability and Unavailability.
   (ii) Failure rate.
   (iii) Reliability for a 50-hour mission.
   (iv) Comment on how this machine's maintenance profile affects occupational health planning in a chemical plant. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Definition:** TPM is a **holistic maintenance philosophy** developed in Japan by Seiichi Nakajima in 1971, aimed at achieving **zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents, and zero losses** through the active participation of all employees — from top management to shop-floor operators.

**Eight Pillars:**
1. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators take ownership of cleaning, lubricating, and inspecting their own machines. Builds ownership and detects early-stage defects.
2. **Planned Maintenance:** Scheduled activities — PM, CM, PdM combined with CMMS data.
3. **Quality Maintenance:** Use of maintenance to achieve zero-defect output.
4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen):** Cross-functional teams tackle chronic losses.
5. **Early Equipment Management:** Maintenance considerations are built into new equipment design.
6. **Training:** All employees trained in maintenance skills and safety.
7. **Safety, Health, Environment:** Maintains a hazard-free workplace.
8. **Office TPM:** Applies TPM thinking to administrative and support functions.

**Role in Occupational Safety:** Autonomous maintenance empowers operators to detect abnormal vibrations, smells, leaks, and temperatures *before* they cause accidents. A well-trained operator prevents the same conditions that lead to occupational injuries — e.g., a frayed wire causing electrocution, or a seized valve causing toxic release.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Given:** MTBF = 1200 h, MTTR = 100 h

**(i) Availability:**

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{1200}{1200 + 100} = \dfrac{1200}{1300} = 0.923 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 92.3\%
$$

**Unavailability:**

$$
U = 1 - A = 1 - 0.923 = 0.077 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 7.7\%
$$

**(ii) Failure rate:**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{1200} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \;\;\text{failures/hour}
$$

**(iii) Reliability for $t = 50$ h:**

$$
R(50) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(8.33 \times 10^{-4})(50)} = e^{-0.04167}
$$

$$
R(50) = 0.9592 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 95.92\%
$$

**(iv) Comment on occupational health planning:**
With 92.3% availability, the plant can expect the machine to be down ~7.7% of operating time, which over a 8000 h/year schedule equates to ~616 hours of downtime annually. In a chemical plant, this **mandates**:
- Continuous **first-aid station readiness** with trained personnel.
- **Redundant emergency shutdown (ESD)** systems in case of failure.
- **Pre-positioned spill kits, eye-wash stations, and respirators** since toxic releases can coincide with breakdown events.
- A **permit-to-work system** ensuring maintenance is performed under controlled conditions.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $A = MTBF \times MTTR$ — **WRONG**. It is MTBF divided by the SUM of MTBF and MTTR. (–2 marks)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $1/MTBF + 1/MTTF$ as failure rate — wrong units. Failure rate is $1/MTBF$ **only**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In part (a), merely listing the 8 pillars without explaining *why* autonomous maintenance relates to occupational safety — you'll lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting units in reliability calculation.
> - **Allocation (7 marks per sub):** 3 marks formula + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark answer + 1 mark unit/comment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Discuss **Predictive Maintenance techniques** in detail. Explain **vibration analysis and thermography** with their occupational health applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A hospital ventilator has MTBF = 5000 h and MTTR = 50 h.
   Calculate:
   (i) Steady-state availability.
   (ii) Expected down-time per month (assuming 720 h of operation).
   (iii) Probability that the ventilator runs without failure for 200 continuous hours.
   (iv) Recommend a maintenance strategy to maximize patient safety. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** is a condition-driven strategy that monitors equipment health in real-time and **predicts failures before they occur**, allowing intervention only when needed.

**Techniques:**

1. **Vibration Analysis:**
   - Measures frequency, amplitude, and pattern of machine vibrations using accelerometers.
   - Detects **imbalance, misalignment, bearing wear, looseness, and gear defects**.
   - **Health application:** In a pharmaceutical plant, vibration analysis on a tablet-coating machine detects early bearing failure, preventing mechanical injury to operators and avoiding contamination events.

2. **Thermography:**
   - Uses **infrared cameras** to map surface temperature distribution.
   - Detects **overheating in electrical panels, motors, refractory linings, and bearings**.
   - **Health application:** Identifies hot spots in switchgear before insulation failure causes arc-flash burns; detects hotspots in chemical storage tanks preventing fire/explosion.

3. **Oil Analysis (Spectrometric):**
   - Measures wear-metal concentration in lubricating oil to detect internal component wear.

4. **Ultrasonic Testing:**
   - Detects high-frequency sounds from leaks, electrical discharge, and bearing defects.

5. **Acoustic Emission:**
   - Listens to crack propagation in pressure vessels — critical for boiler safety.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Given:** MTBF = 5000 h, MTTR = 50 h

**(i) Availability:**

$$
A = \dfrac{5000}{5000 + 50} = \dfrac{5000}{5050} = 0.9901 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 99.01\%
$$

**(ii) Monthly downtime (out of 720 h):**

$$
\text{Downtime} = U \times 720 = (1 - 0.9901) \times 720 = 0.0099 \times 720 = 7.13 \;\;\text{hours/month}
$$

**(iii) Reliability for $t = 200$ h:**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{5000} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \;\;\text{failures/hour}
$$

$$
R(200) = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(200)} = e^{-0.04} = 0.9608 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 96.08\%
$$

**(iv) Recommended Strategy for Patient Safety:**

Given the life-critical role of ventilators:
- Use **Predictive Maintenance (PdM) with continuous monitoring** — pressure sensors, flow sensors, alarm logs.
- Maintain **redundant backup ventilators** at all times.
- Apply **Preventive Maintenance** at strict intervals (e.g., every 1000 hours) for filters, valves, and O₂ sensors.
- Train biomedical engineers and ICU staff in **first response to ventilator failure**.
- Maintain a **CMMS log** (Computerized Maintenance Management System) for full traceability — required for **NABH and JCI accreditation**.
- Conduct **failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA)** quarterly to anticipate risks.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> - Forgetting to convert percentage to fraction before multiplying monthly hours — common error costing 1 mark.
> - In reliability problems, do **not** use $A$ instead of $R(t)$ — they are fundamentally different (steady-state vs. instantaneous probability).
> - "Predictive Maintenance" must be explained with **at least one specific technique** to score full marks in part (a).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Maintenance = retain OR restore** equipment to functional state (BS 3811).
- [ ] **Two super-categories:** Preventive (before failure) vs. Corrective (after failure).
- [ ] **TPM** = Japanese philosophy, **8 pillars**, operator-driven.
- [ ] **PdM** techniques to memorize: **Vibration, Thermography, Oil Analysis, Ultrasonic, Acoustic Emission**.
- [ ] **Golden formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — must know by heart.
- [ ] **Reliability function:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — derived from constant failure-rate assumption.
- [ ] **Failure rate:** $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ (per hour) — only under exponential distribution.
- [ ] **MTTF = MTBF = 1/λ** for exponential distribution, repairable vs. non-repairable context matters.
- [ ] **Bathtub curve** has 3 stages: Infant Mortality, Useful Life (constant λ), Wear-Out.
- [ ] **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** — world-class benchmark > 85%.
- [ ] Maintenance is a **legal obligation** under Indian Factories Act 1948 (Sec. 21, Rule 63).
- [ ] **First-aid box maintenance** is itself a maintenance activity under occupational health.
- [ ] **TPM pillar "Safety, Health, Environment"** directly links maintenance engineering to occupational wellness.
- [ ] Always include **units** in your final answer (hours, %, failures/hour).
- [ ] In KTU problems, **comment on occupational health implications** — Module 2 specifically rewards this.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Final KTU Topper Tip**
> The course code PCSFT503 is **occupational health-focused**, not pure mechanical engineering. So in every maintenance question, **always tie the answer back to worker safety, first aid, health surveillance, or legal compliance**. Examiners reward answers that demonstrate the **interdisciplinary connection** between maintenance engineering and occupational health practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lighting and Colour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lighting and Colour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lighting — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lighting (Industrial/Occupational Lighting)** is the controlled application of visible radiant energy (electromagnetic radiation in the wavelength range of **380 nm to 780 nm**) to the workplace environment in order to provide adequate visibility for the safe, efficient, and accurate performance of visual tasks, while simultaneously ensuring visual comfort, safety, and the psychological well-being of workers.

In the context of Occupational Health and Industrial Hygiene, lighting is classified as a **physical hazard** when inadequate, excessive, or improperly distributed, because it directly affects:
- Visual fatigue (eye strain)
- Postural strain (adopting awkward positions to see better)
- Accident proneness
- Productivity and quality of work
- General morale and health

> [!NOTE]
> **Photometry** is the branch of science that deals with the measurement of light (visible radiant energy) in terms of its perceived brightness to the human eye. The fundamental photometric quantities are luminous flux, luminous intensity, illumination (illuminance), and luminance.

---

## 1.2 Fundamental Photometric Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Definition |
|----------|--------|---------|------------|
| Luminous Flux | $\phi$ or $F$ | **Lumen (lm)** | Total quantity of visible light emitted by a source per second in all directions |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | **Candela (cd)** | Luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction |
| Illumination (Illuminance) | $E$ | **Lux (lx) = lm/m²** | Luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface |
| Luminance (Brightness) | $L$ | **cd/m² (Nit)** | Luminous intensity per unit projected area of a light-emitting or reflecting surface |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | **lm/W** | Ratio of luminous flux emitted to the electrical power consumed |
| Contrast | $C$ | Dimensionless (Ratio) | Difference in luminance or reflectance between an object and its background |

---

## 1.3 Colour — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Colour** is the perceptual response of the human visual system to the spectral composition of visible light. A given colour sensation is produced by the wavelength (or mixture of wavelengths) of light reaching the retina, and is interpreted by the brain in terms of three psychological attributes: **Hue, Saturation, and Brightness (Value)**.

In occupational hygiene, colour is studied because it influences:
- **Safety signalling** (red for danger, green for safety, yellow for caution)
- **Visual discrimination** of parts, tools, gauges
- **Worker comfort and psychological state**
- **Aesthetic quality** of the work environment

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "Lighting and Colour Made Simple"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 (Lighting as "Water from a Hose"):** Imagine a garden hose spraying water in a circular pattern. The **total water sprayed per second** is the *luminous flux* (lumen). The **water sprayed in one specific direction per second** is *luminous intensity* (candela). The **water hitting a square-metre patch of ground** is *illumination* (lux). Now move closer to the hose → the ground gets wetter (more illumination). Move the patch sideways → it gets drier (less water at an angle). This is exactly the **Inverse Square Law** and **Cosine Law** of illumination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 (Colour as a "Recipe"):** Sunlight is like plain white flour — neutral, with no "flavour." When sunlight passes through a stained-glass window, the glass *absorbs* some ingredients (wavelengths) and *transmits* the rest. A red glass absorbs everything except red wavelengths. So the **colour of an object is actually the colour of the light it reflects or transmits** — not a property the object "possesses." This is why a "black" dress looks dark in a yellow-lit room: the cloth absorbs almost all yellow light and reflects very little back to your eye.

---

## 1.5 The Electromagnetic Spectrum — Visual Window

The human eye is sensitive only to a tiny sliver of the electromagnetic spectrum:

$$\text{Wavelength Range: } \lambda \approx 380 \text{ nm (violet)} \longrightarrow 780 \text{ nm (red)}$$

| Colour | Approximate Wavelength (nm) |
|--------|-----------------------------|
| Violet | 380 – 450 |
| Blue | 450 – 495 |
| Green | 495 – 570 |
| Yellow | 570 – 590 |
| Orange | 590 – 620 |
| Red | 620 – 780 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Luminous Efficacy of Monochromatic Radiation** peaks at **555 nm (yellow-green)** because the human eye (photopic vision) is most sensitive at this wavelength. One watt of radiant power at 555 nm = **683 lumens**. This is the very definition of the candela, the SI base unit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Laws of Illumination

The quantitative study of illumination rests on two cornerstone laws. Together they govern every lighting design calculation in industry.

### 2.1.1 Inverse Square Law (Rousseau's Law)

**Statement:** *The illumination on a surface held normal to the rays of light is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source.*

**Step-by-step logical structure:**

1. Consider a point light source $S$ emitting luminous flux uniformly in all directions.
2. The solid angle subtended at $S$ by a sphere of radius $d$ is $\Omega = 4\pi$ steradians.
3. Luminous intensity of the source: $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\Omega} = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi}$.
4. A smaller concentric sphere of radius $d$ encloses the same total flux $\Phi$, but this flux is now spread over an area $A = 4\pi d^2$.
5. Illumination on the inner sphere: $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi d^2} = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inverse Square Law is valid ONLY when:** (a) the light source is a *point source* (negligible size compared with distance $d$), and (b) the illuminated surface is *perpendicular* to the direction of light propagation.

### 2.1.2 Cosine Law (Lambert's Law / Law of Incidence)

**Statement:** *The illumination on a surface is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence (the angle between the direction of light and the normal to the surface).*

**Step-by-step logical structure:**

1. When light strikes a tilted surface at an angle $\theta$ (from the normal), the same flux $\Phi$ is now distributed over a *larger* effective area.
2. The projected area = actual area × $\cos\theta$, so illumination decreases by a factor of $\cos\theta$.
3. This explains why a table tilted away from a lamp receives progressively less light.

### 2.1.3 The Combined Law (Most Used in Practice)

For a point source illuminating a tilted surface:

$$\boxed{\;E = \dfrac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}\;}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Illumination in **lux**
- $I$ = Luminous intensity of the source in **candela** (towards the surface)
- $\theta$ = Angle of incidence (between light ray and surface normal)
- $d$ = Distance from source to point in **metres**

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in every KTU End Semester Exam question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$ | Inverse square law (surface normal to ray) | $E$ in lx, $I$ in cd, $d$ in m |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | Combined law (surface tilted at angle $\theta$) | lx, cd, m |
| 3 | $\Omega = \dfrac{A}{r^2}$ | Solid angle subtended by area $A$ at distance $r$ | steradian (sr) |
| 4 | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\Omega}$ | Luminous intensity from flux and solid angle | cd, lm, sr |
| 5 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Illumination from flux and area | lx, lm, m² |
| 6 | $\text{Lumen} = 4\pi \cdot I$ | Flux from a uniform point source | lm, cd |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | Luminous efficacy of a lamp | lm/W |
| 8 | $\text{MSC} = \dfrac{\text{Number of lamps} \times \text{lumens per lamp} \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}{\text{Area}}$ | Light planning equation | lx |
| 9 | $C = \dfrac{L_b - L_o}{L_b}$ or $\dfrac{L_o}{L_b}$ | Contrast (object vs background luminance) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V = 1.018 - \text{MTF}$ | Visual performance (using modulation transfer function) | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- **UF** = Utilization Factor (fraction of lamp lumens reaching the working plane)
- **MF** = Maintenance Factor (allows for lamp depreciation and dirt accumulation; typically 0.7–0.8)
- **MSC** = Mean Spherical Candlepower (sometimes denoted MSCP)

---

## 2.3 Recommended Illumination Levels (BIS / IS 3646 / IES Standards)

| Task / Location | Recommended Illumination (lux) |
|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| Casual observation, corridors, lobbies | 50 – 100 |
| General office work, conference rooms | 300 – 500 |
| Detailed office work, accounting | 500 – 750 |
| Drawing offices, computer rooms | 750 – 1000 |
| Precision bench work, watchmaking | 1000 – 2000 |
| Fine inspection, electronics assembly | 2000 – 5000 |
| Operating theatres, surgery | 5000 – 10000 |
| Road lighting (urban) | 15 – 30 |
| Road lighting (highways) | 8 – 15 |

---

## 2.4 Classification of Lighting Systems

### 2.4.1 By Source Origin
1. **Natural Lighting (Daylight):** Free, varied, but uncontrolled; provided by windows, skylights, monitors.
2. **Artificial Lighting:** Controlled, predictable, but costly; from electric lamps.
3. **Mixed Lighting:** Combination of the above (most common in modern industry).

### 2.4.2 By Direction of Light Distribution (CIE Classification)

| System | % Flux Downward | % Flux Upward | Typical Use |
|--------|-----------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Direct** | 90–100 | 0–10 | Offices, factories, classrooms (high efficiency, harsh shadows) |
| **Semi-direct** | 60–90 | 10–40 | General industrial lighting |
| **General Diffused** | 40–60 | 40–60 | Drawing offices, clean rooms |
| **Semi-indirect** | 10–40 | 60–90 | Decorative, soft lighting |
| **Indirect** | 0–10 | 90–100 | Conference halls, auditoriums (no shadows, low efficiency) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in occupational health?** Direct lighting is efficient but causes **glare** and **hard shadows**, leading to eye strain. Indirect lighting eliminates shadows but wastes energy. Most modern industrial environments use **direct or semi-direct** systems with diffusers to balance efficiency and comfort.

---

## 2.5 Glare — The Visual Enemy of Workers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glare** is the sensation produced by luminances within the visual field that are sufficiently greater than the luminance to which the eyes are adapted, causing annoyance, discomfort, or a measurable loss in visual performance.

**Two Categories:**
1. **Disability Glare** — actually reduces the ability to see (e.g., oncoming headlights at night).
2. **Discomfort Glare** — causes annoyance/fatigue without measurable loss of acuity (e.g., bare fluorescent tube in peripheral view).

**Cure:** Diffusers, louvres, indirect lighting, anti-glare screens, correct mounting height.

---

## 2.6 Colour Concepts Critical to Industry

### 2.6.1 The Three Attributes of Colour (Munsell System)
- **Hue** — the "name" of the colour (red, blue, green)
- **Value (Lightness)** — how light or dark the colour is
- **Chroma (Saturation)** — the purity or intensity of the hue

### 2.6.2 Colour Temperature ($T_c$)
The temperature (in **Kelvin**) of a *black body radiator* whose emitted light has the same chromaticity as the light source.

| Source | Colour Temperature (K) | Appearance |
|--------|------------------------|------------|
| Candle flame | ~1900 | Warm orange-red |
| Tungsten incandescent | 2700 – 3000 | Warm white |
| Halogen | 3000 – 3500 | Warm white |
| Fluorescent (warm white) | 3000 | Warm |
| Fluorescent (cool white/daylight) | 4000 – 6500 | Cool white |
| Daylight (overcast sky) | ~6500 | Cool blue-white |
| Blue sky | 10000+ | Bluish |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lower colour temperature = warmer (yellower) light; higher = cooler (bluer) light.** Offices generally use 4000 K; factories 4000–5000 K; jewellery display 3000 K.

### 2.6.3 Colour Rendering Index (CRI / $R_a$)
A scale from **0 to 100** measuring how accurately a light source reveals the true colours of objects compared with a reference source (sunlight or incandescent, $R_a = 100$).

| CRI Value | Quality | Typical Use |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 90+ | Excellent | Hospitals, art galleries, textile inspection |
| 80 – 90 | Good | Offices, schools, residences |
| 60 – 80 | Fair | Street lighting, warehouses |
| < 60 | Poor | Rarely used indoors |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial settings, lighting design influences:
- **Accident prevention** (better visibility of hazards, ditches, moving machinery)
- **Quality control** (proper colour discrimination for sorting, inspection)
- **Productivity** (well-lit workers are 10–30% more efficient)
- **Eye health** (prevention of occupational myopia, conjunctivitis, headaches)
- **Energy efficiency** (lumen/watt optimization reduces electricity costs and carbon footprint)
- **Safety colour coding** (red stop buttons, green exits, yellow caution stripes)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Inverse Square Law

**Given:** A point light source $S$ of uniform luminous intensity $I$ is located at the centre of three concentric spheres of radii $d_1$, $d_2$, and $d_3$ metres.

**To Prove:** Illumination at a point on the sphere surface is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source.

---

**Step 1 — Solid angle concept:**
The solid angle subtended at a point by a closed surface is the area on a unit sphere enclosing the point. By definition:

$$\Omega = \dfrac{A}{r^2}$$

where $A$ is the area intercepted on a sphere of radius $r$. The unit is the steradian (sr).

**Step 2 — Total solid angle around a point:**
The total area of a sphere of radius $r$ is $4\pi r^2$. Therefore, the total solid angle surrounding a point is:

$$\Omega_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{4\pi r^2}{r^2} = 4\pi \text{ steradians}$$

**Step 3 — Relating flux and intensity:**
Luminous intensity is defined as luminous flux per unit solid angle in a given direction:

$$I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\Omega}$$

Therefore, the total flux emitted by the point source uniformly in all directions is:

$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = I \times \Omega_{\text{total}} = I \times 4\pi \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 4 — Flux through a small patch:**
Consider a small patch of area $\delta A$ on the surface of a sphere of radius $d$. The solid angle subtended by this patch at the source is:

$$\delta\Omega = \dfrac{\delta A}{d^2}$$

The flux passing through this patch:

$$\delta\Phi = I \times \delta\Omega = I \times \dfrac{\delta A}{d^2}$$

**Step 5 — Definition of illumination:**
Illumination $E$ is the flux incident per unit area. Therefore:

$$E = \dfrac{\delta\Phi}{\delta A} = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$$

**Step 6 — Invert the relationship:**
If $E_1$ and $E_2$ are illuminations at distances $d_1$ and $d_2$:

$$E_1 = \dfrac{I}{d_1^2} \quad \text{and} \quad E_2 = \dfrac{I}{d_2^2}$$

Dividing:

$$\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \dfrac{d_2^2}{d_1^2}$$

$$\boxed{\;E \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}\;}$$

**Hence proved.** Illumination is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Cosine Law

**Given:** A point source $S$ illuminating a flat surface $P$ at an angle of incidence $\theta$ (angle between the incident ray and the normal to the surface).

---

**Step 1 — Projected area geometry:**
Let the actual area of the surface patch be $A$. The patch, when projected onto a plane *perpendicular* to the ray, has area:

$$A_p = A \cdot \cos\theta$$

This is because the projection of a tilted plane is foreshortened by the factor $\cos\theta$.

**Step 2 — Illumination on tilted surface:**
By the inverse square law, the illumination that *would* exist on the perpendicular plane at the same perpendicular distance is:

$$E_{\perp} = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Same flux, larger area:**
The luminous flux $\delta\Phi$ passing through both the perpendicular area $A_p$ and the tilted area $A$ is the same (flux is conserved). But since the tilted area $A$ is larger by the factor $\dfrac{1}{\cos\theta}$, the illumination (flux per unit area) on the tilted surface is smaller:

$$E = \dfrac{\delta\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{\delta\Phi}{A_p / \cos\theta} = \dfrac{\delta\Phi \cdot \cos\theta}{A_p} = E_{\perp} \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Final substitution:**

$$\boxed{\;E = \dfrac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}\;}$$

**Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric interpretation:** A surface tilted away from a light source "catches" fewer lumens per square metre because the same beam of light is spread over a longer, stretched-out patch.

---

## 3.3 Solved Analytical Examples (KTU Pattern)

### Example 1 — Inverse Square Law Application
**Problem:** A lamp emits a uniform luminous intensity of 200 cd in all directions. Calculate the illumination on a surface placed **(a)** 2 m away, and **(b)** 4 m away. Comment on the relationship.

**Solution:**

Given: $I = 200$ cd, $d_1 = 2$ m, $d_2 = 4$ m.

By Inverse Square Law: $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$

**(a)** At $d_1 = 2$ m:
$$E_1 = \dfrac{200}{(2)^2} = \dfrac{200}{4} = 50 \text{ lux}$$

**(b)** At $d_2 = 4$ m:
$$E_2 = \dfrac{200}{(4)^2} = \dfrac{200}{16} = 12.5 \text{ lux}$$

**Comment (Verification of law):**
$$\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \dfrac{50}{12.5} = 4 = \left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4}{2}\right)^2 = 4 \;\; \checkmark$$

Illumination drops by a factor of 4 when distance doubles — confirming the inverse square relationship.

---

### Example 2 — Cosine Law Application
**Problem:** A street lamp with a luminous intensity of 500 cd in the downward direction is mounted at a height of 6 m. A point $P$ on the road is at a horizontal distance of 8 m from the base of the pole. Find the illumination at $P$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry:**
The actual slant distance from the lamp to the point $P$ is:
$$d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2} = \sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Angle of incidence:**
The angle $\theta$ between the downward vertical (normal to the horizontal road) and the light ray $SP$ is:
$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{h}{d} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.6$$

**Step 3 — Apply combined law:**
$$E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2} = \dfrac{500 \times 0.6}{(10)^2} = \dfrac{300}{100} = 3 \text{ lux}$$

**Answer:** $E = 3$ lux at point $P$. (This is in the typical range for highway illumination.)

---

### Example 3 — Lumen Method (Lumen Planning Equation)
**Problem:** A factory hall of 30 m × 20 m is to be illuminated to an average level of 200 lux. Using 4 fluorescent lamps per fitting, each rated at 2500 lumens, with a utilization factor of 0.5 and a maintenance factor of 0.8, find the number of fittings required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Area:**
$$A = 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total lumens required:**
By $E = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{total}}}{A}$, the total flux needed is:
$$\Phi_{\text{required}} = E \times A = 200 \times 600 = 120{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 3 — Lumen output per fitting:**
$$\Phi_{\text{fitting}} = 4 \times 2500 = 10{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 4 — Effective lumens reaching working plane per fitting:**
$$\Phi_{\text{effective}} = \Phi_{\text{fitting}} \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF} = 10{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.8 = 4000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 5 — Number of fittings:**
$$N = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{required}}}{\Phi_{\text{effective}}} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{4000} = 30 \text{ fittings}$$

**Answer:** **30 fittings** are required to achieve the desired illumination.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Conversion Factors (Required by KTU)

| Conversion | Formula |
|------------|---------|
| Foot-candle to Lux | $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lux}$ |
| Lux to Foot-candle | $1 \text{ lx} = 0.0929 \text{ fc}$ |
| Candlepower (Hefner) to Candela | $1 \text{ HK} \approx 0.903 \text{ cd}$ |
| Lumen to Watt (at 555 nm) | $1 \text{ W} = 683 \text{ lm}$ |
| Lambert to cd/m² | $1 \text{ L} = \dfrac{10^4}{\pi} \text{ cd/m}^2 \approx 3183 \text{ cd/m}^2$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Photometric Quantities and Their Interrelationships

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[LIGHT SOURCE] --> B[Luminous Flux phi - lumens]
    B --> C[Distributed over solid angle]
    C --> D[Luminous Intensity I - candela]
    D --> E[Strikes surface at distance d]
    E --> F[Illumination E - lux]
    F --> G[Reflected from surface]
    G --> H[Luminance L - cd per m squared]
    H --> I[Perceived by human eye]
    I --> J[Visual Sensation: Brightness, Hue, Saturation]
    A --> K[Power input P - watts]
    K --> L[Efficacy eta = phi over P]
    L --> M[Energy efficiency rating]
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Laws of Illumination Geometry

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Case1[Case A: Surface Normal to Light Ray]
        S1[Point Source S] -->|distance d| P1[Surface Point P]
        P1 --> N1[Normal coincides with ray]
        N1 --> F1[E equals I over d squared]
    end
    subgraph Case2[Case B: Surface Tilted at Angle theta]
        S2[Point Source S] -->|distance d| P2[Surface Point P tilted]
        P2 --> N2[Normal makes angle theta with ray]
        N2 --> F2[E equals I cos theta over d squared]
    end
    subgraph Case3[Case C: Real Lamp Finite Size]
        S3[Lamp with luminous flux phi] -->|multiple rays| P3[Surface]
        P3 --> N3[Apply Rousseau and Lambert]
        N3 --> F3[Use lumen method with UF and MF]
    end
    style Case1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Case2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Case3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

```mermaid
graph TD

    B --> C[Step 3: Select Lamp Type and CRI]
    C --> D[Step 4: Choose Lighting System Type]

    E --> F[Step 6: Calculate Number of Fittings]
    F --> G[Step 7: Check Glare Index]
    G --> H[Step 8: Verify Uniformity Ratio]
    H --> I[Step 9: Compute Energy Consumption]
    I --> J[Step 10: Final Layout and Installation]

    K -->|Fail| C
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define Luminous Flux, Luminous Intensity, and Illumination. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** The total quantity of visible light (energy per unit time) emitted by a source in all directions, weighted by the eye's sensitivity. **SI unit: lumen (lm).**
> - **Luminous Intensity ($I$):** The luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction. **SI unit: candela (cd).**
> - **Illumination ($E$):** The luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface. **SI unit: lux (lx) = lm/m².**

Mathematically:
$$\Phi = I \cdot \Omega \quad ; \quad I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\Omega} \quad ; \quad E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: State and explain the Inverse Square Law of Illumination. Mention its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
