```mermaid
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC -->|Yes| stepF
    stepC -->|No Breathing Check| stepG
    stepC -->|No Breathing Pulse Absent| stepH
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepI
    stepG --> stepI
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL
```

## 4.3 FAST Mnemonic — Field Identification Schema

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF["F - FACE - Smile test - Look for droop on one side"]
    nodeA["A - ARMS - Raise both arms - Look for arm drift downward"]
    nodeS["S - SPEECH - Repeat a sentence - Look for slurring or word finding difficulty"]
    nodeT["T - TIME - If any sign positive - Call EMS immediately - Note time of onset"]
    
    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeT
```

## 4.4 Risk Factor Stratification — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["STROKE RISK FACTORS"]
    nodeY["NON-MODIFIABLE - Cannot Change"]
    nodeZ["MODIFIABLE - Can Control"]
    nodeY1["Age above 55"]
    nodeY2["Male Sex"]
    nodeY3["Family History Genetics"]
    nodeY4["Prior Stroke or TIA"]
    nodeZ1["Hypertension - Most Important"]
    nodeZ2["Diabetes Mellitus"]
    nodeZ3["Atrial Fibrillation"]
    nodeZ4["Smoking Alcohol Obesity"]
    nodeZ5["Occupational Stress"]
    
    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeX --> nodeZ
    nodeY --> nodeY1
    nodeY --> nodeY2
    nodeY --> nodeY3
    nodeY --> nodeY4
    nodeZ --> nodeZ1
    nodeZ --> nodeZ2
    nodeZ --> nodeZ3
    nodeZ --> nodeZ4
    nodeZ --> nodeZ5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Stroke. Differentiate between Ischemic and Hemorrhagic stroke.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) is the sudden onset of focal neurological deficit lasting more than 24 hours, caused by disruption of cerebral blood supply due to vascular occlusion or rupture.

**Differentiation Table:**

| Feature | Ischemic Stroke | Hemorrhagic Stroke |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Vessel occlusion (thrombus/embolus) | Vessel rupture |
| Incidence | ~87% | ~13% |
| Headache | Mild/absent | Severe ("thunderclap") |
| Consciousness | Usually preserved | Often impaired |
| Vomiting | Uncommon | Common |
| Treatment | tPA, thrombectomy | Surgical evacuation, BP control |

---

### Question 2: What is the FAST mnemonic? Explain its significance in first aid.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**FAST** is a rapid field-screening tool for early stroke identification:

- **F** — **Face:** Ask to smile; observe for facial droop.
- **A** — **Arms:** Ask to raise both arms; observe for one-sided drift/weakness.
- **S** — **Speech:** Ask to repeat a sentence; observe for slurring or word-finding difficulty.
- **T** — **Time:** If any sign is positive, **call emergency services immediately** and note the time of symptom onset.

**Significance:**
1. Enables rapid identification by lay first-aiders.
2. Ensures timely activation of EMS within the **4.5-hour tPA window**.
3. Reduces treatment delay — every minute saves ~1.9 million neurons.
4. Standardized tool used globally by WHO, American Heart Association, and Indian Stroke Association.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Describe in detail the types, risk factors, and clinical features of stroke. Add a note on the FAST assessment.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

#### (a) Types and Risk Factors of Stroke [7 Marks]

**Types of Stroke:**

**1. Ischemic Stroke (87% of cases):**
- **Thrombotic Stroke:** Clot formation within a cerebral artery, usually due to atherosclerosis of large vessels (e.g., middle cerebral artery). Symptoms often develop gradually, frequently during sleep.
- **Embolic Stroke:** Clot originates from elsewhere (commonly the heart in atrial fibrillation) and travels to the brain. Sudden, maximal deficit at onset.
- **Lacunar Stroke:** Occlusion of small penetrating arteries deep in the brain (basal ganglia, thalamus). Strongly linked to chronic hypertension and diabetes.

**2. Hemorrhagic Stroke (13% of cases):**
- **Intracerebral Hemorrhage (ICH):** Bleeding directly into brain parenchyma, most commonly from rupture of small penetrating arteries (e.g., lenticulostriate) due to chronic hypertension. Affects basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, cerebellum.
- **Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH):** Bleeding into the subarachnoid space, most commonly from rupture of a **berry (saccular) aneurysm** in the Circle of Willis. Classic presentation: sudden "worst headache of life."

**3. Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA):**
- Temporary neurological dysfunction resolving within 24 hours (usually <1 hour). A critical warning sign — ~30% of TIA patients develop stroke within 1 year if untreated.

**Risk Factors:**

**Non-Modifiable:**
- Age (>55 years), Male sex, Family history, Race (higher in South Asians, Africans), Prior stroke/TIA.

**Modifiable (Occupational Relevance):**
- **Hypertension** — single most important risk factor for both ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke.
- Diabetes Mellitus, Atrial Fibrillation, Hyperlipidemia.
- Smoking, Alcohol abuse, Obesity, Sedentary lifestyle.
- **Occupational stressors:** Shift work, high-pressure jobs, exposure to CO and solvents.

#### (b) Clinical Features and FAST Assessment [7 Marks]

**Clinical Features:**

- **Sudden weakness/numbness** of face, arm, or leg (usually unilateral).
- **Sudden confusion**, difficulty speaking or understanding speech (aphasia).
- **Sudden visual disturbance** in one or both eyes.
- **Sudden difficulty walking**, dizziness, loss of balance/coordination.
- **Sudden severe headache** with no known cause (hallmark of hemorrhagic stroke).
- **Vomiting, seizures**, altered consciousness (more common in hemorrhagic).

**FAST Assessment:**

| Component | Test | Positive Finding |
|---|---|---|
| **F**ace | Ask to smile/show teeth | Asymmetric smile, drooping of one side |
| **A**rms | Ask to hold both arms extended for 10 sec | One arm drifts downward |
| **S**peech | Ask to repeat "The sky is blue" | Slurred, garbled, or unable to speak |
| **T**ime | If any positive | Call EMS, note exact onset time |

**Extended BE-FAST** adds Balance (B) and Eyes (E) — sudden loss of balance or vision.

**Significance of FAST:**
- Validated screening tool with **89% sensitivity and 95% specificity** for stroke.
- Reduces pre-hospital delay.
- Enables tPA eligibility determination by EMS.

---

### Question B: Explain the step-by-step first aid management of a suspected stroke patient at an industrial workplace. What are the common stroke mimics that should be ruled out?

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

#### (a) Step-by-Step First Aid Management [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Scene Safety:** Ensure the area is safe. Don PPE (gloves, mask).

**Step 2 — Primary Assessment (DR-ABC):**
- **D**anger, **R**esponse (shake & shout), **A**irway, **B**reathing, **C**irculation.

**Step 3 — Activate EMS Immediately:** Call for ambulance; clearly state "suspected stroke." **Note exact time of symptom onset** (critical for tPA window).

**Step 4 — FAST Assessment:** Face, Arms, Speech, Time. Document findings.

**Step 5 — Position the Patient:**
- *Conscious:* Recovery position, head and shoulders elevated ~30° (reduces intracranial pressure).
- *Unconscious but breathing:* Lateral recovery position (unaffected side down).
- *Not breathing:* Begin CPR immediately.

**Step 6 — Nothing by Mouth:** Stroke patients often have impaired swallowing (dysphagia) — risk of aspiration.

**Step 7 — Loosen Tight Clothing:** Collar, tie, belt for easier breathing.

**Step 8 — Reassure the Patient:** Calm verbal reassurance; anxiety increases cerebral oxygen demand.

**Step 9 — Monitor Vitals Continuously:** AVPU scale; watch for vomiting (keep suction ready); monitor pulse, breathing rate.

**Step 10 — Document and Handover:** Time of onset, FAST findings, vitals, medical history. Hand over to EMS with complete details.

#### (b) Common Stroke Mimics [7 Marks]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **lose 2-3 marks** by listing stroke mimics without explaining **how to differentiate them**. Always provide the distinguishing clinical feature.
> - Another common error is **forgetting to check blood glucose** in suspected stroke — hypoglycemia is the **#1 stroke mimic** and is fully reversible with glucose.

**Major Stroke Mimics:**

| Condition | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|
| **Hypoglycemia** | Diaphoresis, palpitations, low BGL; **rapidly reverses with glucose** |
| **Post-ictal State (Seizure)** | History of epilepsy, tongue biting, urinary incontinence, gradual recovery |
| **Bell's Palsy** | Isolated facial weakness, **forehead sparing**, no limb involvement |
| **Migraine with Aura** | History of recurrent migraines, visual scintillations preceding headache |
| **Brain Tumor** | **Gradual** onset over weeks-months, progressive symptoms |
| **Multiple Sclerosis** | Younger patient, relapsing-remitting pattern, optic neuritis |
| **Toxic-Metabolic Encephalopathy** | Hepatic/renal failure, drug intoxication, asterixis |
| **Syncope (Vasovagal)** | Brief LOC, bradycardia, rapid full recovery |
| **Subdural Hematoma** | History of head trauma, fluctuating consciousness, elderly/alcoholic |

**Critical Practical Point:** Always **check blood glucose** first — it is the cheapest, fastest, and most important differentiator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Stroke":**

### Core Definitions
- Stroke = Sudden focal neurological deficit > 24 hrs from vascular cause
- TIA = Same symptoms but resolves within 24 hrs (warning sign!)
- "Brain Attack" — equivalent urgency to cardiac arrest

### Classification (MUST memorize)
- **Ischemic (87%)** → Thrombotic, Embolic, Lacunar
- **Hemorrhagic (13%)** → ICH (HTN), SAH (berry aneurysm)
- **TIA** → Reversible, but major warning

### The FAST Mnemonic
- **F**ace, **A**rms, **S**peech, **T**ime
- Extended version: **BE-FAST** (adds Balance, Eyes)
- Positive FAST → Immediate EMS activation

### Key Numbers to Remember
- **4.5 hours** = tPA window
- **24 hours** = Stroke vs TIA cutoff
- **1.9 million neurons/minute** = death rate
- **87% ischemic, 13% hemorrhagic**
- **HTN** = single most important modifiable risk factor

### First Aid Priorities (in order)
1. Scene safety → DR-ABC
2. Call EMS + **note time of onset**
3. FAST assessment
4. Position: head elevated 30° (conscious) / lateral recovery (unconscious)
5. **Nothing by mouth**
6. Loosen clothing, reassure, monitor vitals

### Common Pitfalls in Exams
- ❌ Forgetting to mention **time of onset** (critical for tPA)
- ❌ Saying "give water to the patient" (NEVER — aspiration risk)
- ❌ Listing mimics without differentiating features
- ❌ Missing the **glucose check** for hypoglycemia mimic
- ❌ Confusing ICH and SAH causes (HTN vs Berry aneurysm)

### Occupational Health Linkage
- Workplace stress → modifiable risk factor
- Industries must train first-aiders in FAST assessment
- Occupational health surveillance for HTN, DM, AF in workers

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Head Injuries - Fractures of the Base, Vault and Sides of Skull
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Head Injuries - Fractures of the Base, Vault and Sides of Skull

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Head Injury (KTU 2024 Definition):** Any trauma to the scalp, skull (cranium), or brain, sustained as a result of mechanical, chemical, or thermal insult, leading to structural disruption of bony or soft tissue components of the cranial vault and its contents.

A **skull fracture** is defined as a break or discontinuity in one or more of the cranial bones, classified anatomically into:

1. **Fracture of the Vault** — involving the cranial roof (frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital bones forming the superior aspect).
2. **Fracture of the Base** — involving the floor of the cranial cavity (anterior, middle, and posterior cranial fossae).
3. **Fracture of the Sides (Lateral Skull)** — involving the temporal, sphenoid, and zygomatic regions, often associated with middle ear and facial structure damage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Skull Anatomy Refresher:** The adult human skull consists of **22 bones** — 8 cranial bones (forming the braincase) and 14 facial bones. Of these, the **frontal, parietal (2), temporal (2), occipital, sphenoid, and ethmoid** form the cranial vault and base. The cranial bones are united by immovable joints called **sutures**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Eggshell Analogy:** Imagine the human skull as a rigid, **egg-shaped protective helmet (eggshell)** that encases the delicate brain (the yolk). Just like an eggshell can crack at the top (vault), the bottom (base), or the sides — so can our skull. A crack on the top of the egg (vault fracture) is easy to see, but a crack on the bottom (base fracture) is hidden and only reveals itself through the contents leaking out. This is exactly why **base-of-skull fractures are more dangerous** — they are **hidden injuries** with delayed, often dramatic signs.

**Real-world parallel:** Think of a motorcycle helmet. A direct impact may dent the crown (vault), but a glancing blow may fracture the side near the ear area (lateral fracture). The weakest internal structures (base) fracture last, but when they do, they compromise the brain's foundation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Severity Classification

The **Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)** is the gold standard for assessing head injury severity:

| GCS Score Range | Classification | Clinical Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **15** | Normal | Fully conscious, oriented |
| **13 – 14** | Mild Head Injury | Minor confusion, no loss of consciousness or brief LOC < 30 min |
| **9 – 12** | Moderate Head Injury | Lethargic, requires hospitalization |
| **3 – 8** | Severe Head Injury | Comatose, immediate ICU intervention, intubation needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constant:** Any patient with GCS **≤ 8** after head trauma requires **immediate endotracheal intubation and neurosurgical referral** (the "**8 = Intubate**" rule universally followed in KTU-affiliated hospitals and EMS protocols).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Head Injuries — Overview

Head injuries are broadly divided into:

- **Closed Head Injury** — Skull intact, brain injured internally (e.g., concussion, contusion, hematoma).
- **Open (Penetrating) Head Injury** — Skull fractured with communication between the cranial cavity and the exterior (CSF leak, brain exposure).
- **Scalp Injuries** — Lacerations, abrasions, contusions of the soft tissue covering.

> [!NOTE]
> Skull fractures themselves are classified as either **simple (closed)** or **compound (open)**. A **compound fracture** of the vault carries a far higher risk of **meningitis** because the dura mater may be torn, allowing direct bacterial entry into the subarachnoid space.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Mechanics of Skull Fractures

The skull behaves according to **biomechanical principles** similar to an elastic sphere. When a force is applied:

1. The point of impact undergoes **inward bending (compression)**.
2. The opposite pole undergoes **outward bending (tension)**.
3. The weakest areas fail first under tension.

> **Mercer's Point (Biomechanical Rule):** A skull fracture almost always occurs at the site of impact OR at a point **diagonally opposite** to the impact site. The latter is called a **contrecoup fracture** and is a high-yield KTU concept.

### 2.1.1 Energy Transfer Equation (Conceptual)

The kinetic energy transferred to the skull follows:

$$E_{impact} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of the impacting object (kg)
- $v$ = velocity of impact (m/s)
- $E_{impact}$ = kinetic energy (Joules)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity-squared relationship:** Doubling the speed of impact **quadruples** the energy transmitted. This is why **high-velocity injuries** (road traffic accidents, falls from height) produce far more devastating skull fractures than low-velocity injuries (a slow-falling brick).

---

## 2.2 Anatomical Regions & Clinical Features

### 2.2.1 Fracture of the VAULT (Cranial Roof)

**Bones involved:** Frontal, Parietal, parts of Temporal, parts of Occipital.

| Feature | Clinical Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Type** | Linear (most common), Depressed, Comminuted, Compound |
| **Presentation** | Obvious deformity, swelling, scalp laceration, palpable depression |
| **Classic Sign** | Boggy swelling (subgaleal hematoma) — **"Pott's puffy tumor"** in frontal sinus fractures |
| **First-Aid Priority** | Control scalp bleeding (rich vascular supply — can cause hypovolemic shock in children) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Depressed vault fracture:** The fractured bone fragment is pushed **inward toward the brain**, often requiring urgent surgical elevation. Compound depressed fractures have a **15–20% infection rate** if not surgically debrided within 24 hours.

### 2.2.2 Fracture of the BASE (Basilar Skull Fracture)

**Bones involved:** Sphenoid, Occipital, Petrous Temporal, Ethmoid (forms the three cranial fossae).

| Fossa | Bones Forming It | Key Clinical Sign |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Anterior Cranial Fossa** | Frontal, Ethmoid, Sphenoid (lesser wing) | **Raccoon eyes** (bilateral periorbital ecchymosis) |
| **Middle Cranial Fossa** | Sphenoid (greater wing), Petrous Temporal | **Battle's sign** (mastoid ecchymosis), **CSF otorrhea** |
| **Posterior Cranial Fossa** | Occipital, Petrous Temporal (posterior) | Cervical bruising, lower cranial nerve palsies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CSF Leak Test (KTU High-Yield):** Clear fluid draining from the nose or ear after head trauma is highly suggestive of CSF leak due to dural tear. The **"Halo Sign"** (or **"Ring Sign"**): when the fluid is dropped on filter paper, blood forms a central spot and CSF spreads outward forming a halo ring. **Confirmation is via β-2 transferrin testing** (a protein found almost exclusively in CSF).

### 2.2.3 Fracture of the SIDES (Lateral Skull)

**Bones involved:** Temporal (squamous and petrous parts), Greater Wing of Sphenoid, Zygomatic arch.

| Feature | Clinical Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Common Cause** | Side-impact collisions (motorbike accidents, industrial blow to temple) |
| **Hallmark Sign** | Bleeding from the ear (**hemotympanum**), ruptured tympanic membrane |
| **Danger Zone** | **Middle Meningeal Artery** runs in a groove on the inner surface of the temporal bone — fracture here → **Extradural (Epidural) Hematoma** |
| **Cranial Nerves at Risk** | CN VII (Facial), CN VIII (Vestibulocochlear) — facial palsy + hearing loss |

> [!WARNING]
> **Epidural Hematoma — The "Talk and Die" Syndrome:** A patient with a temporal bone fracture may have a **brief loss of consciousness (LOC)**, then a **lucid interval (hours)**, followed by rapid deterioration. This is a **neurosurgical emergency** — urgent CT and craniotomy required.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| Parameter | Vault Fracture | Base Fracture | Lateral (Side) Fracture |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bones** | Frontal, Parietal | Sphenoid, Occipital, Ethmoid | Temporal, Sphenoid (greater wing) |
| **Frequency** | Most common | ~ 4% of all skull fractures | Common in RTA |
| **Visible Externally** | Yes (depression, swelling) | No (hidden) | Sometimes (ear bleed) |
| **Pathognomonic Sign** | Boggy swelling | Raccoon eyes, Battle's sign, CSF leak | Hemotympanum |
| **Major Complication** | Intracranial hematoma, infection | Meningitis (via CSF fistula) | Epidural hematoma (middle meningeal a.) |
| **First-Aid Priority** | Hemorrhage control, immobilization | Airway protection, no nasal packing | Side-lying position (bleeding ear down) |
| **Diagnostic Tool** | CT scan (gold standard) | CT + β-2 transferrin test | CT + otoscopic exam |
| **Mortality Risk** | Moderate (depends on depression) | High (brainstem proximity) | High if epidural hematoma missed |

---

## 2.4 First-Aid Management Framework

The **ABCDE Protocol** (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure) is the universal KTU-aligned first-aid approach:

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Real-World Utility:** In an industrial accident (factory/construction site), a worker struck on the head by a falling object may present with any of these three fracture types. The first-aider's role is **NOT to diagnose the specific fracture**, but to:
> 1. **Maintain airway** (jaw thrust — NOT head-tilt in suspected cervical spine injury).
> 2. **Control external bleeding** (direct pressure on scalp wounds).
> 3. **Immobilize cervical spine** (assume co-existing neck injury).
> 4. **Do NOT plug bleeding ears or nose** (to prevent rising intracranial pressure).
> 5. **Activate EMS / shift to trauma center** — the "Golden Hour" principle applies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Clinical Reasoning & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithmic Approach to a Head-Injured Patient

```
STEP 1: Scene Safety & Primary Survey (ABCs)
    ↓
STEP 2: GCS Calculation (Eye + Verbal + Motor = 15)
    ↓
STEP 3: Pupillary Assessment (size, equality, reaction)
    ↓
STEP 4: Look for Lateralizing Signs (one-sided weakness)
    ↓
STEP 5: Inspect for External Signs (Raccoon eyes, Battle's sign, CSF leak)
    ↓
STEP 6: Cervical Spine Immobilization (manual in-line stabilization)
    ↓
STEP 7: Vital Signs Monitoring (Cushing's Triad: ↓HR, ↑BP, irregular RR)
    ↓
STEP 8: Hospital Triage Decision
```

---

## 3.2 Detailed GCS Calculation (Step-by-Step)

The GCS has three components scored independently and summed:

### 3.2.1 Eye Opening Response (E) — Max 4

| Score | Response | Clinical Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 4 | Opens eyes spontaneously | Awake, normal arousal |
| 3 | Opens to verbal command | Drowsy but responsive |
| 2 | Opens to pain | Severe depression of consciousness |
| 1 | No response | Coma |

### 3.2.2 Verbal Response (V) — Max 5

| Score | Response | Clinical Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 5 | Oriented and converses | Normal cognition |
| 4 | Confused conversation | Disoriented but coherent |
| 3 | Inappropriate words | Rambling, no meaningful conversation |
| 2 | Incomprehensible sounds | Moaning, groaning only |
| 1 | No response | Coma |

### 3.2.3 Motor Response (M) — Max 6

| Score | Response | Clinical Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 6 | Obeys commands | Normal motor function |
| 5 | Localizes pain | Purposeful movement toward stimulus |
| 4 | Withdraws from pain | Flexion withdrawal |
| 3 | Abnormal flexion (decorticate) | Brainstem injury above red nucleus |
| 2 | Abnormal extension (decerebrate) | Brainstem injury at/below red nucleus |
| 1 | No response | Deep coma / brain death consideration |

> **Example Calculation:**
> Patient opens eyes to pain (E=2), moans incomprehensibly (V=2), withdraws from pain (M=4).
> Total GCS = 2 + 2 + 4 = **8 → Severe Head Injury → Immediate intubation protocol.**

---

## 3.3 Cushing's Triad — Late Sign of Raised Intracranial Pressure (ICP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cushing's Triad** is a clinical hallmark of **impending brain herniation**:

1. **Bradycardia** (slow heart rate) — $\text{HR} < 60 \text{ bpm}$
2. **Hypertension** with **widened pulse pressure** — $\text{SBP} > \text{normal}$, $\text{DBP}$ unchanged or lower
3. **Irregular respiration** (Cheyne-Stokes, ataxic, or apneustic breathing)

> When all three signs appear together in a head-injured patient, this represents the **terminal compensatory phase** of rising ICP. Immediate hyperventilation, osmotic therapy (mannitol), and neurosurgical decompression are required.

---

## 3.4 Decision Matrix — When to Suspect Each Fracture Type

| Clinical Finding | Suspected Fracture | Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Visible depression in skull | **Vault (depressed)** | High-velocity direct impact |
| Raccoon eyes (periorbital bruising) | **Anterior cranial fossa (base)** | Blow to forehead / fall on face |
| Battle's sign (mastoid bruising) | **Middle cranial fossa (base)** | Blow to back of head / temporal area |
| Clear fluid from nose | **Anterior fossa (cribriform plate)** | Shearing forces tearing dura |
| Clear fluid from ear | **Middle fossa (petrous temporal)** | Lateral impact |
| Bleeding from ear + facial palsy | **Lateral skull (temporal bone)** | Side impact |
| Lucid interval → coma | **Lateral skull → Epidural hematoma** | Middle meningeal artery rupture |
| "Pott's puffy tumor" | **Frontal sinus / anterior vault** | Subperiosteal abscess / hematoma |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: First-Aid Procedure for a Suspected Skull Fracture (On-Site)

**Scenario:** A construction worker is struck on the side of the head by a falling scaffold pipe. He is unconscious, bleeding from the right ear, and has a swelling over the right temple.

**Step 1: Scene Safety**
- Ensure the area is clear of further falling debris; don personal protective equipment (PPE — gloves, mask).

**Step 2: Primary Survey (ABCDE)**

- **A — Airway:** Open using **jaw-thrust maneuver** (do NOT tilt head, as cervical spine injury is suspected).
- **B — Breathing:** Check chest rise, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation (if available).
- **C — Circulation:** Apply direct pressure to any external scalp wound with a sterile dressing. Check pulse (rate, volume).
- **D — Disability:** Calculate GCS. Note pupil size and equality.
- **E — Exposure:** Look for other injuries (especially neck, chest, abdomen).

**Step 3: Critical Interventions**

- Position the patient in the **recovery position with the bleeding ear DOWN** (allows drainage, prevents CSF pooling in middle ear).
- **Do NOT pack the ear or nose** — this could trap CSF and cause meningitis.
- Maintain **manual in-line cervical immobilization**.
- Cover the patient with a blanket (prevent hypothermia).

**Step 4: Activate Emergency Medical Services (EMS)**
- Provide the dispatch with: mechanism of injury, GCS score, pupil findings, presence of CSF/blood leak, vital signs.

**Step 5: Continuous Monitoring**
- Reassess GCS every **5 minutes**.
- Watch for Cushing's Triad.
- Be prepared for vomiting (have suction ready; turn patient to side).

**Step 6: Documentation (SBAR Format)**
- **S**ituation: "40-year-old male, struck on head by falling pipe, unconscious."
- **B**ackground: "Construction site accident, no known medical history."
- **A**ssessment: "GCS 6 (E1, V1, M4), right ear bleeding, right pupil dilated."
- **R**ecommendation: "Immediate CT head, neurosurgical consult, intubation."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Skull Anatomy — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CRANIUM["CRANIAL VAULT - The Braincase"]
        direction TB
        subgraph VAULT["VAULT - Superior Aspect"]
            FR["FRONTAL BONE"]
            RP["RIGHT PARIETAL"]
            LP["LEFT PARIETAL"]
            OC["OCCIPITAL BONE"]
        end
        subgraph BASE["BASE - Inferior Aspect"]
            ACF["ANTERIOR CRANIAL FOSSA - Frontal, Ethmoid, Sphenoid"]
            MCF["MIDDLE CRANIAL FOSSA - Sphenoid, Petrous Temporal"]
            PCF["POSTERIOR CRANIAL FOSSA - Occipital, Petrous Temporal"]
        end
        subgraph SIDES["SIDES - Lateral Aspect"]
            RT["RIGHT TEMPORAL BONE"]
            LT["LEFT TEMPORAL BONE"]
            GWS["GREATER WING OF SPHENOID"]
            ZYG["ZYGOMATIC ARCH"]
        end
    end
    BR["BRAIN - Cerebrum, Cerebellum, Brainstem"]
    CRANIUM --- BR
    RT --> MMA["MIDDLE MENINGEAL ARTERY - Danger Zone"]
    MCF --> CSF["CSF CIRCULATION PATHWAY"]
```

## 4.2 Fracture Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HEAD INJURY PATIENT"] --> B{"Visible External Deformity?"}
    B -- YES --> C["VAULT FRACTURE"]
    B -- NO --> D{"Periorbital or Mastoid Bruising?"}
    D -- YES --> E["BASE FRACTURE"]
    D -- NO --> F{"Ear Bleeding or Facial Palsy?"}
    F -- YES --> G["LATERAL SKULL FRACTURE"]
    F -- NO --> H["Observe and Monitor GCS"]
    C --> I["CT Scan"]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Neurosurgical Management"]
```

## 4.3 Glasgow Coma Scale — Functional Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GCS["GLASGOW COMA SCALE - Total 15"]
        E["EYE OPENING\nE4 - Spontaneous\nE3 - To Voice\nE2 - To Pain\nE1 - None"]
        V["VERBAL RESPONSE\nV5 - Oriented\nV4 - Confused\nV3 - Inappropriate\nV2 - Incomprehensible\nV1 - None"]
        M["MOTOR RESPONSE\nM6 - Obeys Commands\nM5 - Localizes Pain\nM4 - Withdraws\nM3 - Decorticate\nM2 - Decerebrate\nM1 - None"]
    end
    E --> SUM["TOTAL SCORE E + V + M"]
    V --> SUM
    M --> SUM
    SUM --> CLASS["CLASSIFICATION\n15 - Normal\n13 to 14 - Mild\n9 to 12 - Moderate\n3 to 8 - Severe"]
```

## 4.4 First-Aid Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Scene Safety and PPE"] --> S2["Primary Survey ABCDE"]
    S2 --> S3["GCS Calculation"]
    S3 --> S4{"GCS Less Than or Equal To 8?"}
    S4 -- YES --> S5["Immediate Intubation and ICU"]
    S4 -- NO --> S6["Cervical Spine Immobilization"]
    S6 --> S7["Position Ear Down if Bleeding"]
    S7 --> S8["Do NOT Pack Ear or Nose"]
    S8 --> S9["Activate EMS and Continuous Monitoring"]
    S9 --> S10["Hospital Transfer with SBAR Handover"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List any three clinical signs suggestive of a base-of-skull fracture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three classic clinical signs of a base-of-skull fracture are:

1. **Raccoon eyes (bilateral periorbital ecchymosis)** — Indicates fracture of the **anterior cranial fossa** (cribriform plate of ethmoid or orbital roof of frontal bone). Blood tracks forward into the loose periorbital subcutaneous tissue. *(1 mark)*

2. **Battle's sign (retroauricular/mastoid ecchymosis)** — Indicates fracture of the **middle cranial fossa** (petrous part of temporal bone). Blood tracks posteriorly to the mastoid process. *(1 mark)*

3. **CSF otorrhea or rhinorrhea** — Clear watery discharge from the ear (otorrhea) or nose (rhinorrhea), often with a positive "halo sign" on filter paper. Indicates a dural tear with communication between the subarachnoid space and the exterior. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**What is the "lucid interval" in the context of a head injury? Why is it clinically significant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A **lucid interval** is a temporary phase of apparent neurological recovery (normal consciousness and GCS) that occurs **after an initial loss of consciousness** following head trauma, before the patient deteriorates again into coma.

**Mechanism (1 mark):** It classically occurs in **extradural (epidural) hematoma**, usually following a temporal bone fracture that ruptures the **middle meningeal artery**. The initial LOC is from concussion, and the lucid interval occurs before the expanding arterial hematoma causes brainstem compression.

**Clinical Significance (1 mark):** It is a **neurosurgical emergency** — failure to recognize the lucid interval leads to rapid brain herniation and death. **Always obtain a CT head** in any head-injured patient with a history of LOC, even if they appear fully recovered at the time of examination.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 3 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** Classify skull fractures. Describe in detail the clinical features, signs, and complications of a **base-of-skull fracture**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **first-aid management** of a patient with a suspected skull fracture at an industrial accident site. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part A (a) [7 marks]

**Classification of Skull Fractures (2 marks):**

- **Based on Integrity of Skin/Dura:**
  - **Simple (closed)** — Skin intact, dura not torn.
  - **Compound (open)** — Communication between cranial cavity and exterior (dura torn).

- **Based on Fracture Pattern:**
  - **Linear** — Simple crack, common in vault.
  - **Depressed** — Inward displacement of bone fragment.
  - **Comminuted** — Multiple fragments.
  - **Basilar** — Involves skull base.

- **Based on Anatomical Site:**
  - **Vault fractures** (frontal, parietal, occipital)
  - **Base fractures** (anterior, middle, posterior cranial fossa)
  - **Lateral fractures** (temporal, sphenoid)

**Clinical Features of Base-of-Skull Fracture (3 marks):**

| Site | Bone Involved | Sign |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Anterior Fossa | Cribriform plate, orbital roof | **Raccoon eyes**, CSF rhinorrhea |
| Middle Fossa | Petrous temporal, sphenoid | **Battle's sign**, CSF otorrhea, hemotympanum, CN VII/VIII palsy |
| Posterior Fossa | Occipital, clivus | Lower cranial nerve palsy (IX, X, XI, XII), cerebellar signs |

**Complications (2 marks):**
- **Meningitis** (most feared — via CSF fistula).
- **Cranial nerve palsies** (CN I, II, VII, VIII most common).
- **Carotid-cavernous fistula** (if sphenoid fractured).
- **Cerebrospinal fluid leak** (persistent).
- **Intracranial hematoma**.

---

#### Model Answer — Part A (b) [7 marks]

**First-Aid Management of a Suspected Skull Fracture (Industrial Site):**

**Step 1: Scene Safety (1 mark)**
- Clear hazards, don PPE (gloves, mask, eye protection).
- Mark the area to prevent secondary injuries.

**Step 2: Primary Survey — ABCDE (2 marks)**
- **Airway:** Jaw-thrust (not head-tilt, due to suspected C-spine injury).
- **Breathing:** Rate, depth, oxygen saturation.
- **Circulation:** Check pulse, control external scalp bleeding with direct pressure.
- **Disability:** Calculate GCS, check pupils.
- **Exposure:** Full body check for other injuries.

**Step 3: Critical Interventions (2 marks)**
- **Cervical spine immobilization** (manual in-line stabilization with hard collar if available).
- **Position:** Recovery position with the **bleeding ear facing downward** (allows drainage).
- **Do NOT plug ears or nose** (prevents CSF trapping and meningitis).
- **Do NOT remove impaled objects** from skull.
- Monitor vitals every 5 minutes.

**Step 4: Activate EMS and Documentation (2 marks)**
- Call emergency services with mechanism, GCS, and findings.
- **SBAR handover** to receiving hospital.
- Keep patient warm; prevent shock.
- Nothing per oral (NPO) — anticipate surgery.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 3 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Describe the **Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)** in detail. How will you classify head injuries based on GCS? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a detailed note on the **fracture of the vault of the skull** and its first-aid management. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part B (a) [7 marks]

**Glasgow Coma Scale — Detailed Description (5 marks):**

The GCS is a **standardized neurological assessment tool** used to objectively quantify the level of consciousness in head-injured patients. It has three components, scored independently and summed (range: 3–15):

**1. Eye Opening (E) — Score 1 to 4:**
- 4 = Spontaneous
- 3 = To verbal command
- 2 = To pain
- 1 = No response

**2. Verbal Response (V) — Score 1 to 5:**
- 5 = Oriented
- 4 = Confused conversation
- 3 = Inappropriate words
- 2 = Incomprehensible sounds
- 1 = No response

**3. Motor Response (M) — Score 1 to 6:**
- 6 = Obeys commands
- 5 = Localizes to pain
- 4 = Withdraws from pain
- 3 = Abnormal flexion (decorticate posturing)
- 2 = Abnormal extension (decerebrate posturing)
- 1 = No response

**Classification Based on GCS (2 marks):**
- **Mild head injury:** GCS 13–15
- **Moderate head injury:** GCS 9–12
- **Severe head injury:** GCS ≤ 8 (requires intubation)

---

#### Model Answer — Part B (b) [7 marks]

**Vault Fracture of the Skull (4 marks):**

The **vault** is the dome-shaped superior portion of the skull formed by the **frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital bones**.

**Types:**
- **Linear** — Single non-displaced fracture line (most common).
- **Depressed** — Inward displacement of bony fragment.
- **Comminuted** — Multiple fragments.
- **Compound** — Communication with skin/outside.

**Clinical Features:**
- **Scalp swelling** (subgaleal hematoma) — boggy, fluctuant.
- **Palpable depression** in depressed fractures.
- **Scalp laceration** with visible or palpable bone.
- **Cranial nerve deficits** (rare unless basilar extension).
- **CSF leak** (only if fracture extends to base).

**Complications:**
- Intracranial hematoma (subdural, epidural, intracerebral).
- Meningitis (in compound fractures).
- Post-traumatic epilepsy.
- Cosmetic deformity.

**First-Aid Management (3 marks):**
1. **Primary survey** (ABCs) with C-spine immobilization.
2. **Control scalp bleeding** with direct pressure (scalp is highly vascular).
3. **Do NOT apply pressure on depressed fracture** — cover loosely with sterile dressing.
4. **Do NOT remove impaled objects.**
5. Position semi-prone if vomiting; supine with head-up 30° if conscious.
6. **NPO** (nil per oral).
7. Immediate transfer to trauma center with neurosurgical facilities.
8. Continuous GCS monitoring every 5 minutes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Raccoon eyes with direct orbital trauma** — Raccoon eyes are **bilateral, delayed, and not associated with direct periorbital soft tissue injury.** They appear hours after trauma.
> 2. **Battle's sign is NOT a bruise from a blow to the mastoid** — It is blood tracking from a **petrous temporal fracture**, appearing 12–24 hours post-injury.
> 3. **Never use the head-tilt-chin-lift maneuver** in suspected head injury — always use **jaw thrust** due to co-existing cervical spine injury risk.
> 4. **Never pack the nose or ears** in suspected base-of-skull fracture — this **traps CSF**, raises intracranial pressure, and increases meningitis risk.
> 5. **The "lucid interval" is NOT reassurance of recovery** — it is a critical warning sign of expanding intracranial hematoma.
> 6. **Always calculate GCS components separately** — students often write only the total score and lose marks. Examiner wants **E_V_M_ breakdown**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Head Injuries:**

- **Skull = 22 bones** (8 cranial + 14 facial). Cranial bones: **1 frontal, 2 parietal, 2 temporal, 1 occipital, 1 sphenoid, 1 ethmoid**.

- **Three regions of skull fractures:**
  - **VAULT** — Frontal, Parietal, Occipital bones.
  - **BASE** — Anterior, Middle, Posterior cranial fossae.
  - **SIDES (Lateral)** — Temporal (squamous + petrous), Sphenoid (greater wing).

- **Raccoon eyes = Anterior cranial fossa fracture.**

- **Battle's sign = Middle cranial fossa (petrous temporal) fracture.**

- **CSF otorrhea = Middle fossa; CSF rhinorrhea = Anterior fossa (cribriform plate).**

- **Halo/Ring sign = CSF leak test on filter paper.**

- **β-2 transferrin = gold standard laboratory confirmation of CSF leak.**

- **Hemotympanum + Lucid interval + Coma = Extradural hematoma (middle meningeal artery).**

- **GCS formula:** E (1–4) + V (1–5) + M (1–6) = **Total (3–15).**

- **GCS ≤ 8 = Intubate (severe head injury).**

- **Cushing's Triad = Bradycardia + Hypertension (wide pulse pressure) + Irregular respiration = Impending herniation.**

- **Mercer's Point = Fracture at impact site OR at diagonally opposite site (contrecoup).**

- **Kinetic energy = ½mv² — velocity squared means high-velocity injuries are exponentially more destructive.**

- **First-Aid ABCDE approach** with **C-spine immobilization** is non-negotiable.

- **Do NOT plug ears/nose; do NOT remove impaled objects; do NOT give oral fluids.**

- **Position: bleeding ear down, head up 30° if conscious, recovery position if unconscious.**

- **"Golden Hour" principle** — transfer to definitive neurosurgical care within 60 minutes of severe injury.

- **Compound depressed vault fractures** have 15–20% infection rate if not debrided within 24 hours.

- **Meningitis is the most feared complication of base-of-skull fracture with CSF fistula.**

- **CN VII (Facial) and CN VIII (Vestibulocochlear) are most commonly injured in temporal bone fractures.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Circulatory System - Heart Attack, Chest Compression, CPR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The Circulatory System - Heart Attack, Chest Compression, CPR # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 The Circulatory System (Cardiovascular System) ### Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **The Circulatory System** is a closed-loop organ system comprising the **heart**, **blood vessels (arteries, veins, capillaries)**, and **blood**, responsible for the transport of **oxygen (O₂)**, **nutrients**, **hormones**, and **metabolic waste** between the tissues and the lungs, thereby maintaining **homeostasis** and sustaining cellular life. The human circulatory system has two coupled circuits: - **Pulmonary Circulation**: Right heart → Lungs → Left heart (carries deoxygenated blood to the lungs for gas exchange). - **Systemic Circulation**: Left heart → Body tissues → Right heart (delivers oxygenated blood to the entire body). ### Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > Think of the circulatory system as a **building's plumbing and delivery network**. The **heart** is the central **pump station** that pushes fluid (blood) through a network of **pipes** (arteries → capillaries → veins). The **lungs** act as the **water treatment plant** that re-oxygenates the fluid, and the **cells** are the **end-users** receiving oxygen and nutrients while dumping waste (CO₂) back into the system. If the **pump fails** (heart attack/cardiac arrest) or a **pipe gets blocked** (coronary artery blockage), the entire building shuts down within minutes. ### Key Physical Metrics & Constants - Normal adult heart rate: **60–100 beats per minute (bpm)** - Normal blood pressure: **120/80 mmHg** (systolic/diastolic) - Cardiac output (CO): **≈ 5 L/min** at rest - Stroke volume: **≈ 70 mL/beat** - Total blood volume in adult: **≈ 5 L** --- ## 1.2 Heart Attack (Myocardial Infarction – MI) ### Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > A **Heart Attack (Myocardial Infarction)** is the **irreversible death (necrosis) of heart muscle cells (myocardium)** caused by a **sudden and sustained reduction in blood flow** to a region of the heart, most commonly due to **coronary artery occlusion** by an atherosclerotic plaque or a thrombus (blood clot). ### Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > Imagine the heart is a busy city that depends on **three main water supply lines** (coronary arteries) to keep its buildings (heart muscle cells) running. A **heart attack** occurs when one of these supply lines gets **blocked by debris (plaque/clot)**. The buildings downstream of the blockage begin to **starve and die within minutes**. The longer the blockage stays, the more "buildings" (muscle tissue) are permanently destroyed. ### Key Distinction: Heart Attack vs. Cardiac Arrest (HIGH-YIELD FOR EXAMS) > [!WARNING] > Students often confuse these two terms in exams. Memorize the table below carefully. | Feature | Heart Attack (MI) | Cardiac Arrest | |---|---|---| | **Underlying Problem** | Circulation problem (plumbing) | Electrical problem (wiring) | | **Consciousness** | Usually conscious | Unresponsive / unconscious | | **Pulse** | Present | Absent | | **Breathing** | Present (may be labored) | Absent or agonal gasping | | **Heart Rhythm** | Beating (but injured) | Arrhythmia (often VF/VT) | | **First Aid Priority** | Calm the patient, aspirin, oxygen, transport | **CPR + AED immediately** | --- ## 1.3 Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) ### Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)** is a **life-saving emergency procedure** performed when the heart has **stopped beating (cardiac arrest)**. It combines **chest compressions** (to artificially maintain circulatory flow) and **rescue breaths** (to artificially maintain oxygenation), with the goal of preserving **viable brain function** until spontaneous circulation is restored or advanced medical care arrives. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > When the heart's **pump** has failed, the rescuer becomes a **manual backup pump**. Each chest compression **squeezes the heart** between the sternum and the spine, forcing a small bolus of blood forward into the brain and coronary arteries. Rescue breaths inflate the lungs, refilling the blood with oxygen. Together, they keep the **"delivery trucks" (oxygenated blood) rolling** until the real "mechanic" (paramedics/AED) arrives. ### Critical Time Metric > [!WARNING] > **Brain cells begin to die within 4–6 minutes** of oxygen deprivation. For every minute CPR is delayed, survival decreases by approximately **7–10%**. **Early, high-quality CPR can double or triple the chance of survival.** # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Anatomy of the Heart Relevant to First Aid ### Cardiac Chambers (4 chambers) - **Right Atrium (RA)** → receives deoxygenated blood from the body. - **Right Ventricle (RV)** → pumps blood to the lungs via the pulmonary artery. - **Left Atrium (LA)** → receives oxygenated blood from the lungs. - **Left Ventricle (LV)** → pumps oxygenated blood to the entire body via the aorta. ### Coronary Arteries (the "supply lines") - **Left Main Coronary Artery (LMCA)** → branches into **Left Anterior Descending (LAD)** and **Left Circumflex (LCx)**. - **Right Coronary Artery (RCA)**. - Blockage in the **LAD** = "**widow-maker**" heart attack (highest mortality). ### The "Why" Behind a Heart Attack 1. **Atherosclerosis** → fatty plaque builds up in coronary artery walls. 2. **Plaque rupture** → rough surface attracts platelets. 3. **Thrombus formation** → clot blocks the artery lumen. 4. **Ischemia** → downstream myocardium is starved of O₂. 5. **Infarction** → cell death begins within **20–30 minutes** of complete occlusion. --- ## 2.2 Recognizing a Heart Attack: Signs & Symptoms > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently ask: *"List the warning signs of a heart attack."* Memorize these: ### Classic Symptoms - **Chest pain / pressure / squeezing** (often central, described as "an elephant sitting on the chest"). - **Pain radiating** to the left arm, jaw, neck, back, or epigastrium. - **Shortness of breath (dyspnoea)**. - **Cold sweat (diaphoresis)**. - **Nausea / vomiting**. - **Lightheadedness / dizziness**. - **Feeling of impending doom**. ### Atypical Symptoms (more common in women, diabetics, elderly) - Unexplained fatigue. - Upper back or jaw pain. - Indigestion-like discomfort. - Anxiety. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield CPR Formula / Metrics Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes the **2020/2025 AHA (American Heart Association) and Indian Red Cross Society** guidelines for adult CPR. These exact values are high-yield for KTU exams. | Parameter | Standard Value | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Compression Rate | **100 to 120** | compressions per minute | | Compression Depth (Adult) | **At least 5 cm, not more than 6 cm** | centimeters (2 to 2.4 inches) | | Compression-to-Ventilation Ratio (Adult) | **30 : 2** | compressions : breaths | | Rescue Breath Duration | **1 second** each | per breath, sufficient to make chest rise | | Hand Position | **Lower half of the sternum** | avoid xiphoid process | | Chest Recoil | **Allow full recoil** | do not lean on the chest between compressions | | Compression Cycle Duration | **5 cycles in ~2 minutes** | 30:2 × 5 = 150 compressions + 10 breaths | | AED Use | As soon as available | resume CPR immediately after shock | | Survival Drop | **~7 to 10% per minute** | without CPR | ### LaTeX-Isolated Equation (Survival Probability) $$ S(t) = S_0 \cdot (1 - k)^{t} $$ where $S_0$ is the initial survival probability (assumed **100%** at time of arrest), $k$ is the per-minute survival decay constant (**≈ 0.07 to 0.10**), and $t$ is the elapsed time in **minutes** without CPR. --- ## 2.4 The "DRSABCD" Action Plan (Universal First-Aid Algorithm) This is the **gold-standard** emergency response sequence used globally and tested in KTU exams. | Letter | Stands For | Action | |---|---|---| | **D** | **Danger** | Check for hazards to rescuer, victim, and bystanders. | | **R** | **Response** | Tap shoulders, shout "Are you okay?" | | **S** | **Send for help** | Shout for help / call emergency services (**108 in India**). | | **A** | **Airway** | Head-tilt, chin-lift maneuver (unless trauma suspected → jaw thrust). | | **B** | **Breathing** | Look, listen, feel for **≤ 10 seconds**. Gasping ≠ normal breathing. | | **C** | **CPR** | Begin **30 chest compressions : 2 rescue breaths**. | | **D** | **Defibrillation** | Apply **AED (Automated External Defibrillator)** as soon as available. | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Occupational Settings - **Workplace cardiac arrests** account for a significant share of occupational fatalities. - **Trained first-aiders** (mandatory under the **Factories Act 1948** and **OSHA**) can bridge the **4–6 minute** window before ambulance arrival. - Industries with high cardiac risk: **mining, construction, firefighting, electrical work, foundries** (heat stress + exertion). - **AEDs** are increasingly installed in corporate offices, airports, malls, and industrial sites. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP PROCEDURES & ANALYTICAL MATRIX > [!NOTE] > This section provides the **exhaustive, examiner-grade breakdown** of the CPR procedure. Every transition step is explicitly stated, mimicking the rigor of an algebraic derivation but applied to procedural sequences. --- ## 3.1 Step-by-Step: How to Perform Adult CPR (Single Rescuer) ### STEP 1 — Scene Safety Assessment (Danger Check) **Action:** Scan the environment for traffic, fire, electrical hazards, toxic fumes, or unstable structures. **Conversion Logic:** A rescuer who becomes a victim creates **two victims** instead of one. The legal principle of "**Duty of Care**" requires the rescuer to first ensure self-safety. ### STEP 2 — Check Responsiveness **Action:** Tap the victim's shoulders firmly. Shout loudly near both ears: *"Are you okay? Can you hear me?"* **Decision Rule:** - If victim **responds** → leave them in the position found, monitor breathing, call for help. - If victim **does not respond** → proceed to Step 3. ### STEP 3 — Activate Emergency Medical Services (EMS) **Action:** Shout for help. If alone, call **108 (India)** / **112 (EU emergency)** / **911 (US)** immediately. If a mobile phone is available, use **speakerphone** so you can start CPR while talking. **Conversion Logic:** Early EMS activation brings the AED and advanced life support to the scene. The "**phone-first vs. CPR-first**" rule: if alone with an adult, **call first, then CPR** (in children/infants with witnessed collapse, perform **2 minutes of CPR** first, then call). ### STEP 4 — Position the Victim **Action:** Place the victim **flat on their back** on a **hard, firm surface** (floor, not a soft bed). Kneel beside the victim's chest. **Conversion Logic:** A hard surface is essential because the heart must be compressed between the **sternum** and the **vertebral column (spine)**. On a soft mattress, the mattress itself absorbs the compression force, drastically reducing effective cardiac output. ### STEP 5 — Open the Airway (Airway Maneuver) **Action:** Place one hand on the forehead, two fingers of the other hand under the bony part of the chin. **Tilt the head back** and **lift the chin upward** (Head-tilt, Chin-lift). **Conversion Logic:** In an unconscious victim, the **tongue** falls backward and obstructs the pharynx. The head-tilt stretches the soft tissues, lifting the tongue away from the airway opening. ### STEP 6 — Check Breathing (Look, Listen, Feel) **Action:** With the airway open, place your ear near the victim's mouth and nose. **Look** for chest rise, **Listen** for breath sounds, **Feel** for air on your cheek. Spend **no more than 10 seconds**. **Decision Rule:** - **Normal breathing present** → place in **recovery position** (lateral decubitus), monitor. - **Not breathing or gasping (agonal breathing)** → proceed to **Step 7 — begin CPR**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Agonal gasps** are NOT normal breathing. They are reflexive, irregular, and ineffective. Begin CPR immediately. ### STEP 7 — Locate Hand Position for Compressions **Action:** Place the **heel of one hand** on the **lower half of the sternum** (center of the chest, between the nipples). Place the **heel of the other hand on top**, interlock the fingers. Keep fingers **lifted off the ribs** to avoid rib fractures. **Conversion Logic:** The lower half of the sternum overlies the **ventricles** of the heart. Compression here directly squeezes the ventricles, ejecting blood into the aorta and pulmonary artery. ### STEP 8 — Begin Chest Compressions **Action:** - **Lock elbows** straight. - Position shoulders **directly vertically above** the hands. - Compress the chest **straight down** by **at least 5 cm (2 inches)**, but **not more than 6 cm (2.4 inches)**. - **Allow full chest recoil** between compressions (do not lean on the chest). - Compress at a rate of **100 to 120 per minute** (the tempo of the song *"Stayin' Alive"* by Bee Gees is the classic mental metronome). **Conversion Logic (The "Why" of each biomechanical rule):** - **Locked elbows** → transfer body weight, not arm strength → prevents rescuer fatigue. - **Vertical shoulder position** → ensures force is directed downward, not at an angle. - **5–6 cm depth** → generates **systolic arterial pressure of ~60–80 mmHg**, enough to perfuse the brain. - **Full recoil** → allows the ventricles to refill with blood; incomplete recoil reduces cardiac output by up to **50%**. - **100–120/min rate** → optimized for coronary and cerebral perfusion pressure. ### STEP 9 — Deliver Rescue Breaths (After 30 Compressions) **Action:** After 30 compressions, perform **2 rescue breaths**: 1. Maintain head-tilt, chin-lift. 2. Pinch the victim's nose closed. 3. Take a normal breath, seal your lips around the victim's mouth. 4. Blow steadily for **1 second**, watching for the chest to **rise visibly**. 5. Allow the chest to fall, then deliver the second breath. 6. Return immediately to **30 more chest compressions**. **Conversion Logic:** A tidal volume of **~500–600 mL** is sufficient. **Do not over-inflate** — excessive volume causes **gastric distension**, which leads to vomiting and aspiration pneumonia. ### STEP 10 — Continue Cycles **Action:** Continue cycles of **30 compressions : 2 breaths**. After **5 cycles (≈ 2 minutes)**, reassess the victim. If an **AED** is available, apply it without interrupting compressions for more than **10 seconds**. ### STEP 11 — Use the AED **Action:** 1. Power on the AED. 2. Attach **adhesive pads** as shown on the pads (one on the upper-right chest, one on the lower-left ribs). 3. AED analyzes rhythm — **do not touch the victim**. 4. If shock is advised → ensure everyone is clear → press the shock button → **immediately resume CPR** (do not wait to check pulse). 5. If no shock advised → resume CPR immediately. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Compression-Only CPR > [!NOTE] > **Hands-Only CPR** (compressions only, no breaths) is recommended by the AHA for untrained bystanders or when rescue breaths are not feasible. | Scenario | Recommended Action | |---|---| | Trained rescuer, willing to give breaths | Standard CPR (**30:2**) | | Untrained bystander | Hands-Only CPR (compressions only) | | Adult victim of witnessed collapse | Standard or Hands-Only CPR | | Child / Infant + single rescuer | Standard CPR (30:2) preferred | | Drowning victim | Standard CPR (rescue breaths critical) | | Drug overdose / respiratory cause | Standard CPR (rescue breaths critical) | | Rescuer unable / unwilling to give breaths | Hands-Only CPR | --- ## 3.3 Modifications for Children and Infants | Parameter | Adult | Child (1–8 yrs) | Infant (< 1 yr) | |---|---|---|---| | Compression Depth | **5–6 cm** | **5 cm** (≈ 1/3 chest depth) | **4 cm** (≈ 1/3 chest depth) | | Compression Method | 2 hands, heel only | 1 or 2 hands (heel) | **2 fingers** (single rescuer) or **2 thumbs encircling** (2 rescuers) | | Compression-to-Ventilation | 30:2 | 30:2 (1 rescuer) or **15:2** (2 rescuers) | 30:2 (1 rescuer) or **15:2** (2 rescuers) | | Rescue Breaths | 1 second each | 1 second each | 1 second each | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Errors That Cost Marks (and Lives) | Common Mistake | Consequence | Examiner Penalty | |---|---|---| | Inadequate depth (< 5 cm) | Insufficient perfusion | -2 marks (KTU) | | Leaning on chest (incomplete recoil) | Reduced cardiac output | -2 marks | | Compression rate < 100 or > 120 | Suboptimal flow | -1 mark | | Stopping compressions for > 10 sec | Brain ischemia | -2 marks | | Failure to call for AED / help | Delayed defibrillation | -1 mark | | Wrong hand position (xiphoid area) | Risk of liver laceration | -2 marks | | Over-ventilation | Gastric distension, aspiration | -1 mark | | Forgetting to tilt head during breaths | Airway obstruction | -2 marks | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS > [!NOTE] > Below are Mermaid-compliant block diagrams. Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) to satisfy Mermaid compilation safeguards. Labels contain no markdown formatting tags. --- ## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Human Circulatory System Architecture ```mermaid graph TD node1[RIGHT ATRIUM
Receives deoxygenated blood from body] node2[RIGHT VENTRICLE
Pumps blood to lungs] node3[LUNGS
Gas exchange CO2 to O2] node4[LEFT ATRIUM
Receives oxygenated blood from lungs] node5[LEFT VENTRICLE
Pumps oxygenated blood to body] node6[BODY TISSUES AND ORGANS
O2 delivered, CO2 collected] node7[PULMONARY ARTERY
Deoxygenated to lungs] node8[PULMONARY VEIN
Oxygenated from lungs] node9[AORTA
Main artery to systemic body] node10[VENAE CAVAE
Deoxygenated from body] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node7 node7 --> node3 node3 --> node8 node8 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node9 node9 --> node6 node6 --> node10 node10 --> node1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Blood flows in a continuous loop. The **right side** handles deoxygenated blood (toward lungs), and the **left side** handles oxygenated blood (toward body). --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: DRSABCD — Universal Emergency Response Algorithm ```mermaid graph TD stepA[START: Person collapses] stepB[D - DANGER
Check scene safety] stepC[R - RESPONSE
Tap and shout] stepD{Is victim responsive?} stepE[S - SEND FOR HELP
Call 108 in India] stepF[A - AIRWAY
Head tilt chin lift] stepG[B - BREATHING
Look listen feel 10 sec] stepH{Is victim breathing normally?} stepI[C - CPR
30 compressions to 2 breaths] stepJ[D - DEFIBRILLATION
Apply AED if available] stepK[CONTINUE CPR
5 cycles then reassess] stepL[RECOVERY POSITION
Monitor breathing] stepM[WAIT FOR EMS ARRIVAL] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD -->|Yes| stepE stepD -->|No| stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepH -->|Yes| stepL stepH -->|No or gasping| stepI stepI --> stepJ stepJ --> stepK stepK --> stepH stepL --> stepM ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Chest Compression Biomechanics — Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TD compA[RESCUER POSITION
Kneel beside victim
Shoulders directly over hands] compB[HAND PLACEMENT
Heel of one hand on lower half of sternum
Other hand on top, fingers interlocked] compC[COMPRESSION PHASE
Push straight down
Depth 5 to 6 cm in adults] compD[RECOIL PHASE
Allow full chest recoil
Do not lean on chest] compE[FREQUENCY
100 to 120 compressions per minute] compF[CYCLE
30 compressions then 2 rescue breaths] compG[OUTCOME
Generates systolic BP of 60 to 80 mmHg
Maintains brain and coronary perfusion] compA --> compB compB --> compC compC --> compD compD --> compE compE --> compF compF --> compG ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Heart Attack vs. Cardiac Arrest — Decision Branching ```mermaid graph TD brA[VICTIM COLLAPSES OR COMPLAINS OF CHEST PAIN] brB{Is victim conscious AND has a pulse?} brC[HEART ATTACK SUSPECTED
Plumbing problem] brD[CARDIAC ARREST SUSPECTED
Electrical problem] brE[FIRST AID FOR HEART ATTACK
Sit victim down, reassure
Loosen tight clothing
Assist with aspirin 300 mg chewed
Monitor breathing and pulse
Call 108 for transport] brF[FIRST AID FOR CARDIAC ARREST
DRSABCD protocol
Begin CPR 30 to 2
Apply AED
Continue until EMS arrives] brG[BENEFICIAL OUTCOME
Hospital treatment for MI] brH[CRITICAL OUTCOME
Survival depends on early CPR] brA --> brB brB -->|Yes| brC brB -->|No| brD brC --> brE brD --> brF brE --> brG brF --> brH ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP > [!NOTE] > All questions below are modeled on actual KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) patterns. The 14-mark questions feature the **internal choice** typical of Part B questions in KTU question papers. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]** **Q: Define a "heart attack." List any four warning signs of a heart attack.** **Model Answer:** A **heart attack (Myocardial Infarction, MI)** is the **irreversible damage and death of heart muscle cells** caused by a **sudden blockage of blood flow** through a coronary artery, usually due to a **blood clot forming on a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque**. Four warning signs are: 1. **Crushing central chest pain or pressure** (often described as a heavy weight on the chest). 2. **Pain radiating** to the left arm, jaw, neck, or back. 3. **Shortness of breath** and profuse cold sweating (diaphoresis). 4. **Nausea, vomiting, and a feeling of impending doom** with dizziness or lightheadedness. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "heart stops beating" as a sign of a heart attack. **This is wrong** — that is a sign of **cardiac arrest**. In a heart attack, the heart is still beating (but injured). Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this conflation. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]** **Q: What is CPR? State the recommended rate and depth of chest compressions in an adult.** **Model Answer:** **Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)** is a **life-saving emergency procedure** performed on a person in **cardiac arrest** (no heartbeat, no breathing). It combines **chest compressions** (to artificially circulate oxygenated blood) and **rescue breaths** (to artificially oxygenate the blood), with the goal of maintaining **viable brain function** until spontaneous circulation is restored. Recommended adult parameters (per current AHA / Indian Red Cross guidelines): - **Compression rate:** **100 to 120 compressions per minute**. - **Compression depth:** **At least 5 cm (2 inches), not more than 6 cm (2.4 inches)**. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the following TWO questions are alternatives (i.e., the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B). Each has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Pattern]** **Q: (a)** Explain the **anatomy of the human heart** with a neat diagram. Describe the **pulmonary and systemic circulation** pathways. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is a **heart attack (Myocardial Infarction)**? Explain its **causes, warning signs, and the first-aid management** to be provided to a suspected heart-attack victim at a workplace. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer for Q.A(a): The human heart is a **four-chambered muscular organ** located in the **mediastinum**, between the two lungs, behind the sternum. **Chambers:** - **Right Atrium (RA):** Receives deoxygenated blood from the **superior and inferior venae cavae**. - **Right Ventricle (RV):** Pumps blood through the **pulmonary artery** to the lungs. - **Left Atrium (LA):** Receives oxygenated blood from the **pulmonary veins**. - **Left Ventricle (LV):** Pumps oxygenated blood through the **aorta** to the entire body. **Valves (preventing backflow):** - **Tricuspid Valve** (RA → RV), **Pulmonary Valve** (RV → Pulmonary Artery). - **Mitral Valve** (LA → LV), **Aortic Valve** (LV → Aorta). **Circulation Pathways:** - **Pulmonary Circulation** is a short loop from the **right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary vein → left atrium**. Its function is **gas exchange**: releasing CO₂ and absorbing O₂. - **Systemic Circulation** is the long loop from the **left ventricle → aorta → arteries → arterioles → capillaries (tissue exchange) → venules → veins → venae cavae → right atrium**. Its function is to **deliver oxygen and nutrients** to all body tissues and **remove metabolic wastes**. Both circuits work in series; the heart acts as a **dual pump**. The systemic circuit operates at a higher pressure (**≈ 120/80 mmHg**) than the pulmonary circuit (**≈ 25/10 mmHg**). *(Any neat labelled diagram of the heart with pulmonary and systemic circuits marked earns full marks.)* --- #### Model Answer for Q.A(b): **Definition:** A **Myocardial Infarction (MI)** is the **necrosis (death) of heart muscle cells** caused by **prolonged ischemia** (inadequate blood supply) due to occlusion of a **coronary artery**. **Causes:** 1. **Atherosclerosis** (build-up of fatty/cholesterol plaques in the coronary artery wall). 2. **Coronary artery spasm**. 3. **Coronary embolism** (less common). 4. **Risk factors:** smoking, hypertension, diabetes, high cholesterol, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, stress, family history. **Warning Signs:** - Crushing central chest pain, often radiating to left arm, jaw, or back. - Shortness of breath. - Cold sweat, nausea, dizziness. - Sense of impending doom. - Atypical symptoms in women/elderly/diabetics: fatigue, jaw pain, indigestion-like discomfort. **Workplace First-Aid Management:** 1. **STOP** the victim's activity; seat them in a **semi-reclined (Fowler's) position** (not lying flat, not walking). 2. **Call emergency services (108 in India)** immediately — *"suspected heart attack."* 3. **Loosen tight clothing** (tie, belt, collar). 4. **Reassure and calm** the victim — anxiety increases cardiac oxygen demand. 5. If the victim is **not allergic to aspirin** and is **fully conscious**, assist them to **chew and swallow a 300 mg aspirin tablet** (antiplatelet effect). 6. Provide **high-flow oxygen** if available and trained. 7. **Monitor pulse, breathing, and consciousness** continuously. 8. **Be prepared to start CPR** if the victim deteriorates into cardiac arrest. 9. **Do not** allow the victim to walk, eat, drink, or drive. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Pattern]** **Q: (a)** What is **CPR**? List the **DRSABCD action plan** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for performing chest compressions and rescue breaths** in an adult cardiac-arrest victim. State the recommended **rate, depth, and ratio** of compressions to breaths. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer for Q.B(a): **CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)** is a **life-saving emergency first-aid technique** applied to a victim of **cardiac arrest** (no pulse, no breathing) to artificially maintain **circulatory flow and oxygenation** until the heart can be restarted and spontaneous breathing is restored, or until advanced medical care arrives. **The DRSABCD Action Plan:** - **D — Danger:** Before approaching, check the surroundings for hazards (traffic, fire, electricity, chemicals, unstable structures). Do not become a second victim. - **R — Response:** Kneel beside the victim. Tap both shoulders firmly and shout *"Are you okay? Open your eyes."* This checks for consciousness. - **S — Send for Help:** If unresponsive, shout for nearby help. Activate the emergency medical services (EMS) by calling **108 (India)**, or **112 (Europe)**, or **911 (US)**. If alone, use the phone's speaker mode to communicate while beginning CPR. - **A — Airway:** Open the airway using the **head-tilt, chin-lift maneuver** — place one hand on the forehead, two fingers of the other hand under the bony chin, and gently tilt the head back. This lifts the tongue off the pharyngeal wall. - **B — Breathing:** With the airway open, place your ear near the victim's mouth. **Look** for chest rise, **Listen** for breath sounds, and **Feel** for exhaled air on your cheek. Spend no more than **10 seconds**. *Agonal gasps are not normal breathing.* - **C — CPR (Circulation):** If not breathing normally, begin chest compressions combined with rescue breaths at a ratio of **30:2**. - **D — Defibrillation:** As soon as an **AED (Automated External Defibrillator)** is available, switch it on, apply the pads to the victim's bare chest, and follow the spoken/visual prompts. Deliver a shock if advised, then immediately resume CPR. --- #### Model Answer for Q.B(b): **Procedure for Adult CPR (Single Rescuer):** **Phase 1 — Preparation:** 1. Place the victim **flat on a hard surface** (floor, not a soft bed). 2. Kneel beside the victim's chest. 3. Expose the chest (remove clothing if needed). **Phase 2 — Chest Compressions:** 1. Place the **heel of one hand on the lower half of the sternum** (center of the chest, between the nipples). 2. Place the **heel of the second hand on top** of the first; interlace the fingers; lift fingers off the ribs. 3. **Lock elbows** straight and position **shoulders directly above** the hands. 4. Compress the chest **straight down** by at least **5 cm (2 inches)** but **not more than 6 cm (2.4 inches)**. 5. **Allow the chest to fully recoil** after each compression; do not lean on the chest. 6. Compress at a rate of **100 to 120 compressions per minute**. **Phase 3 — Rescue Breaths (after every 30 compressions):** 1. **Tilt the head back** and **lift the chin** to reopen the airway. 2. **Pinch the nose** closed with the hand on the forehead. 3. Take a **normal breath**; seal your lips around the victim's mouth. 4. Blow steadily for **about 1 second**, watching for the **chest to rise**. 5. Allow the chest to fall, then give the **second breath** (also 1 second). 6. Immediately return to **30 more chest compressions**. **Phase 4 — Continuation:** 1. Continue cycles of **30:2**. 2. After **5 cycles (≈ 2 minutes)**, if an AED is available, apply it. 3. If no AED, continue CPR until the victim shows signs of life, an AED is ready, or EMS takes over. **Summary of Adult CPR Metrics:** $$ \text{Compression Rate} = 100 \text{ to } 120 \text{ per minute} $$ $$ \text{Compression Depth} = 5 \text{ cm to } 6 \text{ cm} $$ $$ \text{Ratio of Compressions to Breaths} = 30 : 2 $$ --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Exams on this Topic:** > > 1. **Conflating Heart Attack and Cardiac Arrest** — Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if these are treated as the same condition. Always state the **circulation vs. electrical** distinction. > 2. **Wrong compression depth** — Writing "1 inch" or "2 cm" loses **1–2 marks**. The correct range is **5–6 cm**. > 3. **Forgetting the ratio (30:2)** — A bare mention of CPR without specifying rate, depth, and ratio is considered incomplete; expect a **2-mark deduction**. > 4. **Omitting the DRSABCD sequence** — If asked for "the action plan," writing only "do CPR" without the DRSABCD framework loses **3–4 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part. > 5. **Skipping the "call for help" step** — In an exam scenario, failure to mention **calling emergency services (108 in India)** is flagged as a critical omission. > 6. **Not mentioning AED** — The "D" in DRSABCD is **Defibrillation**. Omitting AED availability loses marks. > 7. **Vague descriptions of hand position** — "Place hand on chest" is not enough. Specify **"lower half of the sternum, between the nipples"**. > 8. **Ignoring pediatric differences** — If asked about children/infants, mentioning the **5 cm depth for children, 4 cm for infants, and 15:2 ratio for two-rescuer pediatric CPR** earns full marks. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:** - **Circulatory System** = heart + blood vessels + blood; two circuits: **pulmonary** (right heart ↔ lungs) and **systemic** (left heart ↔ body). - **Heart Attack (MI)** = **plumbing problem** — coronary artery blockage → muscle cell death. Victim is **usually conscious, has a pulse, has chest pain**. - **Cardiac Arrest** = **electrical problem** — heart rhythm (often VF/VT) stops pumping. Victim is **unresponsive, has no pulse, is not breathing**. - **First aid for MI:** Seat, reassure, loosen clothing, 300 mg chewable aspirin (if no allergy), call **108**, monitor for deterioration to cardiac arrest. - **CPR** is for **cardiac arrest**, NOT for heart attack. - **DRSABCD** = Danger, Response, Send for help, Airway, Breathing, CPR, Defibrillation. - **AED (Automated External Defibrillator)** = portable device that delivers a shock to restart the heart. **Use as soon as available**; do not delay CPR for an AED if not immediately present. - **Adult CPR Metrics:** Rate **100–120/min**, Depth **5–6 cm**, Ratio **30:2**, Breath duration **1 second**, Hand position **lower half of sternum**. - **Hand position rule:** **"Two fingers above the xiphoid process, on the lower half of the sternum"** — never on the xiphoid itself (risk of liver laceration). - **Allow full chest recoil** — incomplete recoil reduces cardiac output by up to 50%. - **Minimise interruptions** to chest compressions — every pause drops perfusion pressure. - **Cycle length:** 5 cycles of 30:2 ≈ 2 minutes. - **Pediatric CPR:** Compression depth ≈ **1/3 of chest depth** (5 cm child, 4 cm infant); two-rescuer ratio **15:2**. - **Hands-Only CPR** is acceptable for untrained bystanders in adult cardiac arrest. - **Brain death begins at 4–6 minutes** without oxygen; survival drops **~7–10% per minute** without CPR. - **Universal emergency number in India:** **108** (ambulance). Unified number: **112**. - **Recovery position**: lateral decubitus (lying on the side) for unconscious but breathing victims, to prevent aspiration. - **Agonal gasps are NOT normal breathing** — start CPR immediately.

topics.title: Shock - Causes, Signs and Symptoms, Management of Shock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shock - Causes, Signs and Symptoms, Management of Shock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Shock

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> **Shock** is a **life-threatening clinical syndrome** characterized by **inadequate perfusion of tissues and organs** with oxygenated blood, leading to **cellular hypoxia, metabolic acidosis, and eventual multi-organ failure** if not recognized and treated promptly.

In first-aid and occupational health terminology (as per the KTU 2024 syllabus), shock refers to the **body's progressive inability to circulate sufficient oxygenated blood to vital organs**, ultimately causing a collapse of compensatory mechanisms. It is **not synonymous with emotional shock or fainting**, though fainting (psychogenic shock) is one mild form.

**Medical Formula Representation of Shock:**

$$\text{Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)} = \text{Cardiac Output (CO)} \times \text{Systemic Vascular Resistance (SVR)}$$

Any factor that severely reduces either **Cardiac Output (CO)** or **Systemic Vascular Resistance (SVR)** will precipitate shock.

$$\text{CO} = \text{Stroke Volume (SV)} \times \text{Heart Rate (HR)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Garden Hose and Sprinkler System"

Imagine your body is a **large garden**, and your heart is the **water pump** connected to a network of hoses (blood vessels) feeding sprinklers (organs and tissues).

- If the **pump fails** (heart cannot pump) → water pressure drops → **Cardiogenic Shock**.
- If the **hoses burst or leak** (bleeding, dehydration) → less water reaches sprinklers → **Hypovolemic Shock**.
- If the **hoses become too wide/dilated** (septic, anaphylactic) → water pressure falls even though volume is the same → **Distributive Shock**.
- If a **hose gets blocked** (pulmonary embolism, tension pneumothorax) → water cannot reach parts of the garden → **Obstructive Shock**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> The KTU 2024 module specifically expects students to identify **hypovolemic, cardiogenic, neurogenic, anaphylactic, septic, and psychogenic shock** — all of which are core to occupational first-aid and workplace emergencies.

### 1.3 Why Shock is Critical in Occupational Settings

Workplaces such as **factories, construction sites, chemical plants, and laboratories** are common sites for incidents leading to shock — including **severe bleeding, electrical burns, crush injuries, anaphylaxis (bee/wasp stings, latex exposure), and chemical exposure**. Early recognition and rapid first-aid response are the **single biggest determinants of survival**.

**Key Clinical Metric to Remember:**

> The **normal Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP)** in adults is approximately **120 mmHg** (range: 90–140 mmHg). A sustained reading **below 90 mmHg** in a symptomatic patient is a red-flag indicator of established shock.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Shock — The Four-Family Model

Shock is broadly classified into **four major pathophysiological families**, each with distinct causes but converging into the same final pathway of cellular hypoxia.

---

#### **A. Hypovolemic Shock** (Most common in occupational injuries)

This occurs due to a **loss of intravascular volume** — either blood or body fluids.

**Causes:**
- **Hemorrhage** — external bleeding (lacerations, amputations) or internal bleeding (ruptured spleen, fractured femur).
- **Plasma loss** — severe burns (third-degree burns > 15% BSA in adults).
- **Fluid and electrolyte loss** — prolonged vomiting, diarrhea, profuse sweating, dehydration.
- **Polyuria** — uncontrolled diabetes mellitus or diabetes insipidus.

**Volume Loss Classification (Advanced Concept):**

| Class | Blood Volume Lost | % Loss | Clinical Features |
|-------|-------------------|--------|-------------------|
| I | < 750 mL | < 15% | Mild anxiety, near-normal vitals |
| II | 750–1500 mL | 15–30% | Tachycardia (>100 bpm), mild hypotension |
| III | 1500–2000 mL | 30–40% | Marked tachycardia, hypotension, confusion |
| IV | > 2000 mL | > 40% | Life-threatening, lethargy, anuria |

---

#### **B. Cardiogenic Shock** (Pump failure)

The heart fails as an effective pump despite adequate blood volume.

**Causes:**
