```mermaid
        B3 --> B4[Deep Penetration]
        B4 --> B5[Anterior Chamber Involvement]
        B5 --> B6[Progressive Damage]
    end
    
    A1 -.->|More Common| C[Industrial Settings]
    B1 -.->|More Dangerous| C
    
    C --> D[First Aid: Immediate Irrigation]
    D --> E[15 min for Acid]
    D --> F[30-60 min for Alkali]
    
    style ACID fill:#ffcc99
    style ALKALI fill:#ff6666
    style E fill:#90ee90
    style F fill:#ff9999
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Examination Pattern Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Structure (PCSFT503):**
> - **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE):** 40 marks total
> - **End Semester Examination (ESE):** 60 marks (2 hours)
> - **ESE Question Paper Pattern:** Part A (3 marks × 10 questions = 30 marks) and Part B (14 marks × 2 questions = 28 marks)
> - **Module Distribution:** Approximately 20% weightage per module
> - **This module carries:** ~12 marks in ESE (2 Part A + 1 Part B questions typically)

---

## 5.2 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Arc Eye. List its three main symptoms.

**Model Answer:**

**Arc Eye (Photokeratitis / Welder's Flash)** is an acute, painful condition of the cornea caused by exposure to intense ultraviolet (UV) radiation, particularly UV-B (280-315 nm) and UV-C (100-280 nm) wavelengths. It is essentially a "sunburn" of the corneal epithelium commonly occurring in welders and individuals working near welding operations without proper eye protection.

**Three Main Symptoms (The Classic Triad):**
1. **Severe Eye Pain** - Often described as a feeling of sand or grit in the eye; described as the most characteristic symptom
2. **Photophobia** - Extreme sensitivity to light; patient prefers dark environments
3. **Excessive Lacrimation (Tearing)** - Profuse watery discharge from the affected eye(s)

**Additional symptoms** include redness of the eye, blurred vision, foreign body sensation, eyelid swelling, and headache. Symptoms characteristically develop **4-12 hours** after UV exposure, leading patients to often present at night after daytime work. The condition is reversible, with complete healing occurring within **24-72 hours** with appropriate management.

[KTU University Exam - Model Question]

---

### Question 2: State any three differences between acid burns and alkali burns of the eye.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Acid Burns | Alkali Burns |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **Mechanism of Damage** | Causes **coagulative necrosis** (protein coagulation forming protective eschar) | Causes **liquefactive necrosis** (saponification of fats and dissolution of proteins) |
| **Depth of Penetration** | **Limited penetration** - the eschar barrier prevents deep tissue damage | **Deep penetration** - rapidly enters anterior chamber within minutes |
| **Severity** | Generally **less severe** and self-limiting | **More severe** and progressive damage |
| **Examples of Agents** | Sulfuric acid (battery acid), hydrochloric acid, nitric acid | Sodium hydroxide (drain cleaners), ammonia, lime (cement), potassium hydroxide |
| **First Aid Duration** | Minimum **15 minutes** of irrigation | Minimum **30-60 minutes** of continuous irrigation |
| **Prognosis** | Usually better, often complete healing | Guarded prognosis, may result in permanent scarring and vision loss |
| **pH Range** | pH < 7 | pH > 7 |

**Clinical Note:** Alkali burns are considered **true ophthalmic emergencies** because the liquefactive process continues until the alkali is completely neutralized or removed, often leading to severe complications including corneal perforation, symblepharon formation, and permanent blindness.

[KTU University Exam - Model Question]

---

## 5.3 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A: Eye Trauma and Foreign Body Management

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of foreign bodies in the eye. Describe the first aid management for a foreign body embedded in the cornea.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the first aid management of penetrating eye injuries. Why is bilateral eye patching important in such cases?

#### Model Answer for Question A:

**(a) Classification of Foreign Bodies in the Eye and First Aid for Corneal Embedded Foreign Body [7 Marks]**

**Classification of Foreign Bodies (3 marks):**

Foreign bodies in the eye are classified based on their **location, composition, and depth of penetration**:

**A. Based on Location:**
1. **Extraocular Foreign Bodies** (outside the eyeball):
   - **Conjunctival** - lodged on conjunctival surface
   - **Corneal** - on the corneal surface
   - **Eyelid (Tarsal)** - embedded in inner eyelid

2. **Intraocular Foreign Bodies** (inside the eyeball - emergencies):
   - In anterior chamber
   - In vitreous body
   - In posterior segment (retina)

3. **Orbital Foreign Bodies** (in the orbit surrounding the eye)

**B. Based on Composition:**
1. **Metallic** (iron, steel, copper - may cause rust ring)
2. **Non-metallic** (glass, plastic, wood, sand)
3. **Organic** (plant material, insect parts - high infection risk)

**C. Based on Penetration:**
1. **Superficial** - on surface, easily removed
2. **Embedded** - partially within tissue
3. **Penetrating** - fully penetrated into eye

**First Aid Management for Corneal Embedded Foreign Body (4 marks):**

1. **Initial Assessment:**
   - Do NOT rub the eye (may cause corneal abrasion or deeper penetration)
   - Do NOT attempt removal of embedded object
   - Wash hands and wear gloves
   - Examine the eye in good lighting

2. **Stabilization and Protection:**
   - Cover the affected eye with a sterile eye pad
   - **Cover BOTH eyes** to prevent consensual movement
   - Position the patient in a comfortable semi-upright position

3. **Medical Referral:**
   - **Arrange IMMEDIATE transport to ophthalmologist**
   - This is critical because:
     - Removal requires specialized instruments (slit lamp)
     - Risk of corneal perforation
     - Need for topical anesthesia
     - Risk of infection (especially organic materials)
     - Metallic foreign bodies may leave rust rings

4. **Transport Considerations:**
   - Keep patient calm and still
   - Do not give food or water (in case surgery needed)
   - Carry details of the foreign body type and injury mechanism

5. **Contraindicated Actions:**
   - Do NOT attempt to remove with fingers, cotton swabs, or tweezers
   - Do NOT instill any drops (except sterile saline in some cases)
   - Do NOT apply pressure on the eye

**(b) First Aid Management of Penetrating Eye Injuries and Importance of Bilateral Patching [7 Marks]**

**First Aid Management of Penetrating Eye Injuries (5 marks):**

Penetrating eye injuries involve disruption of the eye wall (cornea or sclera) by a sharp or high-velocity object and represent **ophthalmic emergencies** requiring surgical repair within 24 hours for optimal outcomes.

**Step-by-Step Protocol:**

**Step 1: Scene Safety and Initial Approach (30 seconds)**
- Ensure scene safety before approaching
- Introduce yourself to the patient
- Maintain calm demeanor to reduce patient anxiety
- Explain each action before performing it

**Step 2: History and Mechanism (60 seconds)**
- Ask mechanism: "How did this happen?"
- Determine object: "What penetrated your eye?"
- Note time of injury
- Check for tetanus immunization status

**Step 3: Initial Examination (DO NOT TOUCH)**
- **DO NOT** attempt to remove the impaled object
- **DO NOT** apply pressure to the eye
- **DO NOT** rinse with water (object may be sealing the wound)
- Observe from a distance, document findings

**Step 4: Object Stabilization (Critical Step)**
- If object is protruding: stabilize it with sterile gauze padding around the base
- Create a protective barrier using a paper cup, cardboard, or specialized eye shield
- The shield should be larger than the object and rest on bony orbit, NOT on the eye

**Step 5: Bilateral Eye Patching (Critical Step)**
- Cover the **AFFECTED eye** with the protective shield
- Cover the **UNAFFECTED eye** with a sterile eye pad
- Secure both with light bandages
- Tape from forehead to cheek

**Step 6: Patient Positioning and Transport**
- Position patient at 30-45° angle (semi-Fowler's position)
- **NPO status** (nothing by mouth) - in case surgery needed
- Call emergency services (108/112) or arrange immediate transport
- Continue to reassure patient
- Monitor vital signs during transport

**Step 7: Documentation and Handoff**
- Document mechanism, time, and interventions
- Provide complete history to receiving medical team
- Bring the object (if separate) for identification

**Importance of Bilateral Eye Patching (2 marks):**

Bilateral patching is critical in penetrating eye injuries for the following reasons:

1. **Consensual Eye Movement Phenomenon:** When one eye moves, the other eye moves in synchrony (this is a normal neurological reflex called **Hering's law of equal innervation**). If only the injured eye is covered, the uninjured eye's movement will cause corresponding movement in the injured eye, potentially causing:
   - Further damage to intraocular structures
   - Vitreous prolapse
   - Hemorrhage
   - Expulsion of intraocular contents

2. **Reduction of Ocular Movement:** Covering both eyes significantly reduces total eye movement, providing a stable environment for the injured eye during transport.

3. **Patient Compliance:** The patient instinctively avoids eye movement when both eyes are patched, as it is disorienting to have no visual input.

4. **Protection from Light:** Bilateral patching reduces photophobia and allows the patient to rest.

5. **Prevention of Sympathetic Ophthalmia:** Although rare, bilateral protection reduces risk of autoimmune inflammatory response in the uninjured eye.

---

### QUESTION B: Chemical Eye Injuries and Arc Eye

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the first aid management of corrosive chemical injury to the eye. Explain why immediate irrigation is critical.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is Arc Eye? Explain its causes, pathophysiology, symptoms, and first aid management in detail.

#### Model Answer for Question B:

**(a) First Aid Management of Corrosive Chemical Eye Injury [7 Marks]**

**Corrosive chemical eye injury** is an **ophthalmic emergency** where caustic substances (acids or alkalis) contact the eye surface, causing tissue damage that continues until the chemical is completely removed or neutralized. The severity depends on the chemical nature, concentration, pH, and duration of contact.

**First Aid Management Protocol:**

**Phase 1: Immediate Response (Within 10 seconds) - 2 marks**

1. **STOP the exposure** by removing the patient from the chemical source
2. **BEGIN irrigation IMMEDIATELY** - do not wait for any equipment
3. Use the **fastest available clean water source** (tap water, shower, eye wash station, bottled water)
4. **Time is tissue** - every second of delay increases permanent damage

**Phase 2: Irrigation Procedure (5-30 minutes) - 3 marks**

1. **Patient Positioning:**
   - Tilt head toward the affected side (affected eye DOWN)
   - This prevents chemical runoff into the unaffected eye
   - Position over sink, basin, or eye wash station

2. **Eyelid Management:**
   - Use fingers to hold eyelids open
   - Reassure patient who may resist due to pain
   - Ensure both upper and lower fornices are flushed

3. **Irrigation Technique:**
   - Direct water flow from **inner canthus to outer canthus**
   - Use gentle but continuous flow
   - Encourage patient to move eye in all directions (up, down, left, right) to reach all surfaces
   - Remove contact lenses if present

4. **Duration and Volume:**
   - **Acid burns:** Minimum **15 minutes** continuous irrigation
   - **Alkali burns:** Minimum **30-60 minutes** (alkali penetrates deeper)
   - **Unknown chemicals:** 30 minutes as default
   - Volume: **1-2 liters minimum** for most exposures
   - Continue until pH of tear film normalizes to **7.0-7.4** (test with pH paper)

5. **Special Considerations:**
   - If only one eye wash station bottle available, prioritize affected eye
   - Do not stop irrigation until medical help arrives
   - If possible, use sterile normal saline (0.9% NaCl) instead of tap water

**Phase 3: Post-Irrigation Care (1 mark)**

1. **After adequate irrigation:**
   - Apply sterile eye pad
   - Reassure patient
   
2. **Transport Preparation:**
   - Bring the **chemical container** or **MSDS (Material Safety Data Sheet)**
   - Note the time of injury and total irrigation duration
   - Transport to hospital with ophthalmology services
   - Continue irrigation during transport if possible

**Why Immediate Irrigation is Critical - 1 mark:**

Immediate irrigation is critical because:
1. **Dilution Effect:** Quickly dilutes the chemical concentration, reducing ongoing tissue damage
2. **Removal Effect:** Physically washes away the chemical from the eye surface
3. **pH Normalization:** Restores normal physiological pH (7.0-7.4) of the ocular surface
4. **Time-Damage Relationship:** Tissue damage is **directly proportional to contact time**; alkali can penetrate the cornea within **5-15 minutes**, and the anterior chamber within **minutes to hours**
5. **Limitation of Necrosis:** Stops both coagulative (acid) and liquefactive (alkali) necrosis processes
6. **Prevention of Complications:** Reduces risk of permanent scarring, symblepharon, corneal perforation, and blindness

---

**(b) Arc Eye - Causes, Pathophysiology, Symptoms, and First Aid [7 Marks]**

**Definition (1 mark):**

**Arc Eye**, also known as **Photokeratitis**, **Welder's Flash**, or **UV Keratitis**, is a painful condition of the cornea caused by acute exposure to intense **ultraviolet (UV) radiation**, particularly UV-B (280-315 nm) and UV-C (100-280 nm) wavelengths. It represents a temporary "sunburn" of the corneal epithelium and is the most common occupational eye injury among welders and metal workers.

**Causes (1 mark):**

**Occupational Sources:**
- **Electric arc welding** (most common cause - 90% of cases)
- **Gas welding and oxygen cutting**
- **Plasma arc cutting**
- **Carbon arc lamps** (theater lighting, searchlights)
- **Mercury vapor lamps**
- **Metal halide lamps** (stadium lighting)
- **Germicidal UV lamps** (sterilization)
- **High-intensity discharge lamps**

**Non-Occupational Sources:**
- **Snow blindness** - UV reflection from snow
- **Sun reflection from water or sand**
- **Tanning beds** (improper use)
- **Solar eclipses** (direct viewing)

**Pathophysiology (2 marks):**

The pathophysiology of arc eye involves several key steps:

1. **UV Absorption:** The cornea is highly transparent to visible light but strongly absorbs UV radiation. UV-B and UV-C are absorbed by the corneal epithelium.

2. **Cellular Damage:** UV photons cause:
   - Direct DNA damage to epithelial cells
   - Formation of thymine dimers
   - Oxidative stress and free radical formation
   - Apoptosis (programmed cell death) of epithelial cells

3. **Inflammatory Cascade:**
   - Release of inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, cytokines)
   - Vasodilation of conjunctival vessels
   - Edema of corneal epithelium
   - Sloughing of damaged epithelial cells

4. **Nerve Exposure:** Loss of epithelial cells exposes the dense network of corneal nerve endings, causing severe pain and photophobia.

5. **Healing Phase:** Within 24-72 hours, healthy epithelial cells migrate from the limbus (corneal periphery) to resurface the cornea, restoring normal function.

**Symptoms (1 mark):**

Symptoms characteristically develop **4-12 hours** after UV exposure (delayed onset):

**Classic Triad:**
1. **Severe Pain** - Often described as "sand in the eye" or "hot needles"
2. **Photophobia** - Extreme light sensitivity; patient prefers darkness
3. **Profuse Lacrimation** - Excessive tearing

**Additional Symptoms:**
- Redness (conjunctival injection)
- Blurred vision or hazy vision
- Foreign body sensation
- Eyelid swelling (blepharospasm)
- Headache
- Temporary decrease in visual acuity
- Difficulty keeping eyes open

**First Aid Management (2 marks):**

**Step 1: Remove from Source**
- Move patient away from UV source immediately
- Turn off welding equipment
- Reassure that the condition is **temporary and reversible**

**Step 2: Symptomatic Relief**
- **Cool Compresses:** Apply over closed eyelids for 10-15 minutes
- **Oral Analgesics:** Paracetamol (500-1000 mg) or Ibuprofen (400-600 mg)
- **Lubricating Eye Drops:** Preservative-free artificial tears
- **AVOID:** Topical anesthetics (delay healing, mask symptoms, can be abused)

**Step 3: Light Protection**
- Move to a dim or dark room
- Provide wraparound UV-protective sunglasses
- Cover eyes with eye pads if needed
- Avoid driving, reading, and screen time

**Step 4: Comfort Measures**
- Encourage rest
- Keep patient in low-light environment
- Cold milk compress (traditional remedy) can provide symptomatic relief

**Step 5: Medical Referral**
- Refer to **ophthalmologist** for all cases
- Healing typically occurs within **24-72 hours**
- Antibiotic eye drops may be prescribed to prevent secondary infection
- Follow-up to ensure complete resolution

**Step 6: Prevention Education**
- **Welding helmets** with proper filter shade (DIN 9-13)
- **Auto-darkening helmets** preferred
- **Safety goggles** with UV protection for nearby workers
- **Protective curtains** around welding areas
- **Warning signs** in welding areas
- **Never** watch welding without proper protection

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings and Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**

**1. Time Specification Errors (1-2 marks lost frequently)**
- Many students incorrectly state arc eye symptoms occur "immediately" - **CORRECT:** Symptoms develop **4-12 hours** after exposure (delayed onset)
- Many students say chemical irrigation "few minutes" - **CORRECT:** Minimum **15 minutes** for acid, **30-60 minutes** for alkali

**2. Confusing Acid and Alkali Burns (2-3 marks lost)**
- Students often write that alkali burns are "less dangerous" - **OPPOSITE IS TRUE**
- Alkali causes **liquefactive necrosis** (more dangerous) vs acid causing **coagulative necrosis** (self-limiting)
- Always emphasize: **Alkali burns are MORE severe and require LONGER irrigation**

**3. Misconception About Penetrating Object Removal (Major marks lost)**
- Students incorrectly advise to "remove the object carefully" - **WRONG**
- **NEVER remove** penetrating objects from the eye
- This is a critical life-and-vision-saving rule

**4. Forgetting Bilateral Eye Patching (2 marks lost)**
- Many students mention only patching the injured eye
- **Both eyes must be covered** in serious injuries to prevent consensual movement

**5. Incomplete First Aid Steps (1-2 marks lost)**
- Listing actions without proper sequence
- Missing key steps like: scene safety, PPE use, calling for help, documentation
- Always use a systematic approach (DRABCDE or similar)

**6. Generic Rather Than Specific Answers (1-2 marks lost)**
- "Take to hospital" is incomplete - specify the type of facility, position, what to bring
- Mention specific medications by name (e.g., paracetamol, not just "pain killers")

**7. Not Mentioning pH and Specific Volumes (1 mark lost)**
- Always mention: irrigation until pH normalizes to 7.0-7.4
- Specify volume: 1-2 liters minimum
- Specify flow rate considerations

**8. Skipping Prevention Aspects (1 mark lost)**
- KTU values prevention aspects
- Always include: appropriate PPE (welding helmet shade, safety goggles), training, warning signs

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

### 📋 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams

### 🔑 Eye Injury - Key Points to Remember:

**I. Foreign Body in Eye:**
- [ ] Most common occupational eye injury (~35% of cases)
- [ ] Classic symptoms: pain, tearing, photophobia, redness, foreign body sensation
- [ ] **DO NOT RUB** the eye
- [ ] Loose particles: irrigate gently, remove with moist gauze
- [ ] **Embedded/penetrating objects: NEVER remove** - refer immediately
- [ ] Cover both eyes for serious cases

**II. Eye Trauma:**
- [ ] **Blunt trauma:** Cold compress, eye pad, refer
- [ ] **Penetrating trauma:** Stabilize object, NO removal, NO pressure
- [ ] **Bilateral patching** is MANDATORY for penetrating injuries
- [ ] Position: semi-Fowler's (30-45°)
- [ ] NPO status (nothing by mouth)

**III. Chemical Burns:**
- [ ] **ACID burns:** Coagulative necrosis, 15 min irrigation, less dangerous
- [ ] **ALKALI burns:** Liquefactive necrosis, 30-60 min irrigation, MORE dangerous
- [ ] **pH normal irrigation endpoint:** 7.0-7.4
- [ ] **Volume:** Minimum 1-2 liters
- [ ] **Tilt head:** Affected eye DOWN
- [ ] **Bring container/MSDS** to hospital
- [ ] Continue irrigation during transport

**IV. Arc Eye (Photokeratitis):**
- [ ] **Cause:** UV-B and UV-C radiation
- [ ] **Mechanism:** Corneal epithelial damage ("sunburn" of cornea)
- [ ] **Onset:** **DELAYED 4-12 hours** (not immediate)
- [ ] **Classic triad:** Pain + Photophobia + Tearing
- [ ] **Healing:** 24-72 hours (self-limiting)
- [ ] **Treatment:** Cool compress, dark room, oral analgesics, NO topical anesthetics
- [ ] **Prevention:** Welding helmet (shade 9-13), safety goggles, UV protection

**V. Universal Eye First Aid Rules:**
- [ ] Three **"DO NOTs":** Don't rub, don't apply pressure, don't remove embedded objects
- [ ] Three **"DOs":** Act quickly, protect from further damage, seek professional help
- [ ] Always **wash hands** and use **gloves**
- [ ] **Cover both eyes** for serious injuries
- [ ] **Document** all interventions

**VI. Key Distinctions (Exam Favorites):**
- [ ] **Acid vs Alkali:** Alkali is more dangerous (liquefactive vs coagulative)
- [ ] **Blunt vs Penetrating:** Different management - no pressure, no object removal
- [ ] **Arc Eye vs Foreign Body:** Delayed vs immediate symptom onset
- [ ] **Irrigation duration:** 15 min (acid) vs 30-60 min (alkali) vs 2-5 min (foreign body)

**VII. Important Numbers to Remember:**
- [ ] **1-2 liters** of irrigation fluid for chemical burns
- [ ] **15 minutes** minimum for acid burns
- [ ] **30-60 minutes** for alkali burns
- [ ] **4-12 hours** delayed onset for arc eye
- [ ] **24-72 hours** recovery time for arc eye
- [ ] **24 hours** surgical window for penetrating injuries
- [ ] **pH 7.0-7.4** is the target for tear film normalization
- [ ] **Shade 9-13** for welding helmet filters (DIN standard)

**VIII. PPE for Eye Protection:**
- [ ] Safety goggles (general work)
- [ ] Face shields (chemical handling)
- [ ] Welding helmets (welding operations)
- [ ] Side shields (for impact protection)
- [ ] UV-protective glasses (outdoor work)

### 🎯 High-Priority Topics for KTU 2024 ESE:

1. **Chemical burn first aid** - Most frequently asked (15 marks typical question)
2. **Arc eye management** - Common Part A question (3-7 marks)
3. **Penetrating eye injury** - Important Part B question (7-14 marks)
4. **Foreign body management** - Often asked as Part A
5. **Acid vs Alkali comparison** - High-yield comparison question

### 📚 Mnemonics for Easy Recall:

**"CORNEA" for Arc Eye Symptoms:**
- **C**ornea affected
- **O**cular pain
- **R**edness
- **N**eed for dark room
- **E**xcessive tearing
- **A**bnormal vision

**"FLASH" for Welding Safety:**
- **F**ilter shade correct (9-13)
- **L**ook away from arc
- **A**uto-darkening preferred
- **S**ide shields attached
- **H**elmet always worn

**"Irrigate" for Chemical Burns:**
- **I**mmediately begin
- **R**emove contact lens
- **R**eassure patient
- **I**nner to outer canthus
- **G**ently but continuously
- **A**ffected eye down
- **T**ake container to hospital
- **E**valuate pH if possible
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wounds - Bleeding, Classification, Types of Wounds, Care of Wounds, Bleeding from Special Sites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wounds - Bleeding, Classification, Types of Wounds, Care of Wounds, Bleeding from Special Sites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition of a Wound

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> A **wound** is a disruption or break in the continuity of any body tissue, caused by an external agent such as a mechanical force, thermal injury, chemical action, or electrical trauma. It involves damage to the skin, underlying soft tissue, mucous membranes, or internal organs, with or without loss of tissue.

In KTU board examinations, the working definition expected is: *"A wound is a break in the continuity of the body tissue, most commonly the skin or mucous membrane."*

## 1.2 Definition of Bleeding (Hemorrhage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hemorrhage** is the escape of blood from the blood vessels (arteries, veins, or capillaries) due to a breach in their wall. When bleeding occurs internally within body tissues, it is called a **hematoma**; when it escapes to the outside, it is called **external bleeding**.

The adult human body contains approximately **5 to 6 litres of blood** (≈ **7% to 8% of body weight**). Loss of even 1 to 1.5 litres can produce **hypovolemic shock**, making bleeding control a top priority in first aid.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Garden Hose System:**
> Imagine your body as a sealed irrigation network where water flows through pipes (blood vessels) to nourish a garden (organs/tissues).
> - A **wound** is like a crack or cut in one of the pipes.
> - **Bleeding** is the water leaking out.
> - **First aid** is your emergency patch job: apply pressure (like placing a thumb over a leaking hose), elevate the pipe (elevation reduces pressure), and if it's a major leak, tie off the supply upstream (tourniquet).
> - Different pipes (arteries vs. veins) leak differently — an artery spurts in time with the heart (pulsatile bright red), while a vein oozes steadily (dark red).

## 1.4 Clinical Significance in Occupational Settings

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Industrial/occupational wounds account for nearly **30% to 40% of all workplace injuries** globally. Prompt hemorrhage control within the **first 10 minutes** (the "Platinum 10") dramatically reduces mortality. This is a recurring short-answer topic in KTU ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Bleeding (Based on Source Vessel)

Bleeding is classified by the **type of blood vessel** involved. This classification is critical because it dictates the urgency and method of first aid.

| Type | Vessel | Color & Flow | Pressure | First-Aid Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Arterial Bleeding** | Artery | Bright red, **spurts** in sync with heart beat | High | **HIGHEST** — life threatening within minutes |
| **Venous Bleeding** | Vein | Dark red/crimson, **steady, slow flow** | Low–Medium | Moderate — controlled by direct pressure |
| **Capillary Bleeding** | Capillaries | Bright red, **slow oozing** | Very Low | Low — usually stops with minor pressure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Arterial bleeding is the most dangerous because blood is pumped out under high pressure. A severed femoral artery can cause death in **less than 3 to 5 minutes**.

## 2.2 Classification of Wounds (KTU Board Format)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know the **two-tier classification**:
> 1. **By Causative Agent** (Mechanism of injury)
> 2. **By Degree of Contamination / Open vs. Closed**

### A. Classification by Cause / Type of Wound (Expanded)

1. **Abrasion** (Graze) — superficial scraping; epidermis only; high infection risk (gravel/foreign bodies).
2. **Incision** — clean, sharp cut by knife/blade; well-defined edges; bleeds freely; heals well.
3. **Laceration** — irregular, ragged tear by blunt force; bruised edges; high infection risk.
4. **Puncture** — small entry wound, deep penetration by nail/needle; tetanus-prone.
5. **Penetrating Wound** — entry wound with retained foreign body (e.g., knife, bullet).
6. **Avulsion** — flap or complete tearing away of tissue (e.g., finger tip torn off).
7. **Amputation** — complete severance of a body part (finger, limb).
8. **Contusion (Bruise)** — closed wound, blood vessels under skin rupture; no break in skin.
9. **Crush Injury** — severe compression of tissue; muscle and bone damage; risk of compartment syndrome and rhabdomyolysis.
10. **Burn Wound** — thermal, chemical, electrical, radiation.

### B. Open vs. Closed Wound Matrix

| Feature | Open Wound | Closed Wound |
|---|---|---|
| Skin break | Present | Absent |
| Bleeding | External | Internal (hematoma) |
| Infection risk | High | Low |
| Example | Incision, laceration | Contusion, sprain |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Blood Volume & Shock Estimation

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit / Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Total Blood Volume (Adult) | $BV = 0.07 \times \text{Body Weight (kg)}$ | Litres |
| Total Blood Volume (Child) | $BV = 0.08 \times \text{Body Weight (kg)}$ | Litres |
| Total Blood Volume (Infant) | $BV = 0.09 \times \text{Body Weight (kg)}$ | Litres |
| Class I Hemorrhage | Loss $< 15\%$ of BV (≈ **< 750 mL** adult) | Mild — no symptoms |
| Class II Hemorrhage | Loss $15\%\text{–}30\%$ (≈ **750–1500 mL**) | Tachycardia, anxiety |
| Class III Hemorrhage | Loss $30\%\text{–}40\%$ (≈ **1500–2000 mL**) | Hypotension, confusion |
| Class IV Hemorrhage | Loss $> 40\%$ (≈ **> 2000 mL**) | Lethal — irreversible shock |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This classification (from the American College of Surgeons ATLS protocol) is universally used in occupational first-aid and ER triage. It guides when to activate emergency services.

## 2.4 Wound Healing — Phases (High-Yield)

| Phase | Time Window | Key Events |
|---|---|---|
| **Hemostasis** | 0 to 30 minutes | Platelet plug, fibrin clot |
| **Inflammation** | 1 to 3 days | Neutrophils, macrophages clear debris |
| **Proliferation** | 3 to 21 days | Granulation tissue, collagen, angiogenesis |
| **Remodeling** | 21 days to 2 years | Collagen reorganization, scar maturation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Knowledge of wound healing phases explains *why* we don't apply ointments in the first 24 hours and *why* sutures are removed between **5 to 14 days** depending on site (face 5 days, limbs 10–14 days).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Blood Volume Loss Calculation — Worked Example

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **KTU Model Problem:** A **70 kg male worker** has sustained a deep laceration on his thigh. He has lost approximately **1200 mL** of blood. Calculate his percentage blood loss and identify the class of hemorrhage.

### Step 1 — Calculate Total Blood Volume (BV)

Using the formula:

$$BV = 0.07 \times \text{Body Weight}$$

Substituting:

$$BV = 0.07 \times 70 \text{ kg}$$

$$BV = 4.9 \text{ litres} = 4900 \text{ mL}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Percentage Loss

$$\% \text{Loss} = \left(\frac{\text{Blood Lost}}{\text{Total BV}}\right) \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Loss} = \left(\frac{1200}{4900}\right) \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Loss} \approx 24.49\%$$

### Step 3 — Classification

Since the loss lies between **15% and 30%**, this is **Class II Hemorrhage**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Signs (to be written in exam):** Resting tachycardia (HR > 100), anxiety, narrowing pulse pressure, slight fall in systolic BP, urine output 20–30 mL/hr. Requires IV fluid resuscitation and urgent transport.

## 3.2 Decision Matrix — First Aid by Wound Type

| Wound Type | Immediate First-Aid Action | Special Precaution |
|---|---|---|
| **Abrasion** | Irrigate with clean water, remove grit | Anti-tetanus prophylaxis |
| **Incision** | Direct pressure → Sterile dressing → Suture referral | Check nerve/tendon damage |
| **Laceration** | Pressure → Clean → Sterile dressing | Foreign body removal carefully |
| **Puncture** | Do NOT remove deeply embedded object | Tetanus booster essential |
| **Avulsion** | Preserve flap with saline gauze | Reimplantation window ~ 6 h |
| **Amputation** | Wrap part in saline-soaked gauze, place in sealed bag, then on ice | Do NOT let part touch ice directly |
| **Contusion** | RICE protocol (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) | Watch for compartment syndrome |

## 3.3 Standard Hemorrhage Control Algorithm — DR. CAB (KTU Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2024 KTU syllabus emphasizes the **DR. CAB** mnemonic (modern update of ABC):

1. **D — Danger:** Ensure scene safety (no live wires, no traffic).
2. **R — Response:** Check responsiveness (tap, shout).
3. **C — Catastrophic Hemorrhage:** Apply **direct pressure** immediately. If limb and severe, apply **tourniquet** proximal to wound.
4. **A — Airway:** Open the airway (jaw thrust if cervical spine suspected).
5. **B — Breathing:** Check rise and fall of chest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU board evaluation, stating "**Check for danger first**" carries marks — many students jump to bleeding control ignoring scene safety.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Classification of Wounds — Block Architecture

```mermaid
    classDef closed fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#FFF
    classDef type fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    A[WOUNDS]:::root
    A --> B[Open Wound - Skin Broken]:::open

    B --> B1[Abrasion]:::type

    B --> B7[Amputation]:::type
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q]**
*Define a wound. List any four types of open wounds.*

**Model Answer:**

A wound is defined as a **break or discontinuity in the body tissue**, particularly the skin or mucous membrane, caused by an external agent such as mechanical force, heat, chemicals, or electricity.

**Four types of open wounds:**
1. **Abrasion** — superficial scrape of epidermis (e.g., road rash).
2. **Incision** — clean cut by sharp object (e.g., knife cut).
3. **Laceration** — irregular tear of tissue by blunt force.
4. **Puncture** — deep, narrow wound by pointed object (e.g., nail prick).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **laceration** with **incision**. A laceration has irregular, bruised edges; an incision has clean, sharp edges. Marks are deducted if confused.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q]**
*Differentiate between arterial and venous bleeding.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Arterial Bleeding | Venous Bleeding |
|---|---|---|
| Vessel | Artery | Vein |
| Colour | Bright scarlet red | Dark crimson/maroon |
| Flow | Spurting, pulsatile | Steady, continuous flow |
| Pressure | High | Low |
| First aid | Direct pressure + tourniquet if needed | Direct pressure usually sufficient |
| Severity | Life-threatening | Less urgent (unless major vein) |

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(a)* Classify wounds in detail. Explain the first-aid management of a penetrating abdominal wound. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* Describe the clinical features and first-aid management of **bleeding from the nose (epistaxis)** and **bleeding from the ear**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Answer — Classification & Abdominal Wound Care

**Classification of Wounds (5 marks):**

Wounds can be classified as:

**A. Open Wounds (skin broken):**
- **Abrasion** — superficial scrape, often contaminated.
- **Incision** — sharp, clean cut, bleeds freely.
- **Laceration** — torn, irregular edges.
- **Puncture** — narrow, deep.
- **Penetrating** — foreign body remains lodged.
- **Avulsion** — tissue torn away partially.
- **Amputation** — complete severance.

**B. Closed Wounds (skin intact):**
- **Contusion** — bruise.
- **Hematoma** — localized collection of blood.
- **Crush injury** — compressive damage to tissues.

**First Aid for Penetrating Abdominal Wound (2 marks):**

1. **Do NOT remove** the impaled object — it may be tamponading injured vessels. Removing it could cause fatal hemorrhage.
2. Stabilize the object in place with bulky dressings around it.
3. Cover exposed abdominal contents (evisceration) with a **sterile saline-moistened dressing** and then a waterproof cover.
4. Place patient in **supine with knees slightly flexed** to reduce abdominal wall tension.
5. **Nil per oral** (NPO) — nothing by mouth.
6. Monitor for signs of shock; activate EMS.
7. Keep patient warm; do not give anything to eat or drink.

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer — Epistaxis & Ear Bleeding

**Epistaxis (Nose Bleed) — 3.5 marks:**

**Features:** Bright red bleeding from one or both nostrils, often anterior (Kiesselbach's plexus).

**First Aid:**
1. Sit patient down, head **slightly forward** (NOT backward — to prevent aspiration).
2. Pinch the **soft part of the nose** firmly for **10–15 minutes continuous**.
3. Apply cold compress over the bridge of the nose.
4. Encourage mouth breathing; spit out blood instead of swallowing.
5. If bleeding persists beyond 20 minutes, refer to ENT specialist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Point:** Always lean **forward**, never backward. Tilting back causes blood to enter the airway and stomach, leading to aspiration or vomiting.

**Ear Bleeding (Otorrhagia) — 3.5 marks:**

**Features:** Blood from ear canal, possibly with clear fluid (CSF suggesting skull fracture).

**First Aid:**
1. Place patient in **recovery position with affected ear DOWN**.
2. **Do NOT plug the ear** — plugging increases intracranial pressure if skull base fracture.
3. Place a **sterile dressing loosely** over the ear.
4. **Do NOT irrigate** the ear.
5. Monitor for signs of skull fracture — raccoon eyes, Battle's sign, CSF rhinorrhea.
6. Transport urgently to neurosurgical care.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Students commonly lose 2 marks by **plugging the ear with cotton**. This is strictly prohibited when skull base fracture is suspected because it can mask CSF leak and increase infection risk.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **care of wounds** in first-aid. Discuss the immediate steps for **hemorrhage control**. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* Describe the recognition and first-aid management of **bleeding from special sites** — lungs (hemoptysis), stomach (hematemesis), and uterus (vaginal bleeding in non-pregnant woman). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Answer — Wound Care & Hemorrhage Control

**Principles of Wound Care (4 marks):**

The **AID MEMORY** mnemonic:
- **A — Assess:** Scene safety, mechanism, severity.
- **I — Irrigate:** Clean with running saline or clean water (antiseptics only on intact skin).
- **D — Dress:** Sterile dressing, not too tight.
- **M — Medicate:** Pain relief only after medical assessment.
- **E — Escalate:** Refer for tetanus prophylaxis, antibiotics, sutures.
- **M — Monitor:** Watch for infection (redness, swelling, pus, fever).
- **O — Observe for shock.**
- **R — Record:** Document time, mechanism, intervention.
- **Y — Yield care:** Hand over to EMS with clear notes.

**Hemorrhage Control Steps (3 marks):**

Order of priority:
1. **Direct Pressure** — firm pressure with sterile pad for 10 minutes uninterrupted. (Gold standard)
2. **Elevation** — raise limb above heart level (only if no fracture).
3. **Pressure Bandage** — once bleeding controlled, secure dressing with bandage.
4. **Pressure Points** — brachial (arm), femoral (leg) if direct pressure fails.
5. **Tourniquet** — last resort for life-threatening limb bleeding. Note **time of application** (max 1.5–2 hours).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students often release pressure too early to "check" if bleeding has stopped. **Do not lift the dressing for at least 10 minutes.** If it soaks, add more layers on top — do not remove.

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer — Special Site Bleeding

**Hemoptysis (Lung Bleeding) — 2.5 marks:**

- **Recognition:** Bright red, frothy blood coughed up; often preceded by chest pain or cough.
- **First Aid:** Reassure, position patient **upright or half-sitting**, lean slightly forward. Allow patient to spit blood; do not suppress cough unless violent. Keep warm; oxygen if available. Urgent hospital transfer with suspected TB or pulmonary embolism workup.

**Hematemesis (Stomach Bleeding) — 2.5 marks:**

- **Recognition:** Vomit containing bright red blood or **coffee-ground** material (partially digested blood). May be associated with epigastric pain and history of peptic ulcer or liver disease.
- **First Aid:** Recovery position (left lateral) to prevent aspiration. **Nil per oral**. Monitor vitals (pulse, BP). Keep warm. Activate EMS urgently — suspect upper GI bleed; mortality high.

**Vaginal Bleeding (Non-Pregnant) — 2 marks:**

- **Recognition:** Abnormal vaginal blood loss; may include menstrual flooding.
- **First Aid:** Sanitary pad (do not use tampons). Reassure. Note amount, colour, clots. Monitor for shock. Refer for gynecological evaluation.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST REMEMBER before KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Wound = **break in tissue continuity**; Hemorrhage = **escape of blood**.
- ✅ Adult blood volume = **7% of body weight** (≈ 5 L in 70 kg adult).
- ✅ **Arterial bleeding**: bright red, **spurting** → HIGHEST priority.
- ✅ **Class III hemorrhage** = loss of **30%–40%** blood = 1500–2000 mL = hypotension + confusion.
- ✅ **Pressure** for **10 minutes** minimum — do not lift to peek.
- ✅ **Tourniquet** = last resort; always **note time** of application.
- ✅ **Epistaxis** → lean **forward**, pinch **soft** part of nose.
- ✅ **Ear bleed** → **do NOT plug** (suspect skull fracture if CSF).
- ✅ **Hematemesis** → coffee-ground vomit; **recovery position**.
- ✅ **Hemoptysis** → frothy bright red; upright posture.
- ✅ **Penetrating wound** → **NEVER remove** impaled object.
- ✅ Wound healing phases: **Hemostasis → Inflammation → Proliferation → Remodeling**.
- ✅ **Abrasions** = tetanus-prone; **Amputated parts** = wrap in saline, then ice (do not touch ice).
- ✅ DR. CAB is the modern KTU-preferred primary survey sequence.
- ✅ Always check **Danger** first; **Gloves** before touching blood.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** Memorize the differences in a single comparison table (Bleed color × flow × pressure × first aid). For 14-mark answers, structure with headings — examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fractures - Classification of Fractures, Principles of Immobilisation, Sprains and Dislocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fractures - Classification of Fractures, Principles of Immobilisation, Sprains and Dislocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Fracture?

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
A **fracture** is a complete or incomplete break in the continuity of a bone, cartilage, or tooth resulting from the application of a force greater than the bone's structural tolerance. In occupational and emergency first-aid contexts, a fracture is defined as a traumatic disruption of the cortical and/or cancellous architecture of bone tissue, usually accompanied by injury to surrounding soft tissues, blood vessels, and nerves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):** "A fracture is a break in the continuity of a bone, which may be complete or incomplete, simple or compound, and is typically caused by direct blow, indirect force, twisting, or pathological weakening of bone."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **brittle chalk stick** (fresh, dry chalk). If you press it gently along its length, nothing happens. If you twist it sharply, it may snap. If you hit it directly with a hammer, it shatters into multiple pieces. If you apply repeated mild bending (like bending a green twig slowly back and forth), it may finally crack from fatigue even though no single force was extreme.

A **bone is similar**, but it is:
- **Stronger** in compression (resists pushing forces)
- **Weaker** in torsion (twisting forces)
- **Vulnerable** to high-velocity direct impact
- **Subject to fatigue fractures** from repeated low-grade stress

In workplace settings — construction sites, factories, road accidents — these mechanisms translate to specific fracture patterns that the first-aider must recognize instantly.

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary (Bolded Standard Terminology)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Fracture** | A break in the continuity of bone |
| **Dislocation** | Displacement of a bone from its normal joint position |
| **Sprain** | Stretching or tearing of ligaments around a joint |
| **Strain** | Stretching or tearing of muscle fibres |
| **Immobilisation** | The act of preventing movement of an injured part |
| **Splint** | A rigid device used to immobilise a fractured limb |
| **Crepitus** | A grating sensation or sound heard when bone ends rub together |
| **Deformity** | Abnormal shape of the limb indicating displacement |
| **Traction** | Gentle pulling along the long axis of a bone to align a fracture |
| **Compound (Open) fracture** | Fracture communicating with the external environment through a wound |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** All fractures should be treated as serious injuries until proven otherwise by an X-ray. A first-aider's job is **not to heal** but to **stabilise** and **prevent further damage**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Fractures — The Master Framework

Fractures are classified in **five major ways**. KTU examiners expect you to be able to list, define, and give an example of each.

---

#### **A. Based on Completeness of the Break**

| Sub-type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Complete fracture** | Bone is broken all the way through into two or more separate pieces | Transverse fracture of femur |
| **Incomplete fracture** | Bone is cracked but not separated into two pieces | Greenstick fracture in children; hairline (stress) fracture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Greenstick fracture** is unique to children because their bones are more flexible (more collagen, less mineral). Like a young tree branch, they bend and crack on one side only.

---

#### **B. Based on Communication with the External Environment**

| Sub-type | Definition | First-Aid Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Closed (Simple) fracture** | Skin over the fracture site is **intact**; bone does not protrude | Lower risk of infection |
| **Open (Compound) fracture** | The broken bone **communicates with the outside** through a wound | **HIGH infection risk**; cover with sterile dressing; rush to hospital |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU favourite question:** "Differentiate between open and closed fractures." Always mention: open fractures have **higher risk of osteomyelitis** (bone infection), **haemorrhage**, and **tetanus**. First-aider must NOT push the bone back.

---

#### **C. Based on the Pattern of the Fracture Line**

| Type | Description | Typical Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Transverse** | Break is at right angles to the long axis of the bone | Direct blow |
| **Oblique** | Break is at an angle (diagonal) to the long axis | Combination of angulation and compression |
| **Spiral** | Break curves around the bone like a corkscrew | Twisting force (e.g., sports injury) |
| **Comminuted** | Bone is broken into **three or more fragments** | High-energy trauma (RTA, fall from height) |
| **Impacted** | One fragment is driven into another | Fall from height landing on heels |
| **Complicated** | Fracture is associated with injury to a major structure (nerve, vessel, organ) | Penetrating wound |
| **Depressed** | Bone fragment is pushed inward (typical of skull fractures) | Direct blow to skull with blunt object |
| **Compressional (Wedge)** | Bone collapses under axial load | Vertebral fractures |

> [!NOTE]
> The **pattern** of a fracture is a forensic clue to the mechanism of injury — examiners love asking you to deduce the force from the fracture pattern.

---

#### **D. Based on Etiology (Cause)**

| Type | Cause | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Traumatic fracture** | Single high-energy external force | RTA, fall |
| **Pathological (Spontaneous) fracture** | Bone weakened by disease breaks under trivial force | Osteoporosis, bone cyst, osteomyelitis, cancer metastasis |
| **Stress (Fatigue) fracture** | Repeated sub-threshold loading causes micro-fractures that propagate | "March fracture" in soldiers, runners |
| **Avulsion fracture** | A fragment of bone is torn away by a tendon or ligament under sudden tension | Sudden pull of a muscle |

---

#### **E. Special (Paediatric & Joint-Related) Classifications**

- **Greenstick fracture** – Incomplete, one cortex breaks, the other bends (children).
- **Torus (Buckle) fracture** – Cortex buckles without breaking; common in children after a fall on an outstretched hand.
- **Epiphyseal (Salter-Harris) fracture** – Fracture through the growth plate in children; risk of growth arrest.
- **Articular fracture** – Involves a joint surface; high risk of post-traumatic arthritis.

---

### 2.2 Principles of Immobilisation (KTU High-Yield Concept)

**Definition:** Immobilisation is the process of holding a fractured or injured part **stiff and motionless** to prevent further damage during transportation and to reduce pain and bleeding.

#### **The 7 Golden Rules of Immobilisation**

1. **Do not move the casualty** until the injured part is immobilised, unless there is environmental danger.
2. **Steady and support** the injured part **before and during** application of the splint.
3. **Immobilise the joint above AND the joint below** the fracture site.
4. **Pad the splint** to prevent pressure sores and discomfort.
5. **Tie the bandages above and below** the fracture, **never directly over** the fracture site.
6. **Check circulation** distal to the splint (capillary refill, sensation, movement of fingers/toes) before AND after application.
7. **Reassure the casualty** and transport in the most comfortable position (usually lying down).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Rule 3 is the most frequently asked. "Why immobilise the joint above and below?" — Because muscles attached across those joints can otherwise pull the broken bone fragments out of alignment.

#### **Types of Splints**

| Splint Type | Description | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Rigid splint** | Wooden board, padded board, metal splint | Forearm, leg |
| **Anatomical splint** | Injured limb tied to the body (e.g., arm tied to chest) | Upper limb fractures |
| **Thomas splint** | Traction splint for femur fractures | Mid-shaft femur |
| **Air splint (Pneumatic)** | Inflatable plastic sleeve | Field use; provides uniform pressure |
| **Improvised splint** | Rolled newspaper, umbrella, walking stick, cardboard | Emergency / resource-limited settings |
| **Cravat / Triangular bandage** | Used to form a sling | Upper limb support |

#### **The S.T.O.P. Protocol (First-Aid Mnemonic)**

- **S** – Stop the activity, ensure scene safety
- **T** – Talk to the casualty; assess responsiveness and obtain consent
- **O** – Observe the injury; **look but do not move** (compare both sides)
- **P** – Prevent further harm: immobilise, cover wounds, treat for shock, arrange evacuation

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Quick-Reference Sheet

> There are **no mathematical formulas** in this topic. Instead, here is the **clinical formula sheet** the examiner expects you to memorise:

| Concept | First-Aid Action Formula |
|---|---|
| Any fracture | **Rest + Ice (cold) + Compression + Elevation** (RICE) — only for closed, non-complicated |
| Open fracture | **Sterile dressing + do NOT reduce + antibiotics at hospital + tetanus prophylaxis** |
| Splinting | **Joint above + fracture site + joint below** |
| Splint ties | **Tie above fracture + tie below fracture** (NOT over it) |
| Circulation check | **Colour + Warmth + Sensation + Movement (CSM)** of fingers/toes |
| Spinal suspicion | **Airway + C-spine immobilisation + log-roll with 3 assistants** |
| Shock management | **Recovery position, warmth, fluids only if conscious + no internal injury** |
| Traction rule | **Steady gentle pull along the long axis; STOP if pain increases** |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Occupational Significance

In Kerala's industrial belt (factories, ports, construction), fractures of the **forearm, wrist, femur, tibia, and ankle** dominate. The **Factory Act 1948** and **Kerala Factories Rules** mandate trained first-aiders on every shop floor. The **Workmen's Compensation Act** requires accurate first-aid documentation. A correctly applied splint prevents:
- **Laceration of nerves** (e.g., radial nerve in humeral shaft fractures)
- **Laceration of arteries** (e.g., brachial artery in supracondylar fractures)
- **Conversion of closed fracture to open** by bone spike piercing the skin
- **Fat embolism** by reducing marrow displacement

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Sprains, Strains & Dislocations

### 3.1 Sprain — Detailed Analytical Breakdown

**Definition:** A **sprain** is a stretching or tearing of **ligaments** — the fibrous bands that connect bone to bone and stabilise joints.

**Mechanism:** A joint is forced **beyond its normal range of motion** (e.g., foot twisting inward → lateral ankle ligament injury).

#### **Grading of Sprain (KTU High-Yield)**

| Grade | Pathology | Signs & Symptoms | First Aid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grade I (Mild)** | Stretching of ligament fibres, microscopic tears | Mild pain, swelling, no instability | RICE; rest 2–3 days |
| **Grade II (Moderate)** | Partial tear of ligament | Moderate pain, swelling, bruising, **mild instability** | RICE + immobilisation + medical review |
| **Grade III (Severe)** | Complete rupture of ligament | Severe pain, gross swelling, **definite instability**, inability to bear weight | Splint, do NOT bear weight, urgent orthopaedic referral |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory trick:** A sprain affects **ligaments** (bone↔bone); a **strain** affects **muscles/tendons** (muscle↔bone). The two are often confused in exams.

#### **First-Aid Steps for Sprain (R.I.C.E. + Beyond)**

1. **Rest** – Stop the activity; avoid movement that causes pain.
2. **Ice** – Apply cold pack (wrapped in cloth) for **15–20 minutes** every 2 hours for first 48 hours. Reduces swelling and pain.
3. **Compression** – Apply an elastic bandage from **distal to proximal** (toes → ankle), firm but not constricting.
4. **Elevation** – Raise the injured part **above the level of the heart** to reduce swelling by gravity.
5. **No HARM** in first 72 hours:
   - **H**eat
   - **A**lcohol
   - **R**unning (or any activity)
   - **M**assage

6. **Review by doctor** if pain/swelling persists beyond 48 hours, or if weight-bearing is impossible (possible fracture).

> [!NOTE]
> **In industry, ankle sprains** are the most common injury (40% of all workplace musculoskeletal injuries in many studies). Proper RICE application reduces lost workdays by 50%.

---

### 3.2 Strain — Brief Contrast

| Feature | Sprain | Strain |
|---|---|---|
| Tissue affected | Ligament | Muscle / Tendon |
| Cause | Joint forced beyond ROM | Overstretching or sudden contraction of muscle |
| Common site | Ankle, wrist, knee | Hamstring, calf, back |
| Pain location | Joint line | Belly of muscle or at tendon attachment |
| First aid | RICE | RICE + gentle stretch after 72 h |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write "sprain and strain are the same." Examiners deduct full marks. Always state: **sprain = ligament; strain = muscle/tendon**.

---

### 3.3 Dislocation — Detailed Analytical Breakdown

**Definition:** A **dislocation** (Latin *luxatio*) is the **complete displacement of a bone from its normal position at a joint**, such that the articular surfaces are **no longer in contact**. When the contact is only partially lost, it is called a **subluxation**.

**Mechanism:** Usually a **direct or indirect force** that levers the bone out of its socket, or a sudden muscle contraction (e.g., shoulder dislocation during a fall on an outstretched hand).

#### **Common Sites of Dislocation (KTU must-know)**

| Joint | Direction of Displacement | Frequency | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shoulder (Glenohumeral)** | Anterior (95%) | **Most common** | Fall on outstretched hand, throwing sports |
| **Elbow** | Posterior | Common in children | Fall on outstretched hand |
| **Hip** | Posterior | Severe trauma (RTA) | Dashboard injury |
| **Jaw (Temporo-mandibular)** | Anterior | Yawning, laughing, dental work | Mouth forced open |
| **Finger (interphalangeal)** | Dorsal | Ball sports | Axial load on fingertip |
| **Knee (Patella)** | Lateral | More in females | Direct blow or twist |
| **Ankle** | Variable | Often with fracture | Inversion injury |

#### **Clinical Signs of Dislocation (The 5 D's)**

- **D**eformity – Joint looks abnormal; visible bump or hollow
- **D**isplacement – Loss of normal alignment
- **D**olor – Severe pain
- **D**ysfunction – Inability to move the joint
- **D**istance – Limb may appear shorter or longer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** Always compare with the **opposite (uninjured) side** to detect deformity. Always check **distal circulation and sensation** before and after any manipulation.

#### **First-Aid Management of Dislocation (Step-by-Step)**

$$\text{Step 1: DANGER} \longrightarrow \text{Ensure scene safety, do not move casualty unnecessarily}$$

$$\text{Step 2: ASSESS} \longrightarrow \text{Check DR ABC — Danger, Response, Airway, Breathing, Circulation}$$

$$\text{Step 3: IMMOBILISE} \longrightarrow \text{Support limb in the position found (most comfortable)}$$

$$\text{Step 4: DO NOT REDUCE} \longrightarrow \text{NEVER attempt to put the bone back unless you are trained AND it is an emergency with no medical help available}$$

$$\text{Step 5: CHECK CSM} \longrightarrow \text{Circulation, Sensation, Movement distal to injury}$$

$$\text{Step 6: TREAT SHOCK} \longrightarrow \text{Warmth, reassurance, lying down with legs raised (if no spinal injury)}$$

$$\text{Step 7: HOSPITAL} \longrightarrow \text{Urgent transport for X-ray and closed reduction under anaesthesia}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall (Examiner's red flag):** Writing "first-aider should reduce the dislocation immediately." — **WRONG.** Reduction is a medical act. First-aider **immobilises in the position found** and evacuates.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Fracture vs. Sprain vs. Dislocation

| Feature | Fracture | Sprain | Dislocation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure involved** | Bone | Ligament | Joint (bone displaced) |
| **Mechanism** | Direct/indirect force beyond bone strength | Joint forced beyond ROM | Levering force at joint |
| **Deformity** | Present (angulation, shortening) | Usually absent | Marked (visible bump) |
| **Crepitus** | Present (bone-on-bone grating) | Absent | Usually absent (may be on movement) |
| **Swelling** | Moderate to severe | Mild to moderate | Rapid, marked |
| **Pain** | Severe, localised over fracture | Moderate, diffuse over joint | Severe, over joint |
| **Movement** | Loss (painful) | Painful but partially possible | Absolute loss |
| **First-aid** | Splint + RICE + hospital | RICE | Immobilise in position + hospital |
| **Definitive care** | Cast/plate/traction | Physiotherapy | Reduction under anaesthesia |
| **Complication if untreated** | Non-union, malunion, deformity | Chronic instability | Nerve/vessel damage, avascular necrosis |

---

### 3.5 Salient Differences in First-Aid Action

| Condition | RICE? | Splint? | Reduction? | Hospital Visit? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fracture (closed) | Yes (after splint) | **Yes** (essential) | No | **Yes** (mandatory) |
| Fracture (open) | No ice directly | **Yes** + sterile dressing | **No** | **Yes** (emergency) |
| Sprain Grade I | Yes | Optional | No | Optional |
| Sprain Grade II/III | Yes | **Yes** | No | **Yes** |
| Dislocation | No (just support) | **Yes** (in position) | **No** (by first-aider) | **Yes** (urgent) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Fractures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fracture: Break in bone continuity] --> B{Based on Cause}
    A --> C{Based on Communication with Outside}
    A --> D{Based on Completeness}
    A --> E{Based on Fracture Line Pattern}
    A --> F{Special Paediatric Types}

    B --> B1[Traumatic]
    B --> B2[Pathological]
    B --> B3[Stress or Fatigue]
    B --> B4[Avulsion]

    C --> C1[Closed or Simple]
    C --> C2[Open or Compound]

    D --> D1[Complete]
    D --> D2[Incomplete]

    D2 --> D2a[Greenstick]
    D2 --> D2b[Torus or Buckle]
    D2 --> D2c[Hairline]

    E --> E1[Transverse]
    E --> E2[Oblique]
    E --> E3[Spiral]
    E --> E4[Comminuted]
    E --> E5[Impacted]
    E --> E6[Depressed]
    E --> E7[Complicated]

    F --> F1[Salter Harris or Epiphyseal]
    F --> F2[Greenstick]
    F --> F3[Torus]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: First-Aid Action Plan for a Suspected Fracture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Scene arrives] --> S2[Ensure safety, wear gloves]
    S2 --> S3[Assess responsiveness]
    S3 --> S4{Is casualty conscious?}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Open airway, check breathing]
    S5 --> S6[Treat as life threatening, call ambulance]
    S4 -- Yes --> S7[Expose injury site gently]
    S7 --> S8{Look for open wound or bone protruding}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Open or Compound fracture]
    S9 --> S10[Cover with sterile dressing, do NOT push bone back]
    S8 -- No --> S11[Closed fracture suspected]
    S10 --> S12[Immobilise using splint above and below joint]
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S13[Check distal circulation, sensation, movement]
    S13 --> S14{Trauma to spine suspected}
    S14 -- Yes --> S15[Manual in line stabilisation, log roll with 3 helpers]
    S14 -- No --> S16[Treat for shock, keep warm, reassure]
    S16 --> S17[Arrange urgent transport to hospital]

    style S9 fill:#FF6347,color:#000
    style S11 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style S12 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style S15 fill:#FFA500,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Comparison Schematic: Sprain vs Strain vs Dislocation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Musculoskeletal Injury] --> B{Structure Injured}
    B -- Bone --> F1[Fracture]
    B -- Ligament --> F2[Sprain]
    B -- Muscle or Tendon --> F3[Strain]
    B -- Joint alignment lost --> F4[Dislocation]

    F1 --> A1[Immobilise, splint, hospital]
    F2 --> A2[RICE, hospital if severe]
    F3 --> A3[RICE, gentle stretch later]
    F4 --> A4[Support in position, hospital for reduction]

    style F1 fill:#FF6347,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style F3 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style F4 fill:#FFA500,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Anatomical Splinting Rule — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Fracture at midshaft of bone] --> P2[Identify joint above fracture]
    P1 --> P3[Identify joint below fracture]
    P2 --> P4[Immobilise joint above]
    P3 --> P5[Immobilise joint below]
    P4 --> P6[Splint now stabilises entire bone]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Pad splint and tie above and below]
    P7 --> P8[Check circulation sensation movement]
    P8 --> P9[Transport to hospital]

    style P6 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style P8 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** 
**Define a fracture. Differentiate between a closed (simple) fracture and an open (compound) fracture.**

**Model Answer:**

A fracture is defined as a break in the continuity of a bone, either complete or incomplete, caused by a force that exceeds the bone's structural strength. It may result from direct trauma, indirect force, twisting, or pathological weakening of bone.

| Feature | Closed (Simple) Fracture | Open (Compound) Fracture |
|---|---|---|
| Skin integrity | Skin is intact over the fracture site | Skin is broken; fracture communicates with outside |
| Bone exposure | Bone not visible | Bone may protrude through the wound |
| Risk of infection | Low | **High** (osteomyelitis, tetanus) |
| Bleeding | Internal haematoma | External haemorrhage |
| First aid | Splint and RICE | Sterile dressing, do NOT reduce, urgent hospital |

**Conclusion:** Open fractures are surgical emergencies due to infection risk; closed fractures still require hospital evaluation but have lower immediate infection risk.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** 
**List the principles of immobilisation in first aid for fractures.**

**Model Answer:**

The principles of immobilisation are:

1. **Do not move** the casualty until the part is supported, unless there is environmental danger.
2. **Steady and support** the injured part manually before and during splint application.
3. **Immobilise the joint above and the joint below** the fracture to prevent fragment displacement by muscle pull.
4. **Pad the splint** to prevent pressure on bony prominences.
5. **Tie bandages above and below** the fracture site — never directly over the fracture.
6. **Check distal circulation, sensation, and movement (CSM)** before and after splinting.
7. **Reassure the casualty** and arrange transport in the most comfortable position (usually lying down).

These principles minimise pain, prevent further damage to vessels and nerves, and reduce complications such as fat embolism and shock.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`** 
**(a)** Classify fractures in detail with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** 
**(b)** Explain the first-aid management of a suspected fracture of the femur at an industrial accident site. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification of Fractures (7 marks)**

Fractures can be classified in the following ways:

**(i) Based on Cause (Etiology):**
- **Traumatic fracture** – Caused by a single high-energy force (e.g., RTA, fall from height). Example: transverse fracture of tibia due to a direct blow.
- **Pathological fracture** – Occurs through bone weakened by disease (osteoporosis, osteomyelitis, bone cyst, metastasis) under trivial force.
- **Stress (fatigue) fracture** – Caused by repetitive sub-threshold loading; classic "march fracture" of metatarsals in soldiers.
- **Avulsion fracture** – A fragment of bone is torn off by a tendon or ligament under sudden tension (e.g., calcaneal tuberosity avulsion).

**(ii) Based on Communication with the Outside:**
- **Closed (simple) fracture** – Skin intact, e.g., greenstick fracture.
- **Open (compound) fracture** – Fracture communicates with the external environment through a wound. Higher infection risk.

**(iii) Based on Completeness:**
- **Complete fracture** – Bone broken all the way through; e.g., transverse, oblique, spiral.
- **Incomplete fracture** – Partial break; e.g., greenstick, torus (buckle), hairline.

**(iv) Based on Pattern of Fracture Line:**
- **Transverse** – Right-angle break (direct blow).
- **Oblique** – Angled break.
- **Spiral** – Corkscrew break (twisting force).
- **Comminuted** – Three or more fragments (high-energy trauma).
- **Impacted** – One fragment driven into another.
- **Depressed** – Bone pushed inward (skull).
- **Complicated** – Associated with injury to nerves, vessels, or viscera.

**(v) Special Paediatric Types:**
- **Greenstick** – One cortex breaks, the other bends.
- **Torus** – Buckling of cortex.
- **Salter-Harris** – Through growth plate.

---

**(b) First-Aid Management of Suspected Femur Fracture (7 marks)**

At an industrial accident site, the steps are:

**Step 1 — Scene Safety & PPE (1 mark):**
- Stop the machinery, isolate the hazard, wear gloves and eye protection.

**Step 2 — Primary Survey (DR ABC) (1 mark):**
- Check Danger, Response, Airway, Breathing, Circulation. Femur fracture can cause **1–1.5 litres of internal bleeding**; watch for shock.

**Step 3 — Call for Help / Ambulance (1 mark):**
- Activate emergency medical services; inform about suspected femur fracture.

**Step 4 — Support the Limb (1 mark):**
- Manually support the limb above and below the fracture site, in the position found. **Do NOT straighten** an obviously deformed femur.

**Step 5 — Immobilisation (2 marks):**
- Use a **Thomas splint** (if available) or improvised traction by tying the ankles together with legs straight and a long stick between them. **Immobilise the hip joint above and the knee joint below.**
- Pad the splint, tie above (groin) and below (knee) — never over the fracture.

**Step 6 — Check CSM (1 mark):**
- Check **Colour, Warmth, Sensation, Movement** of the toes. Document before and after splint.

**Step 7 — Treat for Shock and Evacuate (1 mark):**
- Lay casualty flat, cover with blanket, reassure, monitor breathing. Transport supine to a hospital with orthopaedic facilities.

---

**OR**

---

**Q3B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`** 
**(a)** Define sprain. Explain the grading of sprains and the RICE protocol. **(7 marks)** 
**(b)** Define dislocation. List the common sites of dislocation and describe the first-aid management of an anterior shoulder dislocation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Sprain — Definition, Grading & RICE Protocol (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** A sprain is the stretching or tearing of **ligaments** — the fibrous bands that connect bone to bone and stabilise a joint. It is caused when a joint is forced beyond its normal range of motion.

**Grading (3 marks):**

| Grade | Pathology | Signs & Symptoms |
|---|---|---|
| **Grade I – Mild** | Microscopic tearing of ligament fibres | Mild pain, minimal swelling, no instability |
| **Grade II – Moderate** | Partial tear of ligament | Moderate pain, swelling, bruising, **mild joint instability** |
| **Grade III – Severe** | Complete rupture of ligament | Severe pain, marked swelling, **definite instability**, inability to use the joint |

**RICE Protocol (3 marks):**
- **R – Rest:** Stop activity; avoid painful movement.
- **I – Ice:** Apply cold pack (wrapped) for 15–20 min every 2 hours for first 48 h; reduces swelling and pain.
- **C – Compression:** Elastic bandage, distal to proximal, firm but not tight.
- **E – Elevation:** Raise limb above heart level to reduce swelling.
- **No HARM** for 72 hours: no Heat, no Alcohol, no Running, no Massage.
- Refer to doctor if pain persists beyond 48 h or weight-bearing is impossible.

---

**(b) Dislocation — Definition, Sites & First Aid (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** A dislocation is the **complete displacement of a bone from its normal position at a joint**, such that the articular surfaces lose contact. Partial loss is a subluxation.

**Common Sites (3 marks):**

| Site | Direction | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Shoulder (Glenohumeral)** | Anterior (95%) | Most common; fall on outstretched hand |
| **Elbow** | Posterior | Fall on outstretched hand in children |
| **Hip** | Posterior | Dashboard injury in RTA |
| **TMJ (Jaw)** | Anterior | Wide yawning, dental procedures |
| **Finger joints** | Dorsal | Axial load on fingertip |
| **Patella** | Lateral | Direct blow or twist |
| **Ankle** | Variable | Inversion injury |

**First-Aid Management of Anterior Shoulder Dislocation (3 marks):**

1. **Scene safety** and PPE.
2. **Support the arm** in the position found using a pillow or sling. **Do NOT attempt to reduce** the dislocation.
3. Apply a **broad arm sling** (or triangular bandage) to immobilise the arm against the chest.
4. **Check CSM** of the fingers (axillary nerve and artery may be compromised).
5. **Treat for shock** — reassurance, warmth, lying down.
6. **Urgent transport** to a hospital for X-ray and closed reduction under anaesthesia.
7. Document mechanism, position, neurovascular status, and time of injury.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. Writing "**reduce the dislocation**" as a first-aid action — full marks lost. Only a doctor reduces a dislocation.
> 2. Confusing **sprain (ligament)** with **strain (muscle/tendon)** — 3-mark penalty minimum.
> 3. Saying "**massage and rub** the sprain" — wrong, massaging acute injuries worsens bleeding.
> 4. Splinting a fracture **over the fracture site** — wrong; tie above and below.
> 5. Forgetting to **check circulation after splinting** — full deduction on monitoring.
> 6. Writing "**greenstick fracture is complete**" — wrong; it is incomplete and paediatric.
> 7. Saying an **open fracture is less dangerous** than a closed fracture — dangerously wrong.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- **Fracture** = break in bone continuity; may be **complete or incomplete**.
- **Five classifications:** by cause, by communication (open/closed), by completeness, by fracture line pattern, paediatric special types.
- **Greenstick** = paediatric incomplete fracture; one cortex breaks, the other bends.
- **Comminuted** = ≥3 fragments; high-energy trauma.
- **Pathological** = fracture through diseased bone under trivial force.
- **Compound (Open) fracture** = surgical emergency; **sterile dressing + no reduction + hospital**.
- **Immobilisation rules:** support the part, immobilise **joint above + joint below**, pad the splint, **tie above and below** (NOT over the fracture), check **CSM** (Circulation, Sensation, Movement).
- **Splints:** rigid, anatomical, Thomas (traction), air, improvised.
- **Sprain = ligament; Strain = muscle/tendon.** Sprain grades: I, II, III.
- **RICE protocol:** Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation. **No HARM** for 72 h.
- **Dislocation = complete loss of joint contact.** Common sites: shoulder (anterior), elbow (posterior), hip, jaw, fingers.
- **5 D's of dislocation:** Deformity, Displacement, Dolor, Dysfunction, Distance change.
- **First-aider must NEVER reduce a dislocation** — immobilise in the position found and evacuate.
- **Femur fracture** can cause 1–1.5 L internal bleed → treat for shock; use Thomas splint or improvised traction.
- **Always compare** the injured side with the uninjured side; always check **distal CSM** before and after any splint.
- **Spinal suspicion** = manual in-line stabilisation + log-roll with 3 helpers.
- **KTU 2024 marks weight:** Part A 3-mark questions frequently test definitions and classifications; Part B 14-mark questions almost always combine classification (7 marks) with first-aid management (7 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Broad and Narrow Fold Bandages, Hand Bandages, Slings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Broad and Narrow Fold Bandages, Hand Bandages, Slings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bandages – Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **bandage** is a strip of woven material (cotton, gauze, elastic, or linen) used to cover a wound, hold a dressing in place, immobilize an injured limb, support a sprain, control bleeding through pressure, or restrict swelling. In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Occupational Health, Hygiene and First Aid*, bandages are classified by **width**, **function**, and **body region of application**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> A *fold bandage* is a roller bandage that has been folded lengthwise (longitudinally) to obtain a narrower, more controlled, and uniform pressure band. It is the most common form of roller bandage used in industrial first aid kits and workplace medical rooms.

### 1.2 Broad Fold Bandage

A **broad fold bandage** is a roller bandage whose folded width is **10 cm to 15 cm** (standard industrial first-aid specification). It is used to cover **large surface areas** of the body.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Applications of Broad Fold Bandages:**
> - Chest and back injuries
> - Thigh, groin, and hip regions
> - Abdomen (post-operative or post-trauma)
> - Axilla (armpit) and shoulder regions

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a broad fold bandage as the *bedsheet* of the first-aid world — you use it when you need to cover a large "surface area" like wrapping a rolled mattress. A small strip would tear or bunch up; you need width to distribute pressure evenly.

### 1.3 Narrow Fold Bandage

A **narrow fold bandage** is a roller bandage with a folded width of **2.5 cm to 5 cm**. It is designed for **small, contoured, or sensitive areas** where a broad bandage cannot conform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Applications of Narrow Fold Bandages:**
> - Fingers and toes
> - Eyes (monocular bandages)
> - Ears
> - Wrist and ankle joints
> - Scalp and forehead (as a tie-down for head dressings)

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a broad fold bandage as a *wide paint brush* and a narrow fold bandage as a *fine artist's brush* — the latter is used where precision, contour, and tight spaces are required, such as painting between window panes or wrapping a single finger.

### 1.4 Hand Bandages

A **hand bandage** is a specialized application pattern of a roller bandage used to dress the palm, dorsum, fingers, or thumb. Standard patterns include the **spiral**, **figure-of-eight**, and **recurrent (spica)** techniques.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A hand bandage is like wrapping a delicate, irregularly-shaped gift (the human hand) — you cannot simply wind a strip around it like a cylinder because of the thumb, fingers, and the palm's concavity. Specialized patterns mimic the natural anatomy to provide firm, comfortable support.

### 1.5 Slings

A **sling** is a triangular piece of cloth (or improvised substitute) suspended from the neck or shoulder to **support, immobilize, and elevate** an injured upper limb. Slings are *immobilization devices*, not pressure dressings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight – KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> The triangular bandage (90 cm × 90 cm × 127 cm) is the most commonly tested sling in KTU university examinations. Students must be able to demonstrate and name the **arm sling**, **elevation sling (St. John sling)**, and **improvised sling** using everyday materials.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A sling is a *hammock for an injured arm*. Just as a hammock supports a person without any floor contact, a sling holds the forearm/wrist without bearing weight, allowing gravity to gently pull the limb into a healing position.

---

### 1.6 Standard Bandage Width Chart (Industrial First-Aid Norms)

> [!NOTE]
