| 1 | Body Mass Index | $BMI = \dfrac{Weight}{Height^2}$ | $\text{kg/m}^2$ | Body Composition |
| 2 | Maximal Heart Rate (Tanaka) | $HR_{max} = 208 - 0.7 \cdot Age$ | bpm | Cardiorespiratory |
| 3 | Maximal Heart Rate (Fox) | $HR_{max} = 220 - Age$ | bpm | Cardiorespiratory |
| 4 | Target Heart Range | $THR = (HR_{max} - HR_{rest}) \cdot I\% + HR_{rest}$ | bpm | Exercise Prescription |
| 5 | Karvonen Reserve | $HRR = HR_{max} - HR_{rest}$ | bpm | Exercise Prescription |
| 6 | VO₂ Reserve (Swain) | $VO_2R = (VO_2max - VO_2rest) \cdot I\% + VO_2rest$ | $\text{ml/kg/min}$ | Cardiorespiratory |
| 7 | Waist-Hip Ratio (WHR) | $WHR = \dfrac{Waist\ Circumference}{Hip\ Circumference}$ | unitless | Body Composition |
| 8 | Resting Energy Expenditure (Harris-Benedict, Male) | $BMR = 66.5 + 13.75 \cdot W + 5.003 \cdot H - 6.755 \cdot A$ | kcal/day | Metabolic |
| 9 | One-Rep Max Estimator (Brzycki) | $1RM = \dfrac{Weight}{1.0278 - 0.0278 \cdot Reps}$ | kg | Muscular Strength |
| 10 | VO₂ Max from 1.5-Mile Run | $VO_2max = 88.02 - 3.716 \cdot T + 0.0193 \cdot T^2$ | $\text{ml/kg/min}$ | Field Testing |

Where in formula 8: $W$ = weight (kg), $H$ = height (cm), $A$ = age (years); and in formula 10: $T$ = time in minutes.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Occupational Utility

| Profession | Critical Fitness Component | Consequence of Deficiency |
|---|---|---|
| Construction Worker | Muscular Strength & Endurance | Lower back injury, falls |
| Firefighter | Cardiorespiratory Endurance | Heat stroke, exhaustion |
| Software Engineer | Flexibility, Postural Strength | Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI), cervical spondylitis |
| Hospital Nurse | Endurance + Agility | Fatigue, needlestick injuries |
| Industrial Operator | Reaction Time + Coordination | Machinery accidents |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Hook:** Occupational health professionals use fitness profiling to **match worker capacity to job demands**, a concept called **"Job Demand–Worker Capacity Matching"** which is frequently asked in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating Target Heart Rate

**Problem Statement (KTU Sample Style):**
*A 35-year-old male office worker has a resting heart rate of 72 bpm. He wants to exercise at 70% intensity using the Karvonen method. Calculate his Target Heart Rate (THR).*

### Step 1 — Calculate Maximal Heart Rate using Tanaka Formula

$$HR_{max} = 208 - (0.7 \times Age)$$

$$HR_{max} = 208 - (0.7 \times 35)$$

$$HR_{max} = 208 - 24.5$$

$$HR_{max} = 183.5 \text{ bpm}$$

**Logic:** The Tanaka equation provides the upper safety limit of cardiac contraction frequency. Beyond this, the myocardium becomes ischemic.

### Step 2 — Calculate Heart Rate Reserve (HRR) using Karvonen Principle

$$HRR = HR_{max} - HR_{rest}$$

$$HRR = 183.5 - 72$$

$$HRR = 111.5 \text{ bpm}$$

**Logic:** HRR represents the *available cardiac reserve* above resting state. Karvonen (1957) demonstrated this reserve is the true measure of training load, not absolute HR.

### Step 3 — Apply Karvonen Formula for THR

$$THR = (HRR \times I\%) + HR_{rest}$$

$$THR = (111.5 \times 0.70) + 72$$

$$THR = 78.05 + 72$$

$$THR = 150.05 \approx \mathbf{150 \text{ bpm}}$$

### Final Answer & Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The worker should maintain his heart rate around **150 bpm** during exercise to achieve 70% intensity training stimulus. This falls within the **ACSM-recommended aerobic training zone (64–76% HRR)**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — BMI Classification & Health Risk

**Problem Statement:**
*A 28-year-old female engineer weighs 78 kg and is 165 cm tall. Calculate her BMI and classify her health risk using WHO criteria.*

### Step 1 — Apply BMI Formula

$$BMI = \frac{Weight\ (kg)}{[Height\ (m)]^2}$$

$$BMI = \frac{78}{(1.65)^2}$$

$$BMI = \frac{78}{2.7225}$$

$$BMI = \mathbf{28.65 \text{ kg/m}^2}$$

### Step 2 — Classify Using WHO Standards

| BMI Range (kg/m²) | WHO Classification | Health Risk |
|---|---|---|
| < 18.5 | Underweight | Malnutrition risk |
| 18.5 – 24.9 | Normal | Low |
| 25.0 – 29.9 | **Overweight** | Moderate |
| 30.0 – 34.9 | Obese Class I | High |
| 35.0 – 39.9 | Obese Class II | Very High |
| ≥ 40.0 | Obese Class III | Extremely High |

**Classification:** The subject is in the **Overweight** category (BMI = 28.65).

### Step 3 — Occupational Implication

> [!WARNING]
> **Health Alert:** Excess body weight increases **mechanical load on joints**, predisposing to osteoarthritis. In occupational settings, overweight workers face **higher Workers' Compensation claims** for lifting injuries.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — 1-Rep Max Estimation for Muscular Strength

**Problem Statement:**
*An industrial worker can perform 8 repetitions of a bench press at 60 kg. Estimate his 1-Rep Max (1RM) using the Brzycki formula.*

### Step 1 — Apply Brzycki Equation

$$1RM = \frac{Weight}{1.0278 - 0.0278 \times Reps}$$

$$1RM = \frac{60}{1.0278 - (0.0278 \times 8)}$$

$$1RM = \frac{60}{1.0278 - 0.2224}$$

$$1RM = \frac{60}{0.8054}$$

$$1RM = \mathbf{74.49 \approx 74.5 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 2 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use:** Workplaces use 1RM to set safe lifting limits. The **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** for occupational lifting is typically **23 kg** under ideal conditions — a 1RM of 74.5 kg indicates the worker should never lift more than **~31% of 1RM** repeatedly in occupational tasks to avoid injury.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Aerobic vs Anaerobic Training

| Parameter | Aerobic Training | Anaerobic Training |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy System** | Oxidative phosphorylation | ATP-PCr, Glycolysis |
| **Duration** | > 5 minutes sustained | < 2 minutes |
| **Intensity** | 60–80% HRR | 85–100% HRR |
| **Primary Fuel** | Fats (lower intensity), Carbohydrates (higher) | Phosphocreatine, Glucose |
| **VO₂ Max Effect** | Marked increase | Minimal |
| **Muscle Hypertrophy** | Minimal | Marked |
| **Lactate Accumulation** | Low | High |
| **Best Tests** | 1.5-mile run, VO₂ max test | Wingate test, 100m sprint |
| **Occupational Use** | Endurance jobs (nursing, firefighting) | Power jobs (lifting, sprinting) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Physical Fitness Adaptation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resting Baseline State] --> B[Exercise Stimulus Application]
    B --> C{Stress Adequate?}
    C -->|Too Low| D[No Adaptation Plateau]
    C -->|Optimal| E[Alarm Phase - Acute Physiological Stress]
    C -->|Excessive| F[Overtraining Injury Risk]
    E --> G[Recovery Period with Rest and Nutrition]
    G --> H[Supercompensation Phase]
    H --> I[Improved Fitness Level]
    I --> J[New Baseline Established]
    J --> B
    D --> K[Modify Intensity Upward]
    K --> B
    F --> L[Reduce Load and Extend Recovery]
    L --> G
```

**Diagram Logic:** This flow represents the **Progressive Overload Cycle** as a closed-loop feedback system. Each iteration elevates the baseline fitness level.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Components of Physical Fitness (Hierarchical Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PF[Physical Fitness] --> HRFC[Health Related Components]
    PF --> SRFC[Skill Related Components]
    HRFC --> CRE[Cardiorespiratory Endurance]
    HRFC --> MS[Muscular Strength]
    HRFC --> ME[Muscular Endurance]
    HRFC --> FL[Flexibility]
    HRFC --> BC[Body Composition]
    SRFC --> AG[Agility]
    SRFC --> BL[Balance]
    SRFC --> CO[Coordination]
    SRFC --> PW[Power]
    SRFC --> RT[Reaction Time]
    SRFC --> SP[Speed]
    CRE --> CRE1[Heart Lung Efficiency VO2 Max]
    MS --> MS1[Maximal Force Output 1RM]
    ME --> ME1[Sustained Submaximal Effort]
    FL --> FL1[Joint Range of Motion ROM]
    BC --> BC1[Lean to Fat Ratio BMI WHR]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — The FITT Principle Application Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Frequency] --> P[Exercise Prescription Plan]
    I[Intensity] --> P
    T1[Time] --> P
    T2[Type] --> P
    P --> ASS[Assessment of Baseline Fitness]
    ASS --> SM[SMART Goal Setting]
    SM --> IMPL[Implementation Phase 4 to 6 Weeks]
    IMPL --> REV[Review and Reassessment]
    REV --> MOD[Modify FITT Variables]
    MOD --> P
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Occupational Fitness Demand Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    JOB[Job Task Analysis] --> DEM[Physical Demand Profile]
    DEM --> CRD[Cardiovascular Demand]
    DEM --> MSD[Musculoskeletal Demand]
    DEM --> FLD[Flexibility Posture Demand]
    DEM --> SPD[Speed Agility Demand]
    CRD --> MATCH[Worker Capacity Matching]
    MSD --> MATCH
    FLD --> MATCH
    SPD --> MATCH
    MATCH --> SAFE[Safe Work Assignment]
    MATCH --> TRAIN[Targeted Fitness Program]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Study Aid:** When asked to *"draw a diagram showing fitness components,"* the **hierarchical tree (Section 4.2)** is the examiner's most rewarded schematic. Use boxes and arrows in the answer script.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define physical fitness. List any four components of health-related fitness.**

**Model Answer:**

Physical fitness is a state of well-being that enables an individual to perform occupational, recreational, and daily activities without undue fatigue. It reflects the integrated functioning of cardiovascular, musculoskeletal, and metabolic systems.

The four **health-related fitness components** are:

1. **Cardiorespiratory Endurance** — capacity of heart, lungs, and blood vessels to deliver oxygen during sustained activity.
2. **Muscular Strength** — ability of a muscle to exert maximum force in a single effort.
3. **Flexibility** — range of motion available at a joint.
4. **Body Composition** — relative proportion of fat mass and lean body mass.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing *just the names* without one-line definitions loses 1 mark. Each component requires a **functional definition** for full credit.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the FITT principle in exercise prescription.**

**Model Answer:**

The **FITT Principle** is a guideline for designing safe and effective exercise programs. It stands for:

- **F – Frequency:** Number of exercise sessions per week (typically 3–5 days).
- **I – Intensity:** Difficulty level, often measured as percentage of target heart rate (60–80% HRR for aerobic training).
- **T – Time:** Duration of each session (20–60 minutes for aerobic exercise).
- **T – Type:** Mode of activity — aerobic (walking, cycling), anaerobic (sprinting, weightlifting), or flexibility (yoga, stretching).

This principle allows customization based on age, fitness level, and occupational demand.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — **14 Marks**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **five components of health-related physical fitness** with their occupational significance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **FITT principle** and calculate the **target heart rate** for a 40-year-old woman with a resting heart rate of 76 bpm exercising at 65% intensity. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Five Components of Health-Related Fitness (7 marks)

**1. Cardiorespiratory Endurance (CRE) — 1.5 marks**
- Defined as the ability of the circulatory and respiratory systems to supply oxygen during sustained physical activity.
- Measured by **VO₂ Max** (maximal oxygen uptake in ml/kg/min).
- **Occupational Significance:** Critical for firefighters, soldiers, nurses, construction workers — jobs requiring sustained exertion.

**2. Muscular Strength — 1.5 marks**
- Maximum force a muscle or muscle group can generate in a single contraction.
- Measured by **1-Rep Max (1RM)** testing.
- **Occupational Significance:** Essential for lifting, pushing, pulling tasks in warehousing, manufacturing, and emergency rescue.

**3. Muscular Endurance — 1 mark**
- Capacity of a muscle to sustain repeated contractions or hold a contraction over time.
- Measured by **push-up test**, **sit-up test**, or **plank hold duration**.
- **Occupational Significance:** Required for assembly line work, prolonged standing, repetitive manual handling.

**4. Flexibility — 1.5 marks**
- Range of motion (ROM) available at joints.
- Measured by **sit-and-reach test**, **goniometry**.
- **Occupational Significance:** Prevents musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs), back injuries, and repetitive strain injuries common in sedentary jobs.

**5. Body Composition — 1.5 marks**
- Relative proportion of **lean body mass (muscle, bone, water)** and **fat mass**.
- Measured by **BMI**, **skinfold calipers**, **bioelectrical impedance**.
- **Occupational Significance:** Excess body fat increases cardiovascular disease risk and reduces physical work capacity.

---

#### Part (b) — FITT Principle + THR Calculation (7 marks)

**FITT Principle Explanation (4 marks):**

| Component | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| **Frequency** | 3–5 days/week for moderate aerobic activity |
| **Intensity** | 40–60% HRR for beginners; 60–80% for trained |
| **Time** | 30–60 minutes per session |
| **Type** | Aerobic — walking, jogging, cycling, swimming |

**Target Heart Rate Calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate HR_max (Tanaka Formula):**

$$HR_{max} = 208 - (0.7 \times 40)$$

$$HR_{max} = 208 - 28 = \mathbf{180 \text{ bpm}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Heart Rate Reserve (HRR):**

$$HRR = HR_{max} - HR_{rest} = 180 - 76 = \mathbf{104 \text{ bpm}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Karvonen Formula:**

$$THR = (HRR \times 0.65) + HR_{rest}$$

$$THR = (104 \times 0.65) + 76$$

$$THR = 67.6 + 76$$

$$THR = \mathbf{143.6 \approx 144 \text{ bpm}}$$

**Conclusion:** The woman should train within **144 ± 5 bpm** to achieve 65% intensity safely.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — **14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **physiological principles of physical training** including Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **assessment methods** for cardiorespiratory endurance and muscular strength in an occupational health context. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Physiological Principles of Training (7 marks)

**1. Principle of Overload (1.5 marks):**
- For fitness improvement, the body must be stressed beyond its normal capacity.
- Achieved by increasing frequency, intensity, or duration of exercise.

**2. Principle of Progression (1 mark):**
- Overload must be **gradually increased** to prevent plateau and injury.
- Typical progression: 10% increase in load per week.

**3. Principle of Specificity (1 mark):**
- Training adaptations are **specific to the type of exercise** performed.
- A cyclist develops cycling-specific endurance; not swimming endurance.

**4. Principle of Reversibility (1 mark):**
- *"Use it or lose it"* — fitness gains are lost within 2–8 weeks of detraining.
- Critical for workers returning from sick leave or vacation.

**5. Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome (2.5 marks):**

- **Alarm Stage:** Initial physiological stress response; cortisol and adrenaline surge.
- **Resistance Stage:** Body supercompensates; cardiovascular, respiratory, muscular adaptations occur.
- **Exhaustion Stage:** Overtraining syndrome with chronic fatigue, immunosuppression, injury.

---

#### Part (b) — Assessment Methods (7 marks)

**Cardiorespiratory Endurance Tests (3.5 marks):**

| Test | Protocol | Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **VO₂ Max Lab Test** | Treadmill with gas analyzer | Direct oxygen uptake |
| **1.5-Mile Run Test** | Maximum effort run | Estimated VO₂ max |
| **Step Test** | 3-min stepping at fixed cadence | Recovery HR |
| **Astrand-Ryhming Cycle Test** | Submaximal cycling | Estimated VO₂ max |
| **Cooper 12-min Run Test** | Max distance in 12 min | Aerobic capacity |

**Muscular Strength Tests (3.5 marks):**

| Test | Target Muscle Group | Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Handgrip Dynamometer** | Forearm/Hand | Grip strength in kg |
| **1RM Bench Press** | Chest, Triceps | Max lift in kg |
| **Leg Press 1RM** | Quadriceps, Glutes | Max lift in kg |
| **Push-up Test (Max Reps)** | Upper body | Endurance & strength |
| **Plank Hold Test** | Core stability | Time in seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In FITT answers:** Students commonly forget to state **units** (bpm, minutes, sessions/week). Always specify units to earn full marks.
> 2. **In Calculation answers:** Skipping intermediate steps (HRR, HR_max) results in **partial credit loss** even if final answer is correct.
> 3. **In Definition answers:** Avoid colloquial phrases like *"being fit"* or *"healthy body"* — examiners expect **functional/physiological definitions**.
> 4. **In Diagram answers:** Label every box clearly; an unlabeled diagram gets **0 marks** regardless of effort.
> 5. **In Karvonen formula:** Writing *plain percentage of HR_max* instead of Karvonen (HRR method) loses 1–2 marks in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Physical fitness = state of well-being enabling performance of daily, occupational, and recreational activities without undue fatigue.
- **Two Domains:** Health-Related (5 components) and Skill-Related (6 components).
- **Five HRF Components:** Cardiorespiratory Endurance, Muscular Strength, Muscular Endurance, Flexibility, Body Composition.
- **Six SRF Components:** Agility, Balance, Coordination, Power, Reaction Time, Speed.
- **Tanaka Formula:** $HR_{max} = 208 - 0.7 \times Age$ *(preferred over Fox formula)*.
- **Karvonen Formula:** $THR = (HR_{max} - HR_{rest}) \times I\% + HR_{rest}$.
- **BMI Formula:** $BMI = W/H^2$ in $\text{kg/m}^2$; healthy range **18.5–24.9**.
- **Brzycki 1RM Formula:** $1RM = W / (1.0278 - 0.0278 \times Reps)$.
- **FITT Principle:** Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type — universally tested.
- **Selye's GAS:** Alarm → Resistance → Exhaustion (over-training risk).
- **Training Principles:** Overload, Progression, Specificity, Reversibility.
- **Occupational Hook:** Fitness must be **matched to job demands** to prevent MSDs, fatigue, and accidents.
- **VO₂ Max Benchmark:** ≥ 40 ml/kg/min (males), ≥ 36 ml/kg/min (females).
- **Healthy Resting HR:** 60–80 bpm; athlete range: 40–60 bpm.
- **WHO BMI Categories:** Underweight (<18.5), Normal (18.5–24.9), Overweight (25–29.9), Obese (≥30).
- **Always include units** in calculation answers — examiners deduct marks for missing units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lifting and Casualty Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lifting and Casualty Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Casualty Handling** is the disciplined, biomechanically-correct method of moving an injured or unconscious person from the site of injury to a place of safety or definitive medical care, without aggravating the existing injuries. **Lifting** is the specific subset of casualty handling that deals with raising a casualty clear of the ground (vertically) using the rescuer's body mechanics, lever systems, and team coordination.

According to standard occupational first-aid and KTU 2024 syllabus literature, casualty handling and lifting form the **"Movement Phase"** of the emergency response protocol — which sits between *Primary Survey (DR-ABCDE)* and *Definitive Care (Hospital Transfer)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The board examiner expects the student to clearly distinguish between **Emergency Moves** (performed when there is *immediate danger* — fire, explosion, toxic atmosphere) and **Non-Emergency Moves** (performed when the scene is safe and the casualty can be moved carefully). Confusing the two is a guaranteed mark-loser.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just bought a brand-new flat-pack **LCD television** from a shop. You do NOT lift it by the thin corner, drag it across the floor, or carry it alone with one hand stretched out. Instead:
- You read the **manual** (assessment),
- You **bend your knees, keep it close to your body, and lift with your legs** (biomechanics),
- You ask a friend to help carry the **bottom** while you support the **top** (team lift),
- You use the **original packaging** (improvised stretcher), and
- You move slowly on a **clear path** (planned route).

A casualty is a far more precious, fragile, and *irreplaceable* "package" than a television. The principles of lifting a TV are the principles of lifting a human being — except a human can also be in **pain, shock, spinal injury, or unconsciousness**, making the rescuer's job exponentially harder.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Definitions)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Casualty** | A person who has been injured or has fallen ill and requires medical assistance. |
| **Rescuer** | The trained first-aider performing the lift/handling. |
| **Spinal Cord Injury (SCI) Risk** | A casualty whose mechanism of injury (fall, dive, road accident) suggests possible vertebral damage — must be moved with **in-line immobilization**. |
| **Center of Gravity (CoG)** | The point in the body (roughly at the **S2 vertebra / umbilicus level**) where the body's mass is equally distributed in all directions. |
| **Base of Support (BoS)** | The area beneath the rescuer bounded by the feet and the line between them. **BoS must always be wider than the shoulders** during a lift. |
| **In-line Stabilization** | Manual support of the head and neck so the spinal column moves as a single rigid unit. |
| **Log Roll** | Rolling the casualty as a single unit (head, shoulders, hips turn together) to inspect the back or place a board beneath. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:**
> The **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** for a one-person manual lift under ideal conditions is **23 kg (≈ 51 lbs)**. A rescuer should ideally not lift more than this, but in casualty handling, the casualty's weight is *non-negotiable* — which is why **team lifts** and **leverage** are used.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Biomechanics of a Safe Lift (The "Golden Rules")

When the human body lifts a load, three forces must be balanced: the **load** (casualty), the **rescuer's muscular effort**, and the **ground reaction force**. The KTU board expects you to know the following six golden rules:

### Rule 1 — Know Your Capability, Know the Load
- Assess the **casualty's approximate weight** (look, ask, observe clothing/physique).
- Estimate the **distance** to be moved.
- Identify any **obstacles** (stairs, narrow doors, wires on ground).
- Check for **spinal injury suspicion** — if present, choose a log-roll-with-3-rescuers technique, NOT a fireman's lift.

### Rule 2 — Bend the Knees, Not the Waist
- Squat down by flexing the **hip, knee, and ankle joints**.
- Keep the **back straight** (slight lordosis maintained).
- Power comes from the **quadriceps and gluteal muscles**, not the erector spinae of the back.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:**
> The lumbar disc pressure when lifting with **straight legs and bent back** is approximately **8–10 times** greater than when lifting with **bent knees and straight back**. This is why an untrained rescuer often throws out their back while "helping" a fallen person.

### Rule 3 — Keep the Load Close
- The casualty should be **held as close to the rescuer's CoG** (umbilicus) as possible.
- Every additional **10 cm** of horizontal distance multiplies the effective torque on the lumbar spine dramatically.

### Rule 4 — Wide Base of Support
- Feet should be **shoulder-width apart**, one foot slightly ahead.
- This creates a **tripod base** and prevents lateral falls.

### Rule 5 — Lift Smoothly — No Jerks
- Use **continuous, controlled motion**.
- Jerking converts kinetic energy into impact force on the spine.
- Communicate with the casualty: *"Lift on three. One, two, THREE — lift."*

### Rule 6 — Never Twist the Trunk
- **Move the feet, not the spine**, to change direction.
- Twisting under load causes **disc shear injury** — a common occupational hazard for first-aiders.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Principle | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Torque on Lumbar Spine | $\tau = F \times d$ | $F$ = effective load, $d$ = horizontal distance from CoG to spine | **Halving $d$ halves the spinal torque** — keep the casualty close. |
| Safe Weight Limit (Niosh) | $\text{RWL} = 23 \text{ kg (ideal)}$ | Constant | Maximum one-person manual lift; for >23 kg, use team lift. |
| Mechanical Advantage (Lever) | $\text{MA} = \dfrac{L_e}{L_r}$ | $L_e$ = effort arm, $L_r$ = resistance arm | A long lever + short effort arm gives mechanical advantage (e.g., using a pole as a stretcher handle). |
| Two-Person Load Share | $F_{each} = \dfrac{W}{2}$ | $W$ = total casualty weight | In an ideal two-person lift, each rescuer bears **half the weight**. |
| Three-Person Load Share | $F_{each} = \dfrac{W}{3}$ | $W$ = total weight | Used for log-roll and supine lift with spinal precaution. |
| Newton's Second Law (Acceleration) | $F = m \times a$ | $m$ = mass, $a$ = acceleration | **$a = 0$** in a smooth lift — no acceleration means minimum peak force. |
| Pressure on Lumbar Disc | $P \propto F \times \dfrac{d}{A_{disc}}$ | $F$ = force, $d$ = distance, $A_{disc}$ = disc area | Wrong posture multiplies $P$ by 8–10×. |
| In-line Stabilization Condition | $\sum M_{neck} = 0$ | Net moment at neck must be zero | No relative motion allowed between C1–C7 during movement. |
| Estimated Casualty Weight | $W_{est} \approx W_{rescuer} \times \dfrac{V_{casualty}}{V_{rescuer}}$ | Volume-based estimation | Visual volume comparison if weight unknown. |

> [!NOTE]
> The **sum of moments (torques)** acting on a rescuer's lumbar spine must be **zero in equilibrium** during the steady-state of a hold. If you extend your arms, you create a non-zero moment that the erector spinae must counter — leading to fatigue and injury.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Occupational Health

In an industrial plant, a fainting worker on a mezzanine floor, a chemical burn victim in a confined space, or a fall-from-height casualty on scaffolding — **all** require casualty handling. The **Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code (OSH Code), 2020** of India, and the **Factory Act 1948 (amended)** both mandate that every factory must have trained first-aiders who can *competently* lift and move casualties. Loss of marks in this topic means loss of life in the field.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Techniques and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pre-Lift Decision Algorithm (Analytical Walkthrough)

Before selecting a lift, the rescuer must follow this analytical sequence:

### Step 1 — Scene Safety Survey
1. Check for **hazard** (fire, smoke, electricity, traffic, toxic gas).
2. Don **PPE** (gloves, mask, eye protection).
3. Identify the **number of rescuers** available.

### Step 2 — Primary Survey (DR-ABCDE)
1. **D**anger
2. **R**esponse — tap shoulders, shout *"Are you okay?"*
3. **A**irway — open with jaw-thrust (if suspected SCI) or head-tilt-chin-lift.
4. **B**reathing — look, listen, feel (10 seconds).
5. **C**irculation — check pulse, control hemorrhage.
6. **D**isability — pupil response, limb movement.
7. **E**xpose — examine for injuries.

### Step 3 — Decision: Emergency Move or Non-Emergency Move?

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases}
\text{Emergency Move} & \text{if scene is unsafe OR casualty's life at immediate risk} \\
\text{Non-Emergency Move} & \text{if scene is safe AND primary survey complete}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Spinal Precaution Check
If the mechanism of injury (MOI) is **fall, road accident, dive into shallow water, electric shock, or blow to head**, **assume SCI** and use **in-line stabilization + log-roll** techniques.

### Step 5 — Select the Appropriate Lift
Use the **decision matrix** below.

## 3.2 The Master Lift-Selection Matrix (KTU Board-Favourite Table)

| Casualty Condition | No. of Rescuers | Distance | Recommended Lift |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conscious, ambulatory, minor injury | 1 | Short | **Human Crutch / Single Rescuer Assist** |
| Unconscious, no SCI suspicion | 1 | Short | **Cradle Carry (Piggy-back)** |
| Unconscious, no SCI suspicion | 1 | Medium | **Fireman's Lift** |
| Unconscious, suspected SCI | 2–3 | Any | **Log Roll + Spinal Board** |
| Conscious, cannot walk | 2 | Short-Medium | **Two-Handed Seat Carry** |
| Conscious or unconscious, no SCI | 2 | Long | **Improvised Stretcher (Blanket/Cootie)** |
| Mass casualty incident | 4+ | Long | **Stretcher Carry (4-hand rule)** |

## 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step Lift Procedures

### Technique 1 — Human Crutch (Single Rescuer Assist)
*For conscious casualty who can partially bear weight.*

1. Stand on the casualty's **injured side**.
2. Reach across and place the casualty's **near arm over your shoulder**.
3. Hold the casualty's **wrist** with your **outer hand**.
4. Place your **inner arm around the casualty's waist**.
5. Move together, with the casualty stepping in rhythm with you.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomechanical insight:** The rescuer's inner arm acts as a **class 3 lever** with the fulcrum at the elbow — small effort, large support range. This is highly energy-efficient for the rescuer.

### Technique 2 — Cradle Carry (Piggy-Back)
*For conscious child or lightweight unconscious adult over short distance.*

1. Stand in front of the casualty (casualty upright).
2. Bend forward slightly and grasp the casualty's **wrists**.
3. Drape the casualty's arms over your shoulders.
4. Lift by straightening the legs, pulling the casualty onto your back.
5. Pass your arms under the casualty's thighs from behind.
6. Hold the wrists crossed in front of your chest.
7. Walk forward.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Many students write *"pick up the casualty"* without mentioning the **direction of force** or the **leg-driven lift**. A correct answer should state: *"Lift by extension of knee and hip joints while keeping the back straight."*

### Technique 3 — Fireman's Lift
*For unconscious/limp adult, no spinal injury, one rescuer, medium distance.*

1. Position the casualty **face-up on the ground**.
2. Stand at the **head end**, facing the casualty's feet.
3. Reach down, grasp the casualty's **right wrist** with your **right hand** (mirror for left).
4. Roll the casualty **towards you onto their side**, then onto their abdomen (recovery position intermediate).
5. Step over the casualty's body so they lie **face-down between your legs**.
6. Bend at the knees, pass the casualty's right arm **over your head and across your shoulder** (so the wrist dangles over your chest).
7. Reach between the casualty's legs and grasp their **right thigh**.
8. Stand up, distributing the weight on your shoulders.
9. **Free hand** can be used to hold the dangling wrist for balance.
10. Walk in a measured, heel-to-toe gait.

### Technique 4 — Two-Handed Seat Carry
*For two rescuers, conscious casualty who cannot walk.*

1. Two rescuers face each other, squatting on either side of the casualty.
2. Each rescuer passes their **outer arm behind the casualty's back** to grasp the other rescuer's shoulder.
3. Each rescuer passes their **inner arm under the casualty's thighs**, gripping the other rescuer's wrist.
4. On the command *"Lift"*, both stand simultaneously using the legs.
5. Both rescuers walk in **step**, breaking step only to go through doorways.

$$
F_{each} = \frac{W_{casualty}}{2} \quad \text{(ideal case, no bending moment)}
$$

### Technique 5 — Three-Person Lift (with Spinal Precaution)
*For suspected SCI casualty, conscious or unconscious, who must be moved onto a spinal board.*

1. **Rescuer 1 (Commander)** — kneels at the head, hands cradling the skull (heels of palms on mastoid, fingers along jaw line). Maintains in-line stabilization.
2. **Rescuer 2** — kneels at the **shoulders/chest**, hands on the far shoulder and near hip.
3. **Rescuer 3** — kneels at the **hips/knees**, hands on the far hip and near ankle.
4. On command *"Ready — BRACE — ROLL"*, all three turn the casualty **as a single log** towards Rescuer 2 and 3.
5. Rescuer 1 inspects and palpates the spine.
6. A spinal board is slid under the casualty.
7. On command *"Ready — ROLL BACK"*, the casualty is lowered onto the board.
8. Rescuer 1 maintains in-line stabilization **throughout** — only released after the head is secured with head-blocks and straps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Concept:**
> The command sequence **"Ready — Brace — Roll"** ensures *simultaneous movement*. Asynchronous movement (e.g., Rescuer 3 lifting before Rescuer 1 stabilizes) will cause **spinal shear** and permanent paralysis. Board examiners mark this strictly.

### Technique 6 — Improvised Stretcher (Blanket Stretcher / "Cootie")
*When no commercial stretcher is available.*

1. Spread a **blanket or strong cloth** on the ground.
2. Place a **rigid pole** (or two) along the long edge, leaving ~30 cm protruding on each end for handles.
3. Fold the blanket **over the pole** to encase it; a second pole is placed on the folded edge and the blanket is folded over it again.
4. Two rescuers, one at each end, **test the stretcher** with an empty lift first.
5. The casualty is log-rolled onto the stretcher (3-person technique).
6. Both rescuers lift together on the count of three.
7. The casualty is carried **feet-first** (to allow the head-end rescuer to monitor the face and airway).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The folded blanket creates a **truss-like structure** where the poles bear the tensile load and the cloth bears the compressive load — similar in principle to a **pre-stressed concrete beam** or a **tensile fabric roof**.

### Technique 7 — Drag Methods (Emergency Moves Only)
*Used ONLY when there is immediate danger and no time for a lift.*

1. **Clothing Drag** — grasp the casualty's shirt collar, drag head-first; protect the head with your forearms.
2. **Blanket Drag** — roll the casualty onto a blanket, drag by the head end.
3. **Foot Drag** — grasp the ankles, drag feet-first; only suitable for heavy casualties on flat ground.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Trap:**
> Drag methods are **contra-indicated** in suspected SCI. A drag will cause the body to bend at the waist, and the spine will flex — risking permanent cord damage. Students who recommend a drag for an SCI casualty will **lose 4–5 marks immediately**.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Two-Person Seat Carry

**Problem:** A 72 kg male worker has fainted on a workshop floor (no spinal injury). Two rescuers of equal strength are available. Calculate the load borne by each rescuer and the torque at the lumbar spine if the casualty is held 20 cm away from the rescuer's umbilicus.

**Given:**
- $W = 72$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s² → $F = 72 \times 9.81 = 706.32$ N
- $d = 20$ cm $= 0.20$ m
- Two rescuers, equally loaded.

**Step 1 — Force per rescuer**
$$F_{each} = \frac{W}{2} = \frac{72}{2} = 36 \text{ kg}$$
$$F_{each} = 36 \times 9.81 = 353.16 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Torque at lumbar spine (per rescuer)**
$$\tau = F_{each} \times d = 353.16 \times 0.20 = 70.63 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with one-rescuer scenario**
If the same casualty were lifted by **one rescuer** at the same 0.20 m distance:
$$F_{1} = 706.32 \text{ N}$$
$$\tau_{1} = 706.32 \times 0.20 = 141.26 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Reduction factor**
$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{\tau_{1} - \tau_{2}}{\tau_{1}} \times 100\% = \frac{141.26 - 70.63}{141.26} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Conclusion:** A two-person seat carry **halves the spinal torque** compared to a one-person carry at the same horizontal distance, which is why the two-handed seat carry is the **default** for two-rescuer teams in non-SCI casualties.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Decision Flow — Choosing the Correct Lift

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Casualty Found] --> B{Scene Safe?}
    B -- No --> C[EMERGENCY MOVE - Drag Method]
    B -- Yes --> D{Primary Survey - DR-ABCDE}
    D --> E{Spinal Injury Suspected?}
    E -- Yes --> F[In-line Stabilization]
    F --> G[3-Person Log Roll]
    G --> H[Spinal Board / Hard Stretcher]
    E -- No --> I{Casualty Conscious?}
    I -- Yes --> J{Can Walk?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Human Crutch / Single Assist]
    J -- No --> L[2-Handed Seat Carry]
    I -- No --> M{One Rescuer Available?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Fireman Lift or Cradle Carry]
    M -- No --> O[2-Person Seat Carry or Stretcher]
    C --> P[Move to Safety]
    H --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Secondary Survey at Safe Location]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Two-Person Seat Carry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph rescuer1 [RESCUER 1 - LEFT SIDE]
        r1leg1[Feet Shoulder Width]
        r1leg2[Bent Knees]
        r1back1[Straight Back]
        r1arm1[Outer Arm Behind Casualty Back]
        r1arm2[Inner Arm Under Thighs]
    end
    subgraph casualty [CASUALTY - SEATED]
        casback[Back Supported]
        casarm1[Right Arm on R1 Shoulder]
        casarm2[Left Arm on R2 Shoulder]
        casleg[Thighs Supported by Inner Arms]
    end
    subgraph rescuer2 [RESCUER 2 - RIGHT SIDE]
        r2leg1[Feet Shoulder Width]
        r2leg2[Bent Knees]
        r2back1[Straight Back]
        r2arm1[Outer Arm Behind Casualty Back]
        r2arm2[Inner Arm Under Thighs]
    end
    r1arm1 -. supports .-> casback
    r2arm1 -. supports .-> casback
    r1arm2 -. supports .-> casleg
    r2arm2 -. supports .-> casleg
    r1arm1 <--> r2arm1
    r1arm2 <--> r2arm2
```

## 4.3 Three-Person Log-Roll — Functional Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[3 Rescuers in Position] --> comm1[Commander Calls READY]
    comm1 --> comm2[Commander Calls BRACE]
    comm2 --> comm3[Commander Calls ROLL]
    comm3 --> roll[All 3 Rescuers Rotate Casualty as Single Log]
    roll --> insp[Rescuer 1 Inspects Back]
    insp --> board[Rescuer 2 and 3 Slide Spinal Board]
    board --> comm4[Commander Calls ROLL BACK]
    comm4 --> lower[Lower Casualty onto Board]
    lower --> secure[Strap Head, Chest, Pelvis, Legs]
    secure --> done[Ready for Transport]
```

## 4.4 Force Distribution in a Two-Person Lift (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Casualty Weight 72 kg] --> split{Load Splits Equally}
    split --> F1[Rescuer 1 carries 36 kg]
    split --> F2[Rescuer 2 carries 36 kg]
    F1 --> T1[Torque at R1 Spine 70.63 Nm]
    F2 --> T2[Torque at R2 Spine 70.63 Nm]
    T1 --> compare{Compare to 1-Person Lift}
    T2 --> compare
    compare --> result[Spinal Torque Reduced by 50%]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**List any six golden rules of safe lifting in casualty handling.**

**Model Answer (6 points, 3 marks):**

1. Assess the casualty and the environment before lifting (Rule of Assessment).
2. Bend the **knees**, not the waist — keep the back straight.
3. Keep the casualty **close** to the rescuer's center of gravity.
4. Maintain a **wide base of support** (feet shoulder-width apart).
5. Lift **smoothly** — no jerks; communicate with the casualty.
6. **Never twist** the trunk; move the feet to change direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A 3-mark question expects **3–4 points with brief explanation**. Writing all six with one-line descriptions will fetch **full marks**. Writing only the names without the "why" will cap you at 2 marks.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between an Emergency Move and a Non-Emergency Move in casualty handling.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Emergency Move | Non-Emergency Move |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Immediate danger to rescuer or casualty (fire, explosion, toxic gas, structural collapse). | Scene is safe; no time pressure. |
| **Spinal Precaution** | Usually **disregarded** (life over limb). | Strict **in-line stabilization** maintained. |
| **Speed** | Rapid, seconds matter. | Slow, methodical, planned. |
| **Examples** | Clothing drag, blanket drag, foot drag. | Cradle carry, two-handed seat carry, log-roll on spinal board. |
| **Documentation** | Documented after the event with rationale. | Documented step-by-step. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — ANSWER ANY ONE: Q-A OR Q-B]**

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] — **14 Marks**

**Describe in detail the Three-Person Log Roll technique used in casualty handling for a suspected spinal injury. Mention the roles of each rescuer, the command sequence, and the biomechanical principles involved.**

#### (a) Roles of the Three Rescuers (7 Marks)

1. **Rescuer 1 — Team Leader / Head-End Commander (In-line Stabilizer):**
   - Position: Kneels at the **top of the casualty's head**, facing the casualty's feet.
   - Hands: **Heels of palms** on the casualty's **mastoid processes** (behind the ears), fingers along the **mandible** (jaw line).
   - Function: Maintains the head and neck in a **neutral, in-line position** so that the cervical spine does not flex, extend, or rotate. This rescuer **never lets go** until the head is fully immobilized on the spinal board with head-blocks and forehead/chin straps.

2. **Rescuer 2 — Shoulder and Chest:**
   - Position: Kneels on one side of the casualty, level with the **shoulder**.
   - Hands: One hand on the **far shoulder**, one hand on the **near hip**.
   - Function: Initiates and controls the **roll** at the upper torso, ensuring the shoulder girdle moves as a unit with the spine.

3. **Rescuer 3 — Hip and Legs:**
   - Position: Kneels on the same side as Rescuer 2, level with the **pelvis/hips**.
   - Hands: One hand on the **far hip**, one hand on the **near ankle** (or under the far knee).
   - Function: Initiates and controls the **roll** at the lower torso and legs, keeping the pelvis and lower limbs in the same plane as the upper body.

> All three rescuers must perform the roll **simultaneously** (within 1 second of each other) to avoid relative motion between body segments.

#### (b) Command Sequence and Biomechanical Principles (7 Marks)

**Command Sequence (the "Three-Word" Rule):**

1. *"READY"* — all three rescuers confirm hand placement and posture.
2. *"BRACE"* — rescuers tense their core muscles and prepare to exert force.
3. *"ROLL"* — the casualty is rotated **45° towards Rescuer 2 and 3** as a single log.

**Biomechanical Principles Applied:**

1. **Rigid Body Assumption:** The body is treated as a rigid body, so the head, shoulders, and hips must rotate as a **single unit**. Any differential rotation will cause a bending moment in the spine.

2. **Torque Equilibrium at the Neck:**
   $$\sum M_{neck} = 0 \implies F_{Rescuer1} \times d_{skull} = F_{spasm} \times d_{muscle}$$
   Rescuer 1's hands counteract the cervical muscle tone and any external force to keep the head **static** relative to the shoulders.

3. **Friction and Lever Arms:** The rescuer's hands should be placed **as far apart as possible** on the body (far shoulder + near hip) to maximize the **resistance arm** and reduce the **force required per hand**:
   $$\text{Required Force} \propto \frac{1}{\text{arm length}}$$

4. **Center of Gravity Alignment:** The log-roll keeps the body's CoG moving along a smooth arc, avoiding any sudden change in momentum ($F = ma$; if $a$ spikes, so does $F$).

5. **After the Roll:** A spinal board is slid under the casualty at **30° to 45°** to the body, and the command *"ROLL BACK"* lowers the casualty onto the board. Straps are applied at the **chest, pelvis, and legs**, and head-blocks are placed before Rescuer 1 lets go.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice] — **14 Marks**

**With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the procedure of the Fireman's Lift. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and contra-indications.**

#### (a) Step-by-Step Procedure of the Fireman's Lift (7 Marks)

**Preconditions:**
- Casualty is **unconscious or unable to walk**.
- **No suspected spinal injury.**
- Single rescuer, **medium distance** (< 100 m).

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Initial Positioning:** Casualty lies **supine** (face-up) on the ground. Rescuer stands at the **head end**, facing the casualty's feet.

2. **Wrist Grasp:** Rescuer grasps the casualty's **right wrist** with their **right hand**. (For a mirror image, use left-left.)

3. **Rolling to Prone:** Pull the wrist, rolling the casualty **towards the rescuer** so the casualty ends up **prone** (face-down) with the rescuer standing over them.

4. **Step-Over:** Rescuer **straddles** the casualty, feet on either side of the torso.

5. **Shoulder Hoist:** Pass the casualty's right arm over the rescuer's **right shoulder**, so the wrist dangles over the rescuer's **chest**.

6. **Thigh Grasp:** Reach down between the casualty's legs and grasp the **right thigh** firmly from the inner side.

7. **Lift:** Stand up using the **legs** (not the back), lifting the casualty onto the shoulders. The casualty's abdomen rests across the rescuer's shoulders; the thighs are held at the rescuer's side; the dangling wrist is held in the rescuer's free (left) hand for **balance**.

8. **Walk:** Proceed in a steady **heel-to-toe** gait, watching the path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomechanical Note:** The rescuer's shoulder acts as the **fulcrum** of a **first-class lever**. The casualty's torso (load) is on one side, and the rescuer's free hand pulling the dangling wrist (effort) is on the other. By adjusting the effort arm, the rescuer can balance the load dynamically.

#### (b) Advantages, Limitations, and Contra-indications (7 Marks)

**Advantages:**

1. **Single-rescuer capability** — no team required.
2. **Casualty's head is supported** at the rescuer's chest, allowing the rescuer to monitor the airway continuously.
3. **Hands are free** (one to grasp thigh, one to balance via the wrist) — so the rescuer can use a handrail or open a door.
4. **Suitable for medium distances** (up to ~100 m on flat ground).
5. **Casualty's spine is in slight extension** — generally safe if no SCI is suspected.

**Limitations:**

1. **Physically exhausting** — burns ~400–500 kcal/hour; not feasible for long distances.
2. **Rescuer's back is loaded** — risk of lumbar injury if technique is wrong.
3. **Cannot be used on conscious, cooperative casualties** — they will resist the position.
4. **Requires a "limp" casualty** — a struggling or convulsing casualty cannot be fireman-lifted.
5. **Difficult to perform on casualties much heavier** than the rescuer (> 90 kg for an average rescuer).

**Contra-indications (DO NOT use Fireman's Lift in these cases):**

1. **Suspected spinal cord injury** — the lift will flex the spine and risk paralysis.
2. **Suspected pelvic fracture** — the thigh grasp will displace fragments.
3. **Suspected abdominal injury / internal bleeding** — the position compresses the abdomen and may worsen hemorrhage.
4. **Suspected rib fractures with flail segment** — the prone position and shoulder pressure will worsen respiratory compromise.
5. **Obesity or pregnancy** — the rescuer cannot safely balance the load.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Zone:**
> Students frequently **omit the contra-indications** and lose 2–3 marks. Equally, students write *"the fireman's lift is bad — don't use it"* without understanding that it remains a **valid, taught technique** for non-SCI, unconscious casualties. The examiner is looking for a **balanced answer** covering both utility and limitations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Always survey the scene first** — your safety is the prerequisite for the casualty's safety.
- [ ] **Conduct DR-ABCDE primary survey** before deciding to move.
- [ ] **Differentiate Emergency Move (life over limb)** from **Non-Emergency Move (spine first)**.
- [ ] **Assume spinal injury** in all high-energy mechanisms (fall, road accident, dive, electric shock).
- [ ] **Bend knees, keep back straight, hold load close, wide base, smooth lift, no twisting** — the six golden rules.
- [ ] **One-person lifts** — Human Crutch, Cradle Carry, Fireman's Lift. **Max 23 kg ideal**.
- [ ] **Two-person lifts** — Two-Handed Seat Carry (conscious, no SCI); Stretcher Carry (longer distance).
- [ ] **Three-person lifts** — Log Roll for suspected SCI; spinal board is mandatory.
- [ ] **Drag methods** (clothing, blanket, foot) are **emergency-only**; contra-indicated in SCI.
- [ ] **Improvised stretchers** can be made from blankets, coats, doors, or poles + cloth.
- [ ] **Command sequence for team lifts** is *"Ready — Brace — Roll"* (or *"Lift on three"*).
- [ ] **Carry feet-first** so the head-end rescuer can monitor the airway continuously.
- [ ] **Rescuer 1 (head-end) never lets go** of the head in SCI cases until the head is fully strapped.
- [ ] **Mechanical advantage of a team lift** reduces spinal torque **proportionally to the number of rescuers** (e.g., 2-person seat carry = 50% reduction).
- [ ] **Document the lift technique used** — this is a legal and clinical requirement.
- [ ] **Personal protective equipment (PPE)** — wear gloves; casualty may have blood or body fluids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy Tip:**
> Whenever a question mentions *"spinal injury suspected"* in the stem, your answer must contain the words **"in-line stabilization"** and **"log roll"** at minimum. If it mentions *"unconscious, no SCI"*, your answer should focus on **airway protection** and choose a one- or two-person carry. Match the lift to the casualty's condition — this is the single most-tested skill in the KTU board exam for this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Stretchers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT503 - OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH, HYGIENE AND FIRST AID
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Use of Stretchers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **stretcher** (also called a **gurney**, **litter**, or **pram** in different countries) is a lightweight, portable, rigid or semi-rigid framed device used to carry a sick, injured, or disabled person from one location to another in a lying (supine) or seated position. In occupational first aid, stretchers form a critical link in the **chain of survival** at workplaces, construction sites, factories, and industrial plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"A stretcher is a device used for carrying a casualty who is seriously ill or injured and cannot be moved by other means, in a horizontal or near-horizontal position, while maintaining spinal alignment and minimizing further injury during transport."*

The stretcher is governed by the principle of **manual handling safety** and is designed to:
- Maintain the **neutral spine position** of the casualty
- Distribute the **body weight** evenly across the carriers
- Allow safe **lifting, lowering, and carrying** by 2 to 4 rescuers
- Provide a stable platform for **loading into ambulances**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A standard military/industrial stretcher is approximately **183 cm (6 feet) long** and **56 cm (22 inches) wide**, capable of bearing a load of up to **150 kg (330 lbs)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are carrying a sleeping friend from the couch to their bed, but your friend is too heavy and fragile to lift in your arms. What do you do? You grab a **flat board**, lay your friend on it, and carry the board together with a helper — keeping your friend perfectly still.

A **stretcher is exactly that flat board** — but engineered, padded, and equipped with handles so that two or four rescuers can transport a casualty safely without bending, twisting, or jarring the spine.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** If a casualty cannot stand, walk, or sit upright safely → **use a stretcher**. When in doubt, always choose horizontal transport over vertical/drag transport.

### 1.3 Why Stretchers Matter in Occupational Health

In KTU-aligned occupational health practice, stretchers are essential because:
1. **Spinal injuries** at construction sites or factories require rigid support to prevent **secondary spinal cord damage**.
2. **Chemical exposure** or **electrical shock** casualties may lose consciousness and need safe horizontal evacuation.
3. **Confined space rescues** (manholes, tanks, trenches) require specialized stretchers for vertical extraction.
4. **Mass casualty incidents (MCIs)** in industrial settings demand rapid, organized stretcher deployment.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stretchers

Stretchers are classified based on **design, rigidity, and application**. Understanding the type dictates the **carrying technique**.

### 2.1.1 Based on Construction

| S.No. | Type | Description | Best Use |
|:-----:|:-----|:------------|:---------|
| 1 | **Rigid (Orthopaedic) Stretcher** | Solid wooden or metal frame, no flexibility | Spinal injuries, fractures |
| 2 | **Folding Stretcher** | Hinged along the long axis, folds for storage | General transport, ambulances |
| 3 | **Scoop (Orthopaedic) Stretcher** | Splits into two halves longitudinally, "scoops" under patient | Suspected spinal injuries without log-roll |
| 4 | **Wheeled Stretcher (Trolley)** | Mounted on wheeled undercarriage | Hospital/ambulance internal transport |
| 5 | **Basket (Stokes) Stretcher** | Rigid basket-shaped, used with ropes | Vertical lifting, industrial rescue, confined spaces |
| 6 | **Improvised Stretcher** | Made from poles + blankets/jackets | Emergencies when no proper stretcher is available |
| 7 | **Reeves Sleeve (Flexible Stretcher)** | Plywood or canvas sheet rolled along patient | Drag rescues, narrow corridors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **Scoop Stretcher** is the gold standard for suspected spinal injuries because it does not require log-rolling the patient — it slides in from either side.

## 2.2 Basic Anatomy of a Standard Stretcher

A standard rigid stretcher consists of:

- **Frame**: Two longitudinal poles (usually aluminium or hardwood)
- **Cross-pieces**: 3–4 transverse supports to hold the patient
- **Bed/Canvas**: Strong fabric stretched over the frame
- **Feet**: Two small legs that keep the canvas off the ground when laid down
- **Handles**: Two at each end (4 total) for lifting
- **Straps**: 2–3 patient-securing straps to prevent sliding

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Operational Principles

The following rules govern the **safe use of stretchers** in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations:

1. **Minimum Two Carriers:** At least two trained first aiders should carry a loaded stretcher; four is preferred for long distances.
2. **Patient's Head Forward:** When moving in corridors, the casualty's **head should lead** (towards the direction of motion) so the rear carrier can continuously observe breathing and facial color.
3. **Level Carrying:** Both carriers must lift and walk in sync to keep the stretcher **horizontal**.
4. **Patient Secured:** Always fasten straps before moving — never assume a conscious patient will hold on.
5. **Silent Signals:** In noisy industrial environments, carriers should agree on **verbal commands** ("Ready? Steady? Lift!").
6. **Descending Stairs Head-First or Feet-First?** — When going **down stairs**, the casualty should go **feet first** to prevent head injury from a forward fall. When going **up stairs**, the casualty goes **head first**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Staircase Direction:**
> - **Down** → casualty's **Feet** descend first (closer to the stairs) → *"Down, feet down."*
> - **Up** → casualty's **Head** goes up first → *"Up, head up."*

## 2.4 The Mechanics of Weight Distribution

The total load $L$ borne by the stretcher equals the patient's body weight $W$ plus equipment $E$:

$$L = W + E$$

For a stretcher with two carriers, the load is **shared equally**:

$$L_{\text{per carrier}} = \frac{W + E}{2}$$

For a four-handed carry, each pair carries:

$$L_{\text{per pair}} = \frac{W + E}{2} \text{ (split between 2 carriers)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A rescuer should ideally not lift more than **25 kg** in a single manual handling task (occupational safety standard). Thus, for a patient weighing **>100 kg**, **four carriers** must be used.

## 2.5 Improvised Stretchers — KTU 2024 Important

When no proper stretcher is available, an **improvised stretcher** can be constructed using:

- **Two strong poles/sticks** (approximately 2 m long)
- **Blankets, jackets, or shirts** woven between them
- **Rope, belts, or ties** to secure the fabric

> [!WARNING]
> Improvised stretchers should only be used when no rigid stretcher is available AND the patient's condition is not life-threatening. For suspected spinal injuries, **never** use an improvised stretcher — wait for a rigid device.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Procedure 1: Loading a Casualty onto a Stretcher (Log-Roll Method)

This is the **most commonly examined KTU procedure** for suspected spinal injury.

### Steps:

**Step 1: Scene Assessment**
- Ensure the area is safe (no live wires, fire, gas leaks).
- Wear **PPE** — gloves, mask, eye protection.

**Step 2: Primary Survey (DR ABC)**
- Check **D**anger, **R**esponse, **A**irway, **B**reathing, **C**irculation.

**Step 3: Stabilize the Spine**
- Kneel at the patient's head.
- Place hands on either side of the head to maintain **manual in-line stabilization**.
- Do not release until the patient is fully secured on the stretcher.

**Step 4: Position the Stretcher**
- Place the stretcher parallel to the patient's body, ideally on the **uninjured side**.

**Step 5: Log-Roll the Patient**
- 3 rescuers are needed:
  - **Rescuer 1**: At the head, maintaining cervical spine control.
  - **Rescuer 2**: At the shoulders and hips.
  - **Rescuer 3**: At the pelvis and legs.
- On a count, roll the patient **towards Rescuer 2/3** (away from stretcher side), exposing the back.

**Step 6: Slide the Stretcher In**
- Push the stretcher snugly against the patient's back.

**Step 7: Roll Back**
- On a second count, gently roll the patient back onto the stretcher.

**Step 8: Secure and Lift**
- Fasten all straps (chest, hips, legs).
- Confirm the head is immobilized with head blocks or rolled blankets.
- Lift only on command.

## 3.2 Procedure 2: The Four-Handed Carry (Used for Conscious Patients)

This carry requires **two rescuers** standing side-by-side.

### Steps:

**Step 1:** Each rescuer grasps their **own left wrist** with their **right hand**.

**Step 2:** Each rescuer then grasps the **other rescuer's right wrist** with their **left hand**.

This forms a **"seat" of four interlocked hands**.

**Step 3:** The conscious patient sits on the hand-seat and places their arms around the rescuers' shoulders for balance.

**Step 4:** On command ("Lift!"), both rescuers stand simultaneously and walk in step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four-handed carry is **NOT suitable for unconscious or spinal injury patients** — use a stretcher for such cases.

## 3.3 Procedure 3: The Two-Handed (Fore-and-Aft) Carry

Used in **narrow spaces** where a stretcher cannot pass.

- **Rescuer 1** stands at the patient's head, slides hands under the armpits and grasps the forearms crossed over the chest.
- **Rescuer 2** stands between the knees, reaches under the knees, and grasps the patient's wrists (or uses a back-to-back seat).

## 3.4 Procedure 4: Loading a Stretcher into an Ambulance

### Steps:

1. Position the stretcher parallel to the ambulance patient compartment.
2. Adjust the ambulance trolley to the same height.
3. Engage brakes on the ambulance trolley.
4. With 2 carriers holding the stretcher, slide the patient onto the trolley in one synchronized motion.
5. Fasten the trolley locks.
6. Secure the trolley inside the ambulance using anchor straps.

## 3.5 The 12 Golden Rules of Stretcher Use (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| S.No. | Rule | Rationale |
|:-----:|:-----|:----------|
| 1 | Always test the stretcher before use | Detect broken locks or torn canvas |
| 2 | Minimum 2 carriers, 4 for long distances | Distribute load |
| 3 | Patient's head leads in corridors | Continuous monitoring |
| 4 | Feet first when going down stairs | Prevent head injury |
| 5 | Head first when going up stairs | Easier observation |
| 6 | Always strap the patient | Prevent falls |
| 7 | Walk in step, keep stretcher level | Avoid jarring |
| 8 | Use blanket under patient | Comfort, insulation, shock prevention |
| 9 | Never leave patient unattended | Continuous monitoring |
| 10 | For spinal injury — use scoop or rigid stretcher | Prevent secondary injury |
| 11 | In industrial areas, mark the route clear of hazards | Safe transit |
| 12 | After use, decontaminate the stretcher | Infection control |

## 3.6 Mathematical Analysis: Load Distribution on Carriers

Consider a patient of mass $m$ on a stretcher carried by 2 rescuers. Let $m_c$ be the mass of the stretcher.

Total downward force:

$$F_{\text{total}} = (m + m_c) \cdot g$$

where $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ is the acceleration due to gravity.

If the patient is not centered, with the center of mass at distance $d$ from the left carrier, then the load on each carrier is:

$$F_{\text{left}} = \frac{(m + m_c) \cdot g \cdot (L - d)}{L}$$

$$F_{\text{right}} = \frac{(m + m_c) \cdot g \cdot d}{L}$$

where $L$ is the total length of the stretcher.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Implication:** If a patient carries a heavy load (e.g., a tool belt, a backpack), it shifts the center of mass. The rescuer at the heavier end bears more load. Counter-balance by adding weight on the lighter side or repositioning the patient.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stretcher Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Casualty Needs Transport?] --> B{Is Spinal Injury Suspected?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Space Available?}
    B -- No --> D{Available Equipment?}
    C -- Wide --> E[Use Rigid or Scoop Stretcher]
    C -- Narrow --> F[Use Reeves Sleeve Stretcher]
    D -- Standard --> G[Use Folding Stretcher]
    D -- Confined Space --> H[Use Basket Stokes Stretcher]
    D -- No Equipment --> I[Improvise with Poles and Blankets]
    E --> J[Log-Roll Loading]
    F --> K[Drag Extraction]
    G --> L[Standard Lift 4 Carriers]
    H --> M[Vertical Hoist Setup]
    I --> N[Two-Handed Carry]
    J --> O[Secure and Transport]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Staircase Direction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Stretcher on Stairs] --> Q{Direction of Movement?}
    Q -- Going UP --> R[Head Leads First]
    Q -- Going DOWN --> S[Feet Lead First]
    R --> T[Rescuer at Head Watches Airway]
    S --> U[Rescuer at Feet Supports Weight]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Anatomy of a Standard Stretcher

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Handle End 1] --- A2[Longitudinal Pole 1]
    A2 --- A3[Canvas Bed]
    A3 --- A4[Longitudinal Pole 2]
    A4 --- A5[Handle End 2]
    A3 --- B1[Cross Piece 1 - Head]
    A3 --- B2[Cross Piece 2 - Chest]
    A3 --- B3[Cross Piece 3 - Pelvis]
    A3 --- B4[Cross Piece 4 - Legs]
    A3 --- C1[Strap 1 - Chest]
    A3 --- C2[Strap 2 - Pelvis]
    A3 --- C3[Strap 3 - Legs]
    A3 --- D1[Feet Stands]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — Four-Handed Carry (Patient Handoff)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R1 as Rescuer 1
    participant R2 as Rescuer 2
    participant P as Patient
    R1->>R1: Grasp left wrist with right hand
    R2->>R2: Grasp left wrist with right hand
    R1->>R2: Grasp R2 right wrist with left hand
    R2->>R1: Grasp R1 right wrist with left hand
    P->>R1: Sit on interlocked hands
    P->>R2: Arms around shoulders
    R1->>R2: Command: Lift
    R2->>R1: Lift together
    R1->>R2: Walk in step
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three types of stretchers used in occupational first aid and state one specific use of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Rigid Stretcher** — Used for transporting patients with suspected spinal injuries because it provides firm, non-flexing support. *(1 mark)*
2. **Scoop Stretcher** — Used for spinal injury patients in narrow spaces; it splits into two halves and scoops under the patient without log-rolling. *(1 mark)*
3. **Basket (Stokes) Stretcher** — Used for vertical lifting and rescue from confined spaces, trenches, or industrial pits. *(1 mark)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State three golden rules to be observed while carrying a loaded stretcher.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The stretcher must be carried with the **casualty's head leading** in the direction of movement so the rear carrier can monitor breathing and colour. *(1 mark)*
2. When going **down stairs**, the casualty should go **feet first** to prevent a head-first fall. When going **up stairs**, the casualty goes **head first**. *(1 mark)*
3. All carriers must **walk in step** to keep the stretcher level, and the patient must be **fully strapped** before movement begins. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently lose marks in stretcher questions by:
> 1. Forgetting to **strap the patient** before lifting (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. Confusing the staircase direction rule (up = head first, down = feet first).
> 3. Not mentioning the **minimum number of carriers** for spinal vs. non-spinal cases.
> 4. Failing to describe the **log-roll technique step-by-step** (a KTU favourite).
> 5. Omitting **PPE and scene safety** at the start of the answer.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**(a) Describe in detail the procedure for loading a suspected spinal injury casualty onto a rigid stretcher using the log-roll method.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Explain the different types of stretchers used in occupational first aid and their specific applications.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Loading a suspected spinal injury patient requires a **minimum of three trained rescuers**. The steps are:

1. **Scene Safety & PPE (0.5 mark):** Ensure the environment is safe. Rescuers don gloves and protective gear.

2. **Primary Survey (0.5 mark):** Perform DR ABC. Establish responsiveness and airway.

3. **Manual In-Line Stabilization (1 mark):** Rescuer 1 kneels at the head of the patient, placing both hands on either side of the skull, with the elbows resting on the ground or thighs. This position is **maintained continuously** until the patient is fully secured on the stretcher.

4. **Positioning the Stretcher (1 mark):** Place the rigid stretcher parallel to the patient's body, on the **uninjured side** if known. Ensure the canvas is taut, and head blocks / sandbags are ready at the head end.

5. **Rescuer Positioning (1 mark):**
   - Rescuer 1: Head — controls the cervical spine.
   - Rescuer 2: At the chest and pelvis.
   - Rescuer 3: At the hips and legs.

6. **Log-Roll (1.5 marks):** On a clear command (e.g., "On the count of three — One, Two, Three — Roll"), all rescuers roll the patient **as a single unit** towards Rescuer 2/3, exposing the back by approximately 90°. The spine must remain **in a straight line** throughout.

7. **Slide Stretcher In (0.5 mark):** Rescuer 2 slides the stretcher against the patient's back.

8. **Roll Back & Secure (1 mark):** On a second command, roll the patient back onto the stretcher. Immediately fasten the chest, pelvic, and leg straps. Place head blocks or rolled blankets on either side of the head. Confirm the cervical collar (if applied) is in place.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point for 1 Extra Mark:** Mention that Rescuer 1 maintains manual stabilization until the head blocks and straps are fully secured — this is a common valuation checkpoint.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Types of Stretchers and Applications:**

1. **Rigid (Orthopaedic) Stretcher (1 mark):** Solid wooden or metal frame, no flex. Used for **spinal injuries, fractures, and long-distance transport**. Provides maximum immobilization.

2. **Folding Stretcher (1 mark):** Hinged along the long axis; folds into a compact size. Used in **ambulances, factories, and public venues** for general transport of conscious or unconscious patients.

3. **Scoop (Orthopaedic) Stretcher (1.5 marks):** Splits into **two longitudinal halves** that slide under the patient from either side, then lock together. Ideal for **suspected spinal injuries in confined or awkward spaces** because it eliminates the need for log-rolling.

4. **Wheeled Stretcher / Trolley (1 mark):** Mounted on a wheeled undercarriage. Used in **hospitals, airports, and large industrial plants** for smooth rolling transport over flat surfaces.

5. **Basket (Stokes) Stretcher (1.5 marks):** Rigid basket-shaped metal or plastic shell. Used in **vertical lifting, confined-space rescue (trenches, manholes), and mountain rescue**. Can be hoisted by ropes or attached to a crane.

6. **Improvised Stretcher (1 mark):** Made from **two poles and a blanket, jacket, or strong cloth**. Used in **emergencies** when no standard stretcher is available. **Contraindicated** for suspected spinal injuries.

> [!TIP]
> **Add for bonus mark:** The **Reeves Sleeve / Flexible Stretcher** — a thin plywood or reinforced canvas sheet that can be rolled along the patient's side and used for **drag rescues in narrow corridors**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a) Explain the proper technique for carrying a loaded stretcher through a corridor and while negotiating stairs. State the rationale for each rule.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Describe in detail the construction and use of an improvised stretcher. Under what conditions should it NOT be used?** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Corridor Transport — Head Leads (2 marks):**
When carrying a stretcher through a corridor, the casualty's **head must lead** (be at the front, in the direction of motion). The rescuer at the **rear (foot end)** can continuously observe the casualty's **face, breathing, and skin color**, allowing early detection of deterioration. The rescuer at the head is responsible for watching for **obstacles** and **calling out directions**.

**2. Walking in Step (1 mark):** Both (or all four) carriers must walk in **unison**, taking simultaneous steps. This prevents the stretcher from **tilting**, **swaying**, or **jolting**, which could aggravate spinal or fracture injuries.

**3. Strapping the Patient (1 mark):** Before any movement, **all straps** (chest, hip, and leg) must be fastened. A conscious patient may suddenly lose balance, vomit, or lose consciousness — straps prevent a fall.

**4. Staircase Rules (3 marks):**
- **Going UP stairs:** Casualty's **head leads first**. The head-end rescuer walks up first, providing better observation of the patient and reducing the risk of the patient sliding down the inclined stretcher. Rationale: gravity holds the patient against the canvas.
- **Going DOWN stairs:** Casualty's **feet lead first**. The foot-end rescuer descends first, lowering the patient slowly. Rationale: the heavier head-end stays higher, preventing forward head-first momentum that could cause head injury or a fall off the foot-end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exception:** If the patient has a **lower limb injury**, the injured leg should be **kept uphill** (higher) on the stretcher regardless of direction, to reduce swelling and blood flow issues.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Improvised Stretcher — Construction:**

**Materials Required (1 mark):**
- Two **strong, straight poles** (wooden, metal, or bamboo), each approximately **2 metres (6 feet) long**.
- A **strong cloth** — blanket, rug, tarpaulin, or two large jackets/shirts.

**Construction Method (3 marks):**
1. Lay the blanket flat on the ground.
2. Place Pole 1 along one long edge of the blanket (about one-third of the blanket's width from the edge).
3. Fold the remaining one-third of the blanket over the first pole.
4. Place Pole 2 on top of the folded edge.
5. Fold the remaining blanket over Pole 2, sandwiching Pole 2 inside.
6. The poles are now encased in the fabric, forming a firm patient platform.
7. Test the stretcher by lifting a known weight (e.g., a sandbag) before placing the patient.

> Alternative method: Thread **3–4 shirts/jackets** with sleeves inside-out. Pass the two poles through the sleeves. Each shirt becomes a "bridge" supporting the patient.

**Use (1 mark):**
- Place the casualty gently on the improvised stretcher.
- Secure with belts, ropes, or tied cloth strips across the chest, hips, and legs.
- Carry with at least 2 (preferably 4) rescuers.

**When NOT to Use (2 marks):**
- **Suspected spinal injuries** — an improvised stretcher is not rigid enough and will sag, causing spinal flexion.
- **Fractures of the pelvis, femur, or multiple long bones** — the lack of rigidity may displace bone fragments.
- **Industrial chemical contamination** — the cloth may absorb chemicals and harm the patient further; use a decontamination stretcher.
- **When a proper rigid or scoop stretcher is available** — improvisation is a last resort.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Use of Stretchers**

- A **stretcher** is a portable device for transporting a sick/injured person in a horizontal position.
- **Main types:** Rigid, Folding, Scoop, Wheeled, Basket (Stokes), Improvised, Reeves Sleeve.
- **Scoop stretcher** = best for spinal injury in confined spaces (no log-roll needed).
- **Basket stretcher** = best for vertical lifts, confined-space, and trench rescue.
- **Minimum carriers:** 2 (general), 4 (long distance or >100 kg patient).
- **Patient's head leads** in corridors; **rear carrier monitors breathing**.
- **Up stairs → head first.** **Down stairs → feet first.** *(Mnemonic: "Up, head up; Down, feet down.")*
- **Always strap the patient** before any movement — chest, hip, and leg straps.
- **Log-roll method** requires 3 rescuers: head, chest/pelvis, hips/legs.
- **Rescuer 1 (at head)** maintains **manual in-line cervical spine stabilization** throughout.
- **Manual handling limit:** Do not lift >25 kg alone — use 4 carriers for heavy patients.
- **Improvised stretcher** = last resort. **Never** for spinal injury or major fractures.
- **After every use**, decontaminate the stretcher (OSHA/biomedical waste protocol).
- **Standard dimensions:** 183 cm × 56 cm; load capacity ≈ 150 kg.
- **PPE** must be worn by all carriers: gloves, mask, eye protection, closed footwear.
- **In chemical exposure cases**, decontaminate the casualty at the scene **before** stretcher transport.
